conceptName: Explain purpose of a given motion
prompt: During a meeting of the Fairhaven Choral Society, President Mansour Abram sees that a pending motion should be set aside briefly so the assembly can deal with an urgent matter, but he wants to keep the ability to bring it back later in the same meeting by a simple decision. He asks you which motion accomplishes this and what it does.
gold: This is the motion to Lay on the Table. Its proper purpose is to set a pending question aside temporarily so more urgent business can be attended to, with the question able to be brought back later in the same session by a motion to Take from the Table. It requires a second, is not debatable, is not amendable, and is adopted by a majority vote.

ref.quote: If there is reason for the assembly to lay the main motion aside temporarily without setting a time for resuming its consideration, but with the provision that it can be taken up again whenever a majority so decides, this can be proposed by the motion to Lay on the Table .

conceptName: Explain purpose of a given motion
prompt: At the Cedar Valley Homeowners Association, Chair Priya Nair wants to give members a short break in the middle of a long debate, without ending the meeting, so that discussion resumes at the same point afterward. She asks you to name the motion she should use and explain its purpose.
gold: This is the privileged motion to Recess. Its purpose is to provide a short intermission that does not close the meeting; when the recess ends, business resumes exactly where it left off. It requires a second, is not debatable when made while business is pending, is amendable only as to the length of the recess, and is adopted by a majority vote.

ref.quote: A short intermission in a meeting, even while business is pending, can be proposed by moving to Recess for a specified length of time. 4) A member can propose to close the meeting entirely by moving to Adjourn .

conceptName: Explain purpose of a given motion
prompt: The Society of Rare Booksellers adopted a standing policy several months ago that is still in force, and now, with no business pending, President Elena Duarte wants the assembly to wipe it out entirely. She asks you which motion is used and what vote it takes.
gold: This is the motion to Rescind (also called Repeal or Annul). Its purpose is to cancel or countermand a main motion, resolution, order, or rule previously adopted and still in force. It requires a second, is debatable, and is adopted by a two-thirds vote, or a majority vote with previous notice, or a majority of the entire membership.

ref.quote: cancel or countermand an entire main motion, resolution, order, or rule that has been adopted and that has continuing force and effect

conceptName: Name the motion for a purpose
prompt: At the Millbrook Arts Council, a main motion is pending and member Grace Lin believes it would be acceptable if some of its wording were changed before the vote. She asks you what motion she should make to accomplish that.
gold: She should move the subsidiary motion to Amend, which proposes changes to the wording, and within limits the meaning, of a pending motion before it is voted on. It requires a second, is debatable when applied to a debatable motion, is itself amendable, and is adopted by a majority vote even if the motion it amends requires a higher vote.

ref.quote: a proposal to change its wording (either to clarify or, within limits, to modify the meaning) before the main motion is voted on can be introduced by moving to Amend . 3) But it may be that much time would be required to amend the main motion properly, or that additional information is needed, so that it would be better to turn the motion or resolution over to a committee for study or redrafting before the assembly considers it further.

conceptName: Name the motion for a purpose
prompt: During a Riverside Rowing Club meeting, a pending resolution clearly needs study and redrafting before the assembly is ready to act on it. Commodore Tomas Vega asks you which motion sends the question to a small group for that work.
gold: He should move the subsidiary motion to Commit (or Refer), which turns the pending main motion over to a committee for study or redrafting. It requires a second, is debatable, is amendable as to committee details and instructions, and is adopted by a majority vote.

ref.quote: it would be better to turn the motion or resolution over to a committee for study or redrafting before the assembly considers it further.

conceptName: Name the motion for a purpose
prompt: At the Glenwood Garden Club, member Paul Reyes notices that the chair is permitting a clear violation of the rules and wants to require the chair to rule on it immediately. He asks you what device he should use.
gold: He should raise a Point of Order at the time the breach occurs, which requires the chair to rule on the question involved. A Point of Order needs no second, is not debatable, is not amendable, and takes no vote; the chair simply rules, subject to Appeal. It may be raised even when another member has the floor if the breach requires immediate attention.

ref.quote: call attention to it by making a Point of Order ; the effect is to require the chair to make a ruling on the question involved.

conceptName: Meaning of the classes of motions
prompt: While a main motion is being debated at the Northside Teachers Association, an urgent matter about the comfort and rights of members arises, and a newer member asks President Dana Whitfield why some motions are allowed to interrupt pending business. She asks you to explain what the class of privileged motions is.
gold: Privileged motions are a class that do not relate to the pending business but deal with matters of immediate and overriding importance, such as recess, adjournment, and questions of privilege. Because of that urgency they may interrupt consideration of pending business, and as a class they take precedence over all other motions. They are generally not debatable.

ref.quote: do not relate to the pending business, but have to do with special matters of immediate and overriding importance which, without debate, should be allowed to interrupt the consideration of anything else.

conceptName: Meaning of the classes of motions
prompt: At the Harbor City Booster Club, President Alan Frost keeps encountering procedural questions like points of order and appeals during debate and asks you to explain what the class of incidental motions is and why they get resolved right away.
gold: Incidental motions are a class that arise out of, and relate to, another pending motion or business at hand, for example a Point of Order, Appeal, Suspend the Rules, or Division of the Assembly. With few exceptions they must be decided immediately, before business can proceed, and most of them are undebatable. They have no order of precedence among themselves.

ref.quote: incidental motions are related to the main question in such a way that they must be decided immediately, before business can proceed. Most incidental motions are undebatable.

conceptName: Meaning of the classes of motions
prompt: A new member of the Lakeside Camera Club asks President Delia Okafor to explain, in plain terms, what a main motion is and where it stands relative to other motions.
gold: A main motion is a motion whose introduction brings business before the assembly. It can be made only when no other motion is pending, and it ranks lowest in the order of precedence, yielding to all subsidiary, all privileged, and all applicable incidental motions. It requires a second, is debatable and amendable, and normally requires a majority vote.

ref.quote: a main motion can be made only when no other motion is pending, and it ranks lowest in the order of precedence of motions.

prompt: President Ruth Callahan of the Delta Beekeepers Guild is confused by the phrases "takes precedence over" and "yields to" and asks you to explain what they mean, using the relationship between a secondary motion and the main motion as an example.
gold: One motion takes precedence over another when it may be made and considered while the other is pending, temporarily replacing it as the immediately pending question; the lower motion is said to yield to it. A secondary motion takes precedence over the main motion, and the main motion takes precedence over nothing and yields to all applicable secondary motions.

ref.quote: A secondary motion thus takes precedence over the main motion; and a main motion takes precedence over nothing and yields to all applicable secondary motions.

prompt: At the Ashford Debate League, an amendment is immediately pending and a member tries to move a motion that ranks below it. President Miguel Santos asks you to explain the concept of rank so he can tell which motions may be moved.
gold: The thirteen ranking motions, the main motion, the seven subsidiary motions, and the five privileged motions, fall into a fixed order of precedence. Each motion takes precedence over those that rank below it and yields to those that rank above it; the main motion ranks lowest and the privileged motions rank highest. A motion is out of order when a motion of higher rank is already immediately pending, so the lower-ranking motion cannot be entertained now.

ref.quote: taking precedence over the motions that rank below it and yielding to those that rank above it.

prompt: At the Two Rivers Conservation Trust, several motions have stacked up and are pending at once. Presiding officer Karen Blythe asks you in what order the assembly votes on them.
gold: The motion most recently stated by the chair, the highest-ranking immediately pending question, is disposed of first, and then the question recurs on the next-most-recently moved motion. Thus the pending motions are voted on in the reverse of the order in which they were made, with the main motion voted on last, unless the result of one vote halts consideration of the rest of the series.

ref.quote: the motions in the series of pending questions are voted on in the reverse of the order in which they were made-with the main motion being voted on last-unless the result of the vote on one of the other motions causes consideration of the remainder of the series to be halted.

conceptName: Motions that adhere to other motions
prompt: At the Brookfield Alumni Association, a main motion with a pending amendment is laid on the table so the assembly can handle something urgent. Secretary Owen Pratt asks you what becomes of the pending amendment.
gold: The amendment adheres to the main motion. Adhering motions remain connected with the main question when it is interrupted or temporarily disposed of, and they remain to be decided first when it is taken up again. So when the question is taken from the table, the pending amendment comes back with it and must be decided before the main motion.

ref.quote: Adhering motions thus remain connected with the main question if that question is interrupted or temporarily disposed of, and remain to be decided first if and when the main question is taken up again.

conceptName: Motions that adhere to other motions
prompt: A member of the Sierra Trekkers Club asks President Nadia Rahman to explain what it means to say that a subsidiary or incidental motion "adheres" to the main question.
gold: A subsidiary or incidental motion adheres to the main question when, once introduced, it must be decided before the main question can be decided. Such motions stay attached to the main question even if it is interrupted or temporarily disposed of, and they are taken up again together with it when it returns.

ref.quote: it must be decided before the main question can be decided.

conceptName: Motions that adhere to other motions
prompt: At the Coastline Yacht Club, a privileged motion to take a recess is pending along with a main motion and its amendments. Vice Commodore Harold Yee asks you whether the recess motion adheres to the main question the way the amendments do.
gold: No. A motion to Recess, a privileged motion, does not adhere to the main question. Adhering motions are those that must be decided before the main question and stay attached to it when it is set aside; a recess merely interrupts, and when it ends the assembly resumes where it left off, so the recess is not attached to the main question.

ref.quote: Motion (8) in the example does not adhere to the main question.

prompt: Using the order-of-precedence chart to train a presiding officer, President Sofia Marin of the Elmwood Ratepayers League asks you to explain when a main motion may properly be introduced.
gold: A main motion is in order only when no other motion is pending, because it ranks lowest and takes precedence over nothing. If any motion is already pending, a new main motion is out of order and the chair should so rule; the member must wait until the pending business is disposed of.

ref.quote: the main motion, the seven subsidiary motions, and the five privileged motions fall into a definite order of precedence, which gives a particular rank to each of these thirteen motions.

prompt: At the Prairie Quilters Guild, presiding officer Janet Cole is unsure how to read the precedence chart to decide whether a proposed motion is in order at a given moment. She asks you to explain how to use it.
gold: On the chart the privileged, subsidiary, and main motions are listed in order of rank, the top motion taking precedence over all below it. When one motion is immediately pending, any motion listed above it is in order, unless a stated condition makes it out of order, and motions listed below it are out of order. So you compare the proposed motion's rank with the immediately pending question.

ref.quote: Each of the other twelve motions has its proper position in the order, taking precedence over the motions that rank below it and yielding to those that rank above it.

prompt: A member of the Highland Astronomy Society insists to Chair Victor Osei that any motion ranking above the immediately pending one is automatically in order. He asks whether that is correct.
gold: Not exactly. A motion ranking above the immediately pending question is generally in order, but only unless a condition stated opposite that motion causes it to be out of order. Rank is the starting point, but each motion's own rules, its standard descriptive characteristics, can still make it out of order at that particular moment.

ref.quote: The privileged motions rank above all other motions.

conceptName: Order of precedence of ranking motions
prompt: President Cora Nakamura of the Westgate Model Railroad Club asks you to explain the relative ranking of the privileged, subsidiary, and main motions so she can decide what may be moved when.
gold: The ranking motions are arranged with the privileged motions highest, the subsidiary motions in the middle, and the main motion lowest. Each motion takes precedence over all those ranking below it, so a higher-ranking motion may be moved while a lower-ranking one is pending, but a lower-ranking motion may not be moved while a higher one is pending.

ref.quote: the main motion, the seven subsidiary motions, and the five privileged motions fall into a definite order of precedence, which gives a particular rank to each of these thirteen motions.

conceptName: Order of precedence of ranking motions
prompt: At the Bayside Photography Circle, an amendment is pending when member Leah Gordon moves to adjourn. Another member objects that the amendment cannot be interrupted. Chair Ivan Petrov asks you whether the motion to adjourn is in order now.
gold: The motion to Adjourn is a privileged motion and outranks the subsidiary motion to Amend. Because a higher-ranking motion takes precedence over a lower-ranking one already pending, the motion to Adjourn is in order even while the amendment is pending. It requires a second, is not debatable, and is decided by a majority vote before the amendment is resumed.

ref.quote: Each of the other twelve motions has its proper position in the order, taking precedence over the motions that rank below it and yielding to those that rank above it.

conceptName: Order of precedence of ranking motions
prompt: Training the officers of the Fern Hollow Book Society, President Marcus Reed asks you which of the ranking motions may be moved only when nothing is pending, and which one ranks highest of all.
gold: The main motion ranks lowest and is in order only when no other motion is pending, since it is what brings business up. The subsidiary and privileged motions rank above it and are moved while business is already pending. The highest-ranking of all the motions is Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn.
keywords: fix the time to which to adjourn

ref.quote: The privileged motions rank above all other motions.

conceptName: Use descriptive characteristics and Tables II, IV-VII
prompt: A newly credentialed assistant to President Hana Kim of the Verde Valley Growers asks you what the "standard descriptive characteristics" of a motion are for, and how the reference tables use them.
gold: The eight standard descriptive characteristics summarize the key rules governing each motion: its precedence, what it applies to, whether it is in order when another has the floor, whether it needs a second, whether it is debatable, whether it is amendable, what vote it requires, and whether it can be reconsidered. They serve as points of comparison showing how a motion resembles or differs from a main motion, which is why the tables list them side by side for every motion.

ref.quote: these characteristics serve as points of comparison showing how the motion resembles or differs from a main motion.

conceptName: Use descriptive characteristics and Tables II, IV-VII
prompt: At the Oakmont Ratepayers Forum a member interrupts a speaker to make a motion. Chair Ben Alvarez asks you how, using the standard descriptive characteristics, he should decide whether that interruption is allowed.
gold: He should consult standard descriptive characteristic 3, whether the motion is in order when another has the floor. Most motions, including the main motion and amendments, are out of order when another member has the floor, but a few, such as a Point of Order or a Division of the Assembly, may interrupt. If the motion is not one of those, the interruption is improper and the chair should require the member to wait for recognition.
keywords: in order when another has the floor

ref.quote: Whether it is in order when another has the floor. (A main motion is not.) 4.

conceptName: Use descriptive characteristics and Tables II, IV-VII
prompt: President Aisha Bello of the Summit Trail Alliance is unsure how to determine the vote needed for a particular motion. She asks you to explain how the standard descriptive characteristics help.
gold: Consult standard descriptive characteristic 7, what vote is required for adoption. A main motion and most subsidiary motions require a majority vote, while motions that suspend or curtail the rights of members, such as the Previous Question, Limit or Extend Limits of Debate, Suspend the Rules, or an Objection to Consideration, require a two-thirds vote. You identify which characteristic applies to the specific motion in question.

ref.quote: What vote is required for its adoption. (A main motion requires a majority vote, except as noted in 10:8.) 8.

conceptName: Use Table III for proper motion form
prompt: At the Cliffside Ratepayers Union, a member offers a rambling, vague motion. President Nora Whitman asks you how a motion should properly be worded and what she may do about the wording.
gold: A motion should be worded in a concise, unambiguous, and complete form, because once adopted it becomes the officially recorded statement of the assembly's action, and it may not use language that would not be allowed in debate. The chair may ask the maker to reword it, or it may be perfected by amendment before adoption.

ref.quote: A motion should therefore be worded in a concise, unambiguous, and complete form appropriate to such a purpose.

conceptName: Use Table III for proper motion form
prompt: A member of the Maple Ridge Civic League asks Secretary Julian Ford how to phrase a motion that is not being offered as a formal resolution.
gold: A motion that is not offered as a resolution is simply stated or written in the form in which it would be moved orally, beginning with the word "That," for example, "That the League purchase new folding chairs." A long or complicated motion should always be submitted in writing.

ref.quote: it can simply be written out in the form in which it would be moved orally (beginning with the word “That”)

conceptName: Use Table III for proper motion form
prompt: During a Pinecrest Neighborhood Council meeting, the chair informally summarizes an idea and a member simply says, "I so move." President Gloria Mensah asks you whether that is a proper way to make the motion.
gold: It is not the preferred form. A member should avoid statements such as "I so move" and should himself recite the complete motion he offers, so that its exact wording is clear and can be accurately recorded. The chair should ask the member to state the full motion.

ref.quote: should avoid statements such as “I so move,” and should himself recite the complete motion that he offers.

conceptName: Standard descriptive characteristics of main motions
prompt: At the Redwood Debating Society, with no motion pending, a member moves that each member be limited to one two-minute speech for the rest of the meeting. President Clara Jennings asks you what vote this main motion needs.
gold: Although a main motion ordinarily requires only a majority vote, when its adoption would have the effect of suspending a rule of order or a parliamentary right of members, as a special limit on the length or number of speeches does, it requires a two-thirds vote. It still requires a second and is debatable and amendable.

ref.quote: suspending a rule of order or a parliamentary right of members, in which case it requires a two-thirds vote

conceptName: Standard descriptive characteristics of main motions
prompt: At the Willowbrook Ratepayers Association, a member calls out a new main motion while another member is speaking. President Derek Nash asks you whether this is proper and what the requirements for a main motion are.
gold: It is not proper. A main motion is out of order when another member has the floor, so the member must first obtain the floor. A main motion also must be seconded, is debatable and amendable, and requires a majority vote. The chair should decline to entertain the interrupting motion.
keywords: out of order when another has the floor

ref.quote: Is out of order when another has the floor. 4.

conceptName: Standard descriptive characteristics of main motions
prompt: While a main motion is pending at the Grantham Historical Society, member Rosa Iqbal wants to move to refer it to a committee, but another member insists that nothing can be moved while the main motion is pending. President Ed Larkin asks you who is correct.
gold: The member who wishes to refer is correct. A main motion takes precedence over nothing and yields to all subsidiary, all privileged, and all applicable incidental motions, so a subsidiary motion such as Commit may be moved while the main motion is pending. The chair should entertain the motion to Commit.

ref.quote: It yields to all subsidiary, all privileged, and all applicable incidental motions; that is, any subsidiary or privileged motion, and any incidental motion that is applicable in the particular case at the particular time, can be moved while a main motion is pending. 2.

prompt: A year ago the Ironwood Civic Club adopted a strong policy statement that is still in force. At a meeting a member now moves that the club "reaffirm" that statement. President Sam Whitlock asks you whether this motion is proper.
gold: No. A motion to "reaffirm" a position previously taken is not in order. The original motion is still in effect, so reaffirming serves no useful purpose, and it could conflict with the rules on Amend Something Previously Adopted or create an ambiguous situation if it failed. The chair should rule the motion out of order.

ref.quote: Such a motion serves no useful purpose because the original motion is still in effect; also, possible attempts to amend a motion to reaffirm would come into conflict with the rules for the motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted ; and if such a motion to reaffirm failed, it would create an ambiguous situation.

prompt: At the Fairmont Alumni Chapter, a member moves "that our delegates be given no instructions." President Yolanda Cruz asks you whether such a motion is proper.
gold: Generally it is not. A motion whose only effect is to have the assembly refrain from doing something should not be offered when the same result is achieved by making no motion at all, as with "that our delegates be given no instructions." Such a motion is proper only if some purpose is served, for example to bind a subordinate body or to record reasons. Otherwise the chair should rule it out of order.

ref.quote: It is incorrect, for example, to move “that no response be made” to a request for a contribution to a fund, or “that our delegates be given no instructions,” unless some purpose would be served by adoption of such a motion.

prompt: A member of the Lakeshore Merchants Association wants the group to "go on record as not in favor of" a proposed public bond issue. President Hugh Barrett asks you how the motion should be framed.
gold: A motion should avoid a negative statement, because members may become confused about the effect of voting for or against it. Rather than moving that the association go on record as "not in favor of" the bond issue, it should be moved that the association "oppose" or "declare its opposition to" it. The chair should have the maker restate it in the affirmative.

ref.quote: Rather than moving, for example, that the association go on record as “not in favor of the proposed public bond issue,” it should be moved that the association “oppose” or “declare its opposition to” the bond issue.

prompt: At the Kingsley Preservation Society, a member has drafted a resolution with several "Whereas" clauses added mainly for the sake of form. President Amara Devi asks you whether a preamble is required and when one is appropriate.
gold: A preamble is not required by any rule or custom and should not be used merely for the sake of form. Its use should be limited to cases where it supplies little-known information without which the resolution's merits would be poorly understood, or where it is important to make the reasons a matter of record. Unnecessary "Whereas" clauses should be omitted.

ref.quote: In general, the use of a preamble should be limited to cases where it provides little-known information without which the point or the merits of a resolution are likely to be poorly understood, where unusual importance is attached to making certain reasons for an action a matter of record, or the like.

prompt: The Hollis Watershed Council is considering a resolution that has both a preamble and resolving clauses, and a member wants to amend the preamble first. Chair Peter Nolan asks you what the correct order of amendment is.
gold: The resolving clauses are taken up and amended first; the preamble is always amended last, because changes in the resolving clauses may require corresponding changes in the preamble. The chair should open the preamble to amendment only after the resolving clauses have been perfected.

ref.quote: the preamble is always amended last, since changes in the resolving clauses may require changes in the preamble.

prompt: At the Crestview Community Foundation, member Ines Fabre has a number of related actions on one subject that will not fit neatly into a single resolution. President Omar Haddad asks you how they may be framed and brought before the assembly.
gold: When a single subject involves too many actions for one resolution, they may be set forth as a series of separate, numbered resolutions offered by a single main motion, and such a series may include orders. If members want one of a set of related resolutions considered separately, the motion for Division of a Question, adopted by a majority vote, is used; where the resolutions are independent, any one may be separated for a separate vote on the demand of a single member.

ref.quote: it can be set forth in a series of separate resolutions that can be numbered and offered by means of a single main motion, thus: "Mr. President, I move the adoption of [or "I offer"] the following resolutions:…" Such a series of resolutions can include orders as described above.

prompt: At the Aspen Ridge Trail Club, immediately after a member makes a motion but before the chair has stated the question, another member suggests a small change. President Nina Okonkwo asks you whether the maker can simply accept it.
gold: Yes. After a main motion has been made but before the chair states the question, any member may informally suggest one or more modifications, which the maker may accept or reject as he wishes, with no vote or second required. If accepted, the modified motion is what the chair then states. This method should be limited to minor changes.
keywords: before the chair states the question

ref.quote: informally suggest one or more modifications in the motion, which at this point the maker can accept or reject as he wishes .

prompt: At the Braddock Rotary affiliate, the chair has already stated the question on a member's motion, and the maker now wants to change its wording. President Lionel Sparks asks you whether the maker may do that on his own.
gold: No. Once the chair has stated the question, the motion belongs to the assembly, not the maker. The maker may modify it only by requesting unanimous consent; if any member objects, the change must be made by a formal motion to Amend, which requires a second, is debatable, and is adopted by a majority vote.
keywords: after the chair states the question

ref.quote: control over its wording-the maker can request unanimous consent to modify the motion .

prompt: Before the chair has stated a motion at the Verdant Hills Garden Society, a member tries to use the informal "suggest a modification" method to propose a sweeping rewrite of the motion. President Faye Underwood asks you whether that is an appropriate use of the method.
gold: No. The informal method, in which the maker accepts or rejects suggested modifications before the question is stated, should generally be limited to minor changes about which there is unlikely to be a difference of opinion. A substantial rewrite should instead be handled by the formal motion to Amend, or by a substitute, after the chair has stated the question.

ref.quote: Application of this method should generally be limited to minor changes about which there is unlikely to be a difference of opinion. 2) After the question has been stated by the chair-although the assembly, and not the maker of the motion, then has control over its wording-the maker can request unanimous consent to modify the motion .

conceptName: Explain main motion; five means of change
prompt: President Gideon Marsh of the Silverton Arts Guild wants to teach his board every way the proposal in a pending main motion can be changed or improved before the final vote. He asks you to lay out the options.
gold: There are several means, depending on the scope of change desired. First, before the chair states the question, the maker may accept informal modifications; second, after it is stated, the maker may modify it by unanimous consent; third, members may change it by the subsidiary motion to Amend; fourth, it may be sent to a committee by the motion to Commit; and fifth, a member may urge that it be voted down, or move to Postpone Indefinitely, and then offer a different main motion.

ref.quote: There are several means by which such a result can be accomplished, depending on the conditions and the degree or scope of the changes desired.

conceptName: Explain main motion; five means of change
prompt: At the Northlake Ratepayers Society, members disagree over the wording of a pending resolution and want to change it formally on the floor. President Tara Voss asks you what motion to use and what its rules are.
gold: They should use the subsidiary motion to Amend, which proposes changes in the wording, and within limits the meaning, of the pending motion. These amendments must be seconded, are debatable when the motion being amended is debatable, and are adopted by a majority vote, even if the main motion itself requires a higher vote.

ref.quote: These amendments must be seconded, are debatable, and are adopted by a majority vote.

conceptName: Explain main motion; five means of change
prompt: A main motion at the Beacon Hill Preservation League clearly needs significant study before the assembly acts on it. President Vera Lindqvist asks you what the recommended means of handling it is and what happens next.
gold: The recommended means is the subsidiary motion to Commit, or Refer, adopted by a majority vote, which sends the main motion to a committee for study or recasting. When the committee reports, it normally recommends appropriate amendments for the assembly's consideration, which the assembly may then adopt, reject, or further amend.

ref.quote: When this committee reports, it normally recommends appropriate amendments for the assembly’s consideration.

conceptName: Basic process and rules of primary amendments
prompt: At a meeting of the Cedar Falls Historical Society, a main motion "that the Society hold its annual banquet at the Lakeview Inn" is pending and under debate. Member Dana Whitfield rises and says, "I move to change 'Lakeview Inn' to 'Grandview Hall.'" President Okafor is unsure whether such a change can be made while the banquet motion is still only pending, or whether the assembly must first dispose of the banquet motion. How should the chair proceed?
gold: Dana has moved the subsidiary motion to Amend, which modifies the wording of a pending motion before that motion is acted upon, so it is entirely proper to offer it now. It requires a second, is debatable because the banquet motion is debatable, is itself amendable to one degree, and needs only a majority vote. The chair should ask for a second, then state and open the amendment to debate; adopting it does not adopt the banquet motion, which remains pending in its amended form.

ref.quote: The subsidiary motion to Amend is a motion to modify the wording—and within certain limits the meaning—of a pending motion before the pending motion itself is acted upon.

conceptName: Basic process and rules of primary amendments
prompt: During a Riverside Rowing Club meeting, member Luis Cardenas moves to amend a pending motion by inserting a new training requirement. Another member, Priya Nathan, votes in favor of the amendment, and it is adopted. When the amended main motion comes up for its vote, Priya announces she intends to vote against it. The chair, Ms. Halloran, wonders aloud whether Priya is bound by her earlier vote and whether the maker of the amendment can be said to have "amended" the motion himself. How should the chair respond?
gold: Priya is free to vote as she pleases on the main motion; her vote for the amendment does not obligate her to support the amended motion. Only the assembly amends a motion, not the individual member who offers the amendment or the maker of the main motion. The chair should let Priya vote against the amended main motion and proceed with the vote by the ordinary threshold required for that main motion.

ref.quote: A member’s vote on an amendment does not obligate him to vote in a particular way on the motion to which the amendment applies; he is free to vote as he pleases on the main motion, whether it is amended or not.

conceptName: Basic process and rules of primary amendments
prompt: The Fairmont Tenants Association is considering a motion to amend its standing rules, a main motion that itself requires a two-thirds vote to adopt. While it is pending, member Grace Yeboah moves to strike one sentence from the proposed rule. The presiding officer, Mr. Doyle, assumes that because the underlying rules change needs two-thirds, the amendment must also pass by two-thirds. Is he correct, and what does the amendment need to carry?
gold: The chair is incorrect. The subsidiary motion to Amend requires only a majority vote, regardless of the vote required to adopt the question being amended, so the two-thirds requirement on the underlying rules change does not raise the threshold for the amendment. Grace's amendment needs a second and is debatable, but carries on a majority vote; the two-thirds requirement applies only to the final vote on the amended main motion.

ref.quote: Requires a majority vote, regardless of the vote required to adopt the question to be amended.

prompt: At the Oakmont Garden Club, a debatable main motion "that the club sponsor a spring plant sale" is pending. Member Theo Ramirez moves to amend it by adding "and donate the proceeds to the community food bank." In debate on the amendment, member Ruth Kellner begins arguing at length that the club should not hold any plant sale at all. President Awad must decide whether Ruth's line of argument is proper. How should she rule?
gold: The amendment is debatable because the main motion to which it applies is debatable, but debate on the amendment must be confined to the desirability of the amendment itself and may not extend to the merits of the plant-sale motion, except as needed to judge whether the amendment is advisable. The chair should rule Ruth out of order on this point and direct her to confine her remarks to whether donating the proceeds is a good idea. The amendment still requires a second and a majority vote.

ref.quote: Is debatable whenever the motion to which it is applied is debatable. Such debate must be confined to the desirability of the amendment, however, and must not extend to the merits of the motion to be amended, except as may be necessary to determine whether the amendment is advisable.

prompt: The Brookdale Alumni Board has a main motion pending. Member Sofia Reyes moves to insert the words "by December 1" (a primary amendment). Member Grant Ito then moves to change that to "by December 15" (a secondary amendment). A third member, Ellis Park, now rises and moves to further amend Grant's proposal to read "by December 20." Chair Nakamura must decide whether Ellis's motion can be entertained. What is the ruling?
gold: The motion to Amend is itself amendable, but only to one degree, creating primary and secondary amendments; a secondary amendment cannot be amended. Ellis's motion attempts to amend Grant's secondary amendment, so it is out of order and the chair should rule it not in order. The pending secondary amendment ("by December 15") stands for a vote, needing a second and a majority.

ref.quote: Is generally amendable. This characteristic, however, creates two degrees of amendment-primary and secondary -and a secondary amendment cannot be amended . 7.

prompt: At the Westbrook Service League, a member has moved to Limit debate on the main question to two minutes per speaker, and a member now moves to amend that limit to three minutes per speaker. President Vance opens the floor and one member starts to debate whether three minutes is wiser than two. Vance recalls that motions to limit debate are not themselves debatable and is unsure whether the amendment to it can be debated. How should the chair handle the amendment?
gold: Because the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is undebatable, the amendment applied to it is likewise undebatable, so the chair should not allow debate on the three-minute amendment. The amendment still requires a second and is decided by a majority vote (though a motion to limit debate itself needs two-thirds); the chair should take the vote on the amendment without debate. Amend is debatable only when the motion it is applied to is debatable.

ref.quote: Amend is undebatable whenever the motion to be amended is undebatable (but see 43:31-32 regarding allowable explanation of an undebatable motion). 6.

prompt: The Hollis Park Neighborhood Council has pending the motion "that the Council install two benches along the river trail." Member Aisha Bello wants the words "and one water fountain" placed at the very end of the sentence. She rises and says, "I move to insert 'and one water fountain' after 'trail.'" President Kim tells her the term she used does not fit where the words will go and asks her to restate the motion. Is the chair right, and what is the correct wording?
gold: When new words are placed at the end of the sentence or passage being amended, the proper term is to "add" the words rather than to "insert" them; insert is used when words go somewhere in the middle. The chair is right to have Aisha restate it as "I move to add 'and one water fountain' at the end." It is a primary amendment requiring a second, debatable, and adopted by majority vote.

ref.quote: To insert words, or, if they are placed at the end of the sentence or passage being amended, to add words. b. To insert a paragraph, or, if it is placed at the end, to add a paragraph. 2.

prompt: At the Delmar Arts Guild, a one-sentence main motion is pending: "that the Guild adopt blue as its official color." Member Roland Pierce rises and says, "I move to substitute 'green' for 'blue.'" President Alvarez pauses, believing Roland has used the wrong term for a change of just one word. How should the chair guide the member, and what is the proper form?
gold: "Substitute" is a technical term reserved for replacing a complete paragraph or the entire text of a resolution or motion, and it does not apply to changing a single word. The correct form here is to "strike out 'blue' and insert 'green'" (Form 3(a)). The chair should have Roland restate the motion in that form; it requires a second, is debatable, and is adopted by a majority vote.

ref.quote: substitute is a technical parliamentary term that is not applied to anything less than a complete paragraph of one or more sentences, so that this term is not applicable to Form 3(a).) 12:9 Forms 1(a), 2(a), and 3(a), relating to words, can be applied to change the wording within a single sentence, or occasionally within two or more consecutive sentences that make up a part of a single paragraph.

prompt: The Marlowe Debate Society is amending a lengthy pending resolution. Member Nadia Frost wants to alter only a few words spread across one sentence of the first paragraph, while member Owen Blake insists that because the resolution is long, any change must be handled as a paragraph-level amendment. Chair Ferreira asks which approach is correct when the change touches only wording inside a single sentence. What should the chair explain?
gold: Amendments dealing with words (inserting, striking out, or striking out and inserting words) can be applied to change the wording within a single sentence, so Nadia may amend just those few words without treating the whole paragraph. The paragraph-level forms are used when inserting, striking, or substituting a whole paragraph or larger unit. Nadia's word-level amendment requires a second, is debatable, and is adopted by a majority vote.

ref.quote: Forms 1(a), 2(a), and 3(a), relating to words, can be applied to change the wording within a single sentence, or occasionally within two or more consecutive sentences that make up a part of a single paragraph.

prompt: At the Sunridge Homeowners Association, a main motion is pending. A primary amendment to insert "$500" is pending, and a secondary amendment to change it to "$750" is also pending. Member Beatriz Lang now rises and moves to amend the "$750" figure to "$800." President Nguyen must decide whether Beatriz's motion can be received. What is the ruling and why?
gold: Beatriz's motion would be an amendment of the third degree, because it seeks to amend a pending secondary amendment, and a third-degree amendment is not permitted. The chair should rule it out of order and proceed to take the vote on the pending secondary amendment. Only one primary and one secondary amendment may be pending at a time; each needs a second and a majority vote.

ref.quote: An amendment of the third degree is not permitted.

prompt: The Glenwood Choir Association has a main motion pending, with a primary amendment already before the assembly. Member Hassan Ali moves to amend that primary amendment (a secondary amendment), and it is stated by the chair. Member Carla Dunn then asks the chair whether she may in turn move to amend Hassan's secondary amendment so the assembly can "fine-tune it further." How should President Rojas answer?
gold: The motion to Amend can be applied to a pending primary amendment, producing a secondary amendment, but it cannot be applied to a secondary amendment. Carla's proposed motion is therefore out of order, and the chair should decline to entertain it. The pending secondary amendment stands for disposition, requiring a second and a majority vote.

ref.quote: Amend can be applied to itself (that is, to a pending primary amendment), so that a secondary amendment (or "amendment to an amendment") will result, but it cannot be applied to a secondary amendment .

prompt: At the Ashford Booster Club, a primary amendment and a secondary amendment are both pending. Member Priscilla Vane thinks the pending secondary amendment does not go far enough and wants the assembly to consider a different secondary change, but she knows she cannot pile a further amendment on top of the one already pending. She asks Chair Whitcomb how she can get her alternative idea before the assembly. What should the chair advise?
gold: Because a third-degree amendment is not permitted, Priscilla cannot amend the pending secondary amendment. Instead, while the secondary amendment is pending she may state that if it is voted down she will offer her own alternative secondary amendment in its place, indicating it briefly. The chair should proceed to a vote on the current secondary amendment first; a new secondary amendment would then need a second and a majority vote.

ref.quote: To accomplish the same purpose, a member can say, while a secondary amendment is pending, that if it is voted down, he will offer another secondary amendment—which he can then indicate briefly—in its place.

prompt: The Pinehurst Civic League has a lengthy main motion pending. Member Darius Cole offers a single motion to amend that bundles three unrelated changes: correcting a date, adding a new clause about parking, and striking a sentence about signage. Member Lena Voss objects that she supports two of the changes but opposes the parking clause, and asks to vote on them separately. Chair Abernathy must decide whether Lena is entitled to a separate vote. What is the ruling?
gold: A series of amendments to a pending motion may be offered in one motion to Amend, but unless they are conforming amendments, any member may demand a separate vote on one or more of them. Because these three changes are independent and do not all have to stand or fall together, Lena is entitled to her demand, and the chair should grant a separate vote on the parking clause. The other amendments are then voted on together; each amendment is decided by a majority vote.

ref.quote: Unless these amendments meet the standard for conforming amendments described immediately below, any member may demand a separate vote on one or more of them.

prompt: At the Harborview Foundation, a pending resolution refers to the "Ways and Means Committee" in five separate places. Member Iris Sunwoo moves "to strike out 'Ways and Means Committee' wherever it appears and insert 'Finance Committee' in lieu thereof." Member Todd Klein demands a separate vote on each of the five occurrences. President Barros must decide whether the demand is allowed. How should the chair rule?
gold: These are conforming amendments: all five changes must be made together, or the resolution would be left incoherent, so they are properly offered in a single motion to Amend and may not be divided. The chair should deny Todd's demand for separate votes and take one vote on the whole conforming amendment. It requires a second, is debatable, and is adopted by a majority vote.

ref.quote: In such cases (where all of the individual amendments must be made, if any one of them is made, in order to leave a coherent resolution pending if the motion to Amend is adopted), the proposed amendments are offered in a single motion, as in the example given. Such proposed amendments may not be divided.

prompt: The Lakewood Scholars Committee is considering a resolution that repeatedly names a fund the "Emergency Fund." Member Priya Sethi wants to rename it the "Relief Fund" everywhere it appears so the document stays consistent. She is uncertain whether she must move a separate amendment for each occurrence or may do it all at once. Chair Donnelly asks how such a change should be handled. What should the chair explain?
gold: When several changes throughout a resolution are needed to achieve one end, they are proposed and adopted by a single subsidiary motion to Amend rather than one motion per occurrence. Priya should move to strike "Emergency Fund" wherever it appears and insert "Relief Fund" in lieu thereof, as a single conforming amendment that may not be divided. It requires a second, is debatable, and is adopted by a majority vote.

ref.quote: Sometimes several changes throughout a motion or resolution are needed in order to achieve one end, in which case these separate amendments are proposed and adopted by a single subsidiary motion to Amend.

prompt: At the Rockford City Council, a motion is pending "that the Council commend the Public Works Director for the snow-removal plan." Member Gloria Estrada moves to strike "commend" and insert "reprimand." A member protests that this reverses the intent of the motion and so cannot possibly be a proper amendment. President Halvorsen must rule on whether the amendment is in order. How should the chair rule?
gold: An amendment may be hostile to, or even defeat, the spirit of the original motion and still be germane, so long as it does not introduce an independent question. Reprimanding versus commending both address the Council's opinion of the same conduct, so the amendment is germane and in order despite reversing the intent. The chair should admit the amendment; it needs a second, is debatable, and carries on a majority vote.

ref.quote: An amendment cannot introduce an independent question; but an amendment can be hostile to, or even defeat, the spirit of the original motion and still be germane.

prompt: The Millbrook Literary Society has pending a motion "that the Society purchase a new desk for the Secretary." Member Frank Osei moves to amend by adding "and the payment of the President's travel expenses to the state convention." President Lindqvist must decide whether this addition can be received as an amendment. What is the ruling and the reasoning?
gold: The amendment is not germane and is out of order, because paying the President's convention travel expenses introduces an independent question unrelated to furnishing the Secretary with a desk. To be germane, an amendment must involve the same question raised by the pending motion, as adding "and matching chair" would. The chair should rule the amendment out of order; a germane amendment would need a second and a majority vote.

ref.quote: It would be germane and in order to amend by inserting after “desk” the words “and matching chair,” since both relate to providing the secretary with the necessary furniture.

prompt: During a contentious meeting of the Ferndale Watershed Alliance, member Yuki Tanaka moves an amendment that the chair, President Reyes, genuinely cannot decide is germane to the pending motion. It is an important resolution and the room is divided. Reyes does not want to guess wrong. What are the chair's proper options for handling the doubtful amendment?
gold: In borderline cases where germaneness is difficult to judge, the chair should, when in doubt, admit the amendment, or in important cases refer the question of germaneness to the assembly for decision. Given the importance and division here, the chair may put the question of whether the amendment is germane to a vote of the assembly. That germaneness question is decided by a majority; if admitted, the amendment itself needs a second and a majority vote.

ref.quote: There are borderline cases where a presiding officer will find it difficult to judge the germaneness of an amendment. Whenever in doubt, he should admit the amendment or, in important cases, refer the decision to the assembly: "The chair is in doubt and will ask the assembly to decide whether the amendment is germane. [Debate, if any, provided that debate is in order.] The question is on whether the amendment is germane to the resolution [or "to the primary amendment"].

prompt: At the Tri-County Federation, a motion is pending "that our delegates be instructed to vote in favor of the increase in Federation dues." Member Omar Farouk moves to amend by inserting "not" before "be," so it would read "that our delegates not be instructed to vote in favor." President Whitaker must decide whether this amendment is in order. How should the chair rule?
gold: The amendment is out of order, because inserting "not" before "be" merely makes adopting the amended motion equivalent to rejecting the original motion, which members can already accomplish by voting the motion down. The chair should rule it not in order. It would, however, be in order to move to insert "not" before "to" ("instructed not to vote in favor"), since that gives a different instruction; such a proper amendment needs a second and a majority vote.

ref.quote: One that merely makes the adoption of the amended question equivalent to a rejection of the original motion.

prompt: The Ridgemont Alumni Chapter has pending a motion to "postpone the question until 2 P.M." Member Sylvia Chen moves to amend it by striking "until 2 P.M." and inserting "indefinitely." Chair Delacroix suspects that this change would not really amend the motion but turn it into something else entirely. How should the chair rule on the amendment?
gold: The amendment is out of order, because it would have the effect of converting one parliamentary motion (Postpone to a Certain Time) into a different motion (Postpone Indefinitely). An amendment may not transform one motion into another. The chair should rule the amendment not in order; a proper amendment to the postponement, such as changing the hour, would need a second and a majority vote.

ref.quote: One that would have the effect of converting one parliamentary motion into another.

prompt: At the Northgate Preservation Trust, a resolution is pending that begins "Resolved, That the Trust oppose the proposed rezoning." Member Victor Amoah moves to amend by striking out the word "Resolved" at the beginning. President Castellano must decide whether this amendment can be entertained. What is the ruling?
gold: The amendment is out of order, because an amendment may not strike out the word "Resolved" or other enacting words of a resolution. The chair should rule it not in order and leave the enacting word intact. Members who oppose the resolution should instead vote against it when it is put to the assembly; a proper amendment would require a second and a majority vote.

ref.quote: One that strikes out the word “Resolved” or other enacting words.

prompt: The Hillcrest Garden Club is considering a resolution with three "Whereas" clauses followed by a resolving clause directing the club to sponsor a spring plant sale. While a member's amendment to the resolving clause (changing "spring" to "autumn") is still pending and not yet voted on, another member, Dana Whitfield, rises and moves to amend the second "Whereas" clause to correct a date. The chair is unsure whether to entertain Dana's motion right now. How should the chair rule?
gold: The chair should rule Dana's motion out of order for now. The preamble is not opened to amendment until after amendment of the resolving clauses has been completed, so the pending amendment to the resolving clause must be disposed of first. Only when the resolving clauses are finished are the "Whereas" clauses opened to amendment; the chair should so state and return to the pending business.

ref.quote: When a resolution has a preamble (one or more clauses beginning “Whereas”), the preamble is not opened to amendment until after amendment of the resolving clauses has been completed.

prompt: At the Riverside Homeowners Association, all of the resolving clauses of a pending resolution have been perfected and no further amendment to them is offered. President Marcus Feld announces that the resolution is ready for a vote and begins to put the question. A member interrupts, objecting that the preamble still contains an error he wishes to fix. What should the chair have done before putting the question, and is the member's objection sound?
gold: The member's objection is sound. Once amendment of the resolving clauses is complete, the chair must open the preamble to amendment before the resolution as a whole is voted on. Rather than proceed to the final vote, President Feld should declare the preamble open to amendment and entertain the member's correction; a single vote on the entire resolution comes only afterward.

ref.quote: When a resolution has a preamble (one or more clauses beginning “Whereas”), the preamble is not opened to amendment until after amendment of the resolving clauses has been completed.

prompt: The Fairmont Rotary Club has a resolution with a lengthy preamble and two resolving clauses. After the resolving clauses were amended and then the preamble was amended, the secretary asks the chair, Priya Nair, whether the assembly must take one vote to adopt the preamble and a separate vote to adopt the resolving clauses. How should the chair proceed?
gold: No separate votes are taken. After any amendment of the preamble, a single vote is taken on the question of adopting the entire resolution or paper—preamble and resolving clauses together. The chair should state the question on adopting the entire resolution and take one vote, which requires a majority.

ref.quote: After any amendment of the preamble, a single vote is taken on the question of adopting the entire resolution or paper .

prompt: A resolution before the Lakeside Camera Club contains the words "and shall meet monthly." Earlier in the same meeting, the assembly voted down a motion to strike out "and shall meet monthly." Later in that meeting, member Tom Alvarez moves to strike out the identical words "and shall meet monthly." May the chair entertain his motion?
gold: No. The assembly has already voted, during the same session, that those words shall form part of the resolution, so a fresh motion to strike out the identical words raises the same question of content and effect and is not in order. The chair must rule Tom's motion out of order. It could be admitted only if the wording or place were changed to present an essentially new question, or through reconsideration of the earlier vote.

ref.quote: After the assembly has voted that certain words (or a certain paragraph) shall, or shall not, form part of a pending resolution, it is not in order, during the same session at which that vote was taken, to make another motion to Amend that raises the same question of content and effect.

prompt: The Delta Debate Society earlier in its meeting adopted an amendment inserting the phrase "in writing" into a motion. Now member Gwen Ash moves an amendment that would strike out "in writing" and insert "by email or in writing," which she argues is a genuinely new proposal. The chair must decide whether this raises a question already decided. How should the chair analyze and rule?
gold: The chair must judge whether the new motion presents essentially the same question of content and effect as the one already decided. Because striking out "in writing" and inserting "by email or in writing" changes the substance and presents a materially different question, it is not barred and is in order. Common sense should guide the chair: the rule bars only motions raising a question the assembly has already settled, not real improvements.

ref.quote: Common sense should guide the presiding officer in interpreting the rules, both to give freedom for improvement of the main motion finally to be voted on, and at the same time to protect the assembly from motions for amendment that present questions it has already decided.

prompt: Earlier in a meeting of the Summit Chess Federation, the assembly voted down a motion to insert the word "annual" before "dues." Later in the same meeting, member Raj Patel moves to insert the word "annual" before "dues" in the identical place. A point of order is raised against his motion. How should the chair rule?
gold: The chair should rule the motion out of order. The assembly has already decided, during the same session, that the word "annual" shall not form part of the resolution in that place; re-offering the identical insertion raises the same question of content and effect and is not in order. It could be admitted only if the words or the place were changed so as to present an essentially new question.

ref.quote: After the assembly has voted that certain words (or a certain paragraph) shall, or shall not, form part of a pending resolution, it is not in order, during the same session at which that vote was taken, to make another motion to Amend that raises the same question of content and effect.

prompt: The Northgate Theater Guild has pending a motion "That the Guild produce two plays next season." Member Ellen Cho rises and says, "I move to amend by inserting the word 'musical.'" She does not indicate where in the motion the word should go. How should the chair handle this?
gold: The chair should require Ellen to specify the exact place of insertion, because a motion to insert words must name the word before or after which, or the words between which, the new word is to be placed. Until she states where "musical" goes (for example, before "plays"), the chair cannot state the question. Once properly stated, the amendment needs a second, is debatable, and is adopted by a majority vote.

ref.quote: A motion to insert words must specify the exact place of insertion by naming the word before or after which, or the words between which, the insertion is to be made—whichever will better locate and point out the effect of the change.

prompt: During a meeting of the Bayview Sailing Club, a pending motion uses the word "member" three times. Carlos Mendez moves "to strike out the word 'member,'" but it is not clear which occurrence he means. What should the chair do before proceeding?
gold: The chair should ask Carlos to specify the location, because a motion to strike out words must specify their location when it is not otherwise clear. Once he identifies which occurrence of "member" he intends, the chair can state the question. The motion requires a second, is debatable, and is adopted by a majority vote.

ref.quote: A motion to strike out words must specify their location when it is not otherwise clear.

prompt: The Central Valley Co-op has pending a motion to "hold the annual meeting in March." Member Nadia Rahman moves to strike out "March" and insert "May." Another member then moves to divide the question so the assembly can vote separately on striking out "March" and on inserting "May." Is that division in order?
gold: No. A motion to strike out and insert is a single motion whose two parts cannot be separated, either by a division of the question or by secondary amendment. The chair should rule the proposed division out of order; the strike-out-and-insert is debatable and is voted on as one question requiring a majority.

ref.quote: The motion to strike out and insert is especially applicable in situations where it may be impossible to secure the desired result without making the act of “striking out” inseparable from that of “inserting”—as may happen if some members are unwilling to vote for the one unless assured of the other.

prompt: At a meeting of the Westfield Teachers Association, member Lena Brooks proposes what she calls a "friendly amendment" to a pending motion, and the maker of the main motion, Sam Ortiz, says, "I accept it." The chair is about to treat the motion as changed accordingly without any vote. Is that correct?
gold: No. A "friendly amendment" has no special status; once the chair has stated the main motion, it belongs to the assembly, not its maker. Regardless of whether Sam accepts it, the amendment must be opened to debate and voted on formally under the ordinary amendment rules—needing a second, debatable, majority vote—unless it is adopted by unanimous consent.

ref.quote: Regardless of whether or not the maker of the main motion "accepts" the amendment, it must be opened to debate and voted on formally (unless adopted by unanimous consent) and is handled under the same rules as amendments generally .

prompt: The Glenwood Choir Board is debating a motion the chair has already stated. Member Priya Rao offers a "friendly amendment," and the chair asks the maker whether he accepts it; he does. A director then objects, saying she opposes the change. The chair announces, "Since the maker accepted it, the change stands." Was the chair right?
gold: No, the chair was wrong. The maker's acceptance does not control, because once the chair states the motion it is in the assembly's hands. Since a member objects, it cannot be adopted by unanimous consent, so the so-called friendly amendment must be opened to debate and put to a formal vote under the regular amendment rules, requiring a majority.

ref.quote: Regardless of whether or not the maker of the main motion "accepts" the amendment, it must be opened to debate and voted on formally (unless adopted by unanimous consent) and is handled under the same rules as amendments generally .

prompt: At the Ridgeline Booster Club, a member offers a minor "friendly amendment" that merely corrects a typo in a pending motion. The chair senses that no one objects and would like to avoid a formal vote. May the chair adopt the amendment without a formal vote, and how?
gold: Yes. Although a friendly amendment has no privileged status and must ordinarily be debated and voted on like any amendment, it may be adopted by unanimous consent. The chair may say, "If there is no objection, the amendment is adopted," and, hearing none, declare it adopted. If any member objects, the amendment must be put to a formal majority vote.

ref.quote: Regardless of whether or not the maker of the main motion "accepts" the amendment, it must be opened to debate and voted on formally (unless adopted by unanimous consent) and is handled under the same rules as amendments generally .

prompt: The Monroe County Historical Society has a main motion pending with two amendments being handled in sequence. The chair, Grace Lin, notices members seem confused about which motion they are actually voting on at each step. What duty does RONR place on the chair when stating and putting each amendment in such a series?
gold: When stating the question on each motion in a series of amendments, and again when putting it to a vote, the chair must take care that members understand which motion is under immediate consideration and the effect of adopting or rejecting it. Grace should clearly identify the immediately pending amendment, may read how the motion would read if the amendment were adopted, and make plain that it is the amendment being voted on. This is a duty of the chair, requiring no motion from the floor.

ref.quote: In stating the question on each motion in any series involving amendments, and again when putting the motion to a vote, the chair should take care that the members understand which motion is under immediate consideration, as well as the effect of adopting or rejecting it.

prompt: At the Portside Yacht Club, the chair takes the vote on a primary amendment to a pending resolution, and the amendment carries. The chair simply says, "The amendment passes," and then pauses, unsure what to say next. What must the chair announce, and what becomes immediately pending?
gold: After taking the vote, the chair must announce the result and then state the question that consequently becomes immediately pending. Here the chair should declare the amendment adopted and immediately state that the question is now on the main motion as amended, which is again open to debate and further amendment. Adoption of the main motion as amended will require a majority.

ref.quote: After taking the vote, the chair announces the result of the vote on the amendment, and then states the question that consequently becomes immediately pending: CHAIR: The ayes have it and the amendment is adopted.

prompt: The Cedar Falls Library Friends have just voted down a primary amendment to a pending resolution. A member asks the chair whether any further amendments may now be offered, or whether the assembly must proceed straight to a vote on the resolution. How should the chair respond?
gold: The chair should explain that, regardless of whether the amendment was adopted or rejected, the resolution is again open to debate and it is in order to offer a different amendment. The assembly is not required to vote at once; further germane amendments and debate on the main motion are in order before the resolution is finally adopted by a majority vote.

ref.quote: At this point, regardless of whether the amendment is adopted or rejected, the resolution is again open to debate, and it is in order to offer a different amendment.

conceptName: Nature and procedure for filling blanks
prompt: The Auburn Sportsmen's Club is debating a motion to set the date of its annual banquet, and members have half a dozen different dates in mind. The chair worries that under ordinary amendment rules only three alternatives can be pending at once. Is there a device that lets the assembly consider all the proposed dates together, and what is its effect?
gold: Yes—the chair should use the device of filling a blank. Filling blanks, though not itself a form of amendment, is a closely related device by which an unlimited number of alternative choices for a particular specification can be pending at the same time, an exception to the rule that only one primary and one secondary amendment may be pending. A blank is created for the date, members suggest dates (no second required), and the assembly then chooses among them.

ref.quote: Filling blanks, although not a form of amendment in itself, is a closely related device by which an unlimited number of alternative choices for a particular specification in a main motion or primary amendment can be pending at the same time.

conceptName: Nature and procedure for filling blanks
prompt: The Harborview Kiwanis has created a blank for the number of members on a new committee, and several numbers have been suggested and debated. Member Joel Tan asks how the assembly finally settles on a number, and whether choosing the number also adopts the motion. How should the chair explain the procedure?
gold: When debate on the suggestions ends, the assembly decides by majority vote which suggestion shall fill the blank, each proposal being voted on in turn until one receives a majority. Filling the blank does not adopt the motion: the vote that fills a blank does not decide the question that contained it. As soon as the blank is filled, the chair states the question on adopting the completed motion, which remains pending and needs its own majority vote.

ref.quote: When debate on the suggestions has ended (or, if the pending motion is undebatable, when no more suggestions are offered), the assembly decides, by majority vote, which suggestion(s) shall fill the blank.

conceptName: Nature and procedure for filling blanks
prompt: The Grandview PTA is filling a blank for the amount of a scholarship, and four amounts have been suggested. A member complains that under normal amendment procedure the last amount moved would have to be voted on first, which seems unfair. How does filling blanks address this concern, and how many alternatives may be considered?
gold: Filling blanks is designed for exactly this situation. In ordinary amendment, at most three alternatives can be pending and the last one moved must be voted on first; in filling blanks the number of alternatives is not limited, and members may weigh all choices and vote on them in a fair and logical order. Each amount is voted on in turn until one receives a majority, which then fills the blank.
keywords: number of alternatives is not limited

ref.quote: In amending by the ordinary method, a maximum of three alternatives can be pending at once, and the last one moved must be voted on first. In filling blanks, the number of alternatives is not limited; members have an opportunity to weigh all choices before voting and to vote on them in a fair and logical order.

prompt: The National Ferns Society must decide which city will host next year's convention, and members are enthusiastic about five different cities. President Owen Bradley asks whether there is a preferred parliamentary method for choosing among so many proposed cities, rather than moving them one at a time as amendments. What do you advise?
gold: Advise filling a blank. Cases containing names of places are precisely the kind adapted to filling blanks, which allows all five cities to be considered together rather than as competing amendments limited to three. A blank for the host city is created, members suggest cities (no second needed), debate follows, and the city receiving a majority fills the blank. That vote does not by itself adopt the underlying motion, which the chair then puts as the completed question.

ref.quote: Among cases adapted to such treatment are main motions or primary amendments containing names of persons or places, dates, numbers, or amounts.

prompt: The Elmhurst Volunteer Fire Company has a motion to authorize an equipment purchase "at a cost not to exceed $___," with the figure left open, and members have proposed several different dollar limits. Chief Dana Roswell asks the parliamentarian how to handle the competing amounts efficiently. What is the recommended approach?
gold: The recommended approach is filling a blank, since motions containing amounts are among the cases adapted to this treatment. A blank is created for the dollar limit, members suggest amounts (no second required), and the amounts are voted on in turn in a fair and logical order until one receives a majority and fills the blank. That vote does not adopt the purchase; the chair then states the question on the completed motion, which needs its own majority vote.

ref.quote: Among cases adapted to such treatment are main motions or primary amendments containing names of persons or places, dates, numbers, or amounts.

prompt: The Springdale Community Band is fixing both the date of its summer concert and the number of rehearsals beforehand; several dates and several numbers have each been proposed on the floor. Director Mia Fontaine wants an orderly way to let members choose among all the suggestions. How should this be handled under RONR?
gold: Both specifications are well suited to filling blanks, because cases containing dates and numbers are among those adapted to this device. Separate blanks are created for the date and for the number of rehearsals; members suggest values (no second needed), debate them, and each blank is filled by the suggestion receiving a majority, voted in a fair order. Filling the blanks does not adopt the motion—the chair afterward states the question on the completed motion, requiring its own majority vote.

ref.quote: Among cases adapted to such treatment are main motions or primary amendments containing names of persons or places, dates, numbers, or amounts.

conceptName: Main vs subsidiary motion to Commit
prompt: At the Riverside Camera Club, no business is currently before the assembly. During new business, member Dana Whitfield rises and says, "I move that we create a committee to study whether the club should start a youth mentorship program and report its findings at our fall meeting." The club has never previously considered anything about a mentorship program. The presiding officer is unsure whether this should be treated as the subsidiary motion to refer something to committee. How should the chair classify and handle Whitfield's motion?
gold: Because no question is pending, this is not the subsidiary motion to Commit; it is a main motion, and specifically an original main motion since it relates to an entirely new subject. As a main motion it requires a second, is debatable and amendable, and is adopted by a majority vote. The chair should process it as an ordinary main motion, not as a subsidiary referral.

ref.quote: When a motion proposes to assign a task or refer a matter to a committee when no question is pending, such a motion is not the subsidiary motion to Commit, but is a main motion.

conceptName: Main vs subsidiary motion to Commit
prompt: Earlier this year the Oakdale Neighborhood Association voted to renovate its community hall. Tonight nothing is pending on the floor. During the meeting, member Luis Ramirez says, "I move that the question of selecting a general contractor for the hall renovation be referred to a committee of three appointed by the president." The secretary asks the chair how to characterize this motion, given that the assembly already adopted the renovation itself. What is the correct ruling?
gold: Since no question is pending, the motion is not the subsidiary motion to Commit but a main motion. Because it is pursuant to a subject on which the assembly has already acted (the renovation), it is an incidental main motion. It requires a second, is debatable and amendable, and needs a majority vote for adoption.

ref.quote: It is an incidental main motion if the assignment or referral is pursuant to a subject on which the assembly has already taken some action; but it is an original main motion if the matter to be assigned or referred relates to a new subject.

conceptName: Main vs subsidiary motion to Commit
prompt: At the Lakeside Historical Society, the program committee has just finished presenting a report recommending that the society launch a public lecture series, but the report contains no resolution or motion putting the recommendation into effect. Member Priscilla Nolan believes proper resolutions still need to be drafted and rises to move that the whole matter be sent to a committee to prepare them. Nothing is pending in the form of a motion. How should the chair treat Nolan's motion, and what does it take to adopt it?
gold: Because no resolution or motion is pending, this is not the subsidiary motion to Commit but a main motion: with no resolutions before the assembly, a member may move to refer the matter to the same or another committee to draft them. As a main motion it needs a second, is debatable and amendable, and is adopted by a majority vote.
