ref.quote: In cases where the committee has offered no resolutions embodying its recommendations and the drafting of satisfactory resolution(s) covering them is likely to require the further attention of a committee, another member can move to refer the matter to the same or another committee for this purpose.

prompt: The Westbrook Rotary Club is debating a resolution to fund a scholarship, and a primary amendment to raise the dollar amount is currently pending on that resolution. Member Grace Okafor then moves to refer the resolution to the finance committee, and the referral is adopted. A confused member asks whether the pending amendment is now dead, or must be voted on separately before the referral takes effect. How should the chair rule on what happens to that amendment?
gold: The pending amendment adheres to the main motion and therefore goes to the committee together with the resolution; it is not dropped, and it is not voted on separately. The committee considers it and reports it back with the main motion. The motion to Commit that sent it there needed a second, was debatable and amendable, and was adopted by a majority vote.

ref.quote: On the other hand, any pending amendments, motions for Division of a Question or Consideration by Paragraph or Seriatim, and adhering debatable appeals (or points of order submitted to the judgment of the assembly) go to the committee with the main motion, and are reported with it. (No question can be referred to a committee while an undebatable appeal or point of order is pending.) If, at the time a main motion is committed, a motion to reconsider an adhering subsidiary or incidental motion is pending or has been made but not yet taken up, the motion to Reconsider is thereafter ignored.

prompt: During a meeting of the Fairview Garden Society, a main motion to purchase new greenhouse equipment is pending, and a motion to postpone the question indefinitely has been made and is now immediately pending. Member Harold Stein instead moves to refer the equipment question to a committee, and the assembly adopts the referral by a majority. The secretary asks whether the indefinite-postponement motion also travels to the committee along with everything else. What is the correct ruling?
gold: The motion to Postpone Indefinitely does not adhere to the main motion, so it does not go to the committee; it is simply dropped from further consideration when the main motion is referred. Only genuinely adhering matters (such as pending amendments) accompany the main motion. The referral itself was proper as a majority-vote decision.

ref.quote: If a motion to Postpone Indefinitely is pending when the main motion is referred to a committee, the motion for indefinite postponement is dropped from further consideration.

prompt: At the Green Valley Homeowners Association, a resolution is pending, and earlier in the meeting a member made a motion to reconsider the vote on an amendment that adheres to that resolution; that reconsideration has not yet been taken up. Member Tomiko Sato now moves to commit the resolution to a special committee, and the assembly agrees. A member asks whether the pending motion to reconsider must still be dealt with. How should the chair rule?
gold: Because the motion to reconsider was aimed at an adhering subsidiary/incidental motion and had only been made but not yet taken up when the main motion was committed, it is thereafter ignored. The committee proceeds with the resolution and its adhering amendments. The motion to Commit that referred the matter required a second, was debatable and amendable, and passed by a majority vote.

ref.quote: If, at the time a main motion is committed, a motion to reconsider an adhering subsidiary or incidental motion is pending or has been made but not yet taken up, the motion to Reconsider is thereafter ignored.

prompt: A main motion to adopt a new membership policy is pending before the Pinehurst Choral Association. Member Elena Marsh, recognized on the floor, says, "I move to refer this to the membership committee," and another member immediately says, "Second." The chair, new to the role, is unsure whether a referral like this needs a second and what proportion of the vote is required to send the matter to committee. How should the chair proceed?
gold: The subsidiary motion to Commit does require a second, which was given, so it is properly before the assembly. It is debatable and amendable, and it is adopted by a majority vote, not two-thirds. The chair should open it to limited debate if desired and then put it to a majority vote.

ref.quote: Requires a majority vote. 8. An affirmative vote on the motion to Commit can be reconsidered if the committee has not begun consideration of the question.

prompt: The Cedar Falls Booster Club is considering a motion to refer a pending fundraising resolution to a committee. As soon as the referral is stated, member Bradford Kline is recognized and begins delivering a lengthy argument about why the fundraising plan itself is a bad idea and would waste money. Another member objects that this is off-limits. Is debate on the referral permitted at all, and if so, may Kline argue the merits of the fundraising plan this way?
gold: The motion to Commit is debatable, so Kline may speak, but debate is confined to the desirability of committing the question and the appropriate details of the referral, not the merits of the underlying fundraising plan. The chair should stop Kline from arguing the merits of the main question and keep debate to the referral. The motion is then decided by a majority vote.

ref.quote: Is debatable. The debate can extend only to the desirability of committing the main question and to the appropriate details of the motion to Commit, as explained below, however, and not to the merits of the main question. 6.

prompt: At the Northgate Arts Council, a motion to refer a pending grant proposal to a special committee of three is on the floor. Member Yusuf Demir wants to change the referral so the special committee has five members chosen by ballot and is instructed to report by the March meeting. Another member insists that a motion to send something to committee is a fixed, take-it-or-leave-it proposal that cannot be altered. Is Demir's proposed change in order, and what vote would it take?
gold: The motion to Commit is amendable, so Demir's changes are in order. For a special committee it may be amended as to the committee's composition and manner of selection, and for any committee as to the instructions it is to follow, so adjusting the size, the ballot selection, and the reporting deadline are all proper amendments. Such an amendment is adopted by a majority vote.

ref.quote: Is amendable as follows: in the case of a standing committee, as to the committee to which the main question is to be referred; in the case of a special committee, as to the committee’s composition and manner of selection; and in the case of any form of committee, as to any instructions the committee is to follow.

prompt: At a meeting of the Lakeside Rowing Club, a resolution to purchase two new racing shells is under debate. Member Dana Whitfield rises and says, "The vendor won't have final pricing until next week's meeting, so I move that we put off deciding this resolution until then." A director objects that the club can only kill a motion, not shelve it to a date. How should the chair treat Dana's motion, and what are its handling requirements?
gold: Dana has moved the subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (Postpone Definitely), which is in order: it puts off action on the pending resolution to a definite time. It requires a second, is debatable (limited to whether and when to postpone), is amendable as to the time, and needs a majority vote. It is not Postpone Indefinitely, which would kill the measure.

ref.quote: The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event. (The expression "to defer" should be avoided, since it is often subject to vague usage.) This motion can be moved regardless of how much debate there has been on the motion it proposes to postpone.

prompt: During the regular meeting of the Fairview Historical Society, no report is currently pending. Member Louis Trent gains the floor and says, "I move to postpone the Finance Committee's report until our April meeting." The presiding officer starts to treat this as an ordinary subsidiary postponement. Is that classification correct, and how should it actually be handled?
gold: Because the committee's report is not actually pending, this is not the subsidiary motion to Postpone; it is an incidental main motion setting the time for the report. As a main motion it requires a second, is debatable and amendable, and is adopted by a majority vote. The chair should state it as a main motion, not as the subsidiary Postpone.

ref.quote: When a motion proposes to postpone a matter that is not pending-for example, the hearing of a committee's report- such a motion is not the subsidiary motion to Postpone, but is an incidental main motion .

prompt: The Glenwood Garden Club previously voted to hold its annual dinner on October 15. At tonight's meeting, with nothing else pending on the subject, Member Priya Kapoor moves "to postpone for three weeks the dinner scheduled for October 15." A member calls it a simple motion to postpone requiring a majority. What is the correct characterization of Priya's motion, and how is it adopted?
gold: Because the dinner date was already fixed by a prior vote, changing it is not the subsidiary motion to Postpone but a particular case of Amend Something Previously Adopted. It requires a second and is adopted by a two-thirds vote, or a majority with previous notice, or a majority of the entire membership (any one suffices). The chair should handle it under those rules, not as an ordinary postponement.

ref.quote: If the effect would be to change action already taken by the assembly, as, for example, "to postpone for three weeks the dinner scheduled for October 15," such a motion is a particular case of the motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted .

prompt: At the Riverton Arts Council, a main motion to adopt a new grant policy is pending, and a motion to amend it by inserting a residency requirement is also pending. Member Omar Reyes moves to postpone consideration to the next meeting. A member asks whether the pending amendment dies or travels with the main motion if the postponement is adopted. How should the chair answer?
gold: The postponement applies to the main question and carries the adhering amendment (and any pending Postpone Indefinitely or Commit) with it; the amendment does not die. When the question is resumed at the next meeting, business stands in the same condition as before the postponement. The motion to Postpone needs a second, is debatable and amendable, and takes a majority vote.

ref.quote: its application to a main question also affects any motions to Postpone Indefinitely, Amend, or Commit that may be pending, as noted above.

prompt: During debate on a bylaws amendment at the Delta Camera Club, the chair's ruling on a point of order is appealed, and that appeal must be decided before the main question. Member Grace Lin then moves to postpone the appeal alone until the next meeting so tempers can cool. May the appeal be postponed by itself?
gold: No. Because the appeal adheres to the main question (it must be decided before the main question), the motion to Postpone can be applied to it only in connection with the main question, which is thereby postponed as well. The chair cannot postpone the adhering appeal by itself; a proper motion would postpone the whole matter, requiring a second, debate, and a majority vote.
keywords: only in connection with the main question

ref.quote: the motion to Postpone can be applied to the appeal or point of order only in connection with the main question, which is thus also postponed ; can be applied to nonadhering debatable appeals (or points of order submitted to the judgment of the assembly) separately, without affecting the status of any pending questions not involved in the appeal or point of order; and can be applied to an immediately pending, debatable motion to Reconsider when it is in order to postpone the question or series of adhering questions containing the motion(s) to be reconsidered, in which case all such questions and adhering motions are postponed with the motion to Reconsider.

prompt: At the Summit Debate League's May session, the assembly had adopted the Previous Question and an order limiting debate on a resolution, then postponed the resolution to the June session. In June, Member Aaron Cole assumes those debate limits still bind and that members who already spoke twice may not speak again. Is he right about the carryover of the earlier debate orders?
gold: No. When a postponed question is resumed at a later session, any orders limiting or extending debate or ordering the Previous Question that were adopted at the original session are exhausted, and debate proceeds under the regular rules. Because it is a different session (and day), each member's right to debate begins anew and each may again speak twice. Only if consideration resumed in the same session would those orders remain in effect.

ref.quote: When consideration of a postponed question is resumed at a later session, any orders limiting or extending the limits of debate or for the Previous Question that were adopted at the original session are exhausted , and debate takes place according to the regular rules.

prompt: A resolution to endorse a candidate is pending before the Oakmont Civic Association. Member Bianca Ford moves to postpone it to the next meeting. During debate on her motion, a member begins arguing at length about why the candidate is unqualified. The chair wonders how far debate on the postponement may range. What is the rule?
gold: The motion to Postpone is debatable, but debate must not go into the merits of the main question any more than is necessary to decide whether, and to what time, the question should be postponed. The chair should confine the speaker to reasons for or against postponing and the timing. The motion needs a second, is amendable, and is adopted by a majority vote.
keywords: not the merits of the main question

ref.quote: Is debatable; but debate is limited in that it must not go into the merits of the main question any more than is necessary to enable the assembly to decide whether the main question should be postponed and to what time. 6.

prompt: At the Pinehurst Booster Club, a motion to postpone a pending budget resolution "until the next meeting" is on the floor. Member Kevin Doss wants to change the target to "until 8 P.M. tonight," and another member wants to add "and make it a special order." A director claims the postponement motion cannot be amended at all. Is that correct?
gold: Incorrect. The motion to Postpone is amendable both as to the time to which the question is postponed and as to making it a special order, so both proposed amendments are in order; each needs a second and a majority vote to adopt. Note that if the motion as amended would make the resolution a special order, the motion to Postpone itself would then require a two-thirds vote.

ref.quote: Is amendable as to the time to which the main question is to be postponed, and as to making the postponed question a special order . 7.

prompt: At the Harborview Yacht Club, a member calls out from her seat, "Just postpone it to next month!" while a resolution is pending, and sits down. No one else speaks. The chair is unsure whether the motion is properly before the assembly and what vote it will take. What must happen for this postponement to be adopted?
gold: The motion to Postpone to a Certain Time must be seconded before it can be considered, and it must be made after obtaining the floor rather than called out. In its simple and usual form it requires only a majority vote. It would rise to a two-thirds vote only if it made the question a special order.

ref.quote: Requires a majority vote in its simple and usual form. If (as originally moved or as a result of amendment) it makes a question a special order, however, the motion to Postpone then requires a two-thirds vote, because it suspends any rules that will interfere with the question’s consideration at the time specified.

prompt: The Cedar Falls Toastmasters chapter holds a regular business meeting on the same day every week. During tonight's meeting a member moves to postpone a pending motion "to our meeting three months from now." May the assembly postpone the question that far out?
gold: No. In a society meeting weekly, a question cannot, at one meeting, be postponed for longer than a week, that is, no further than the next regular business session. A postponement three months ahead is out of order, and the chair should so rule (or entertain the motion only to the next regular meeting). A proper postponement needs a second and a majority vote.

ref.quote: For example, in a society that holds regular business meetings on the same day of each week, a question cannot, at one meeting, be postponed for longer than a week.

prompt: At the annual convention of the Statewide Beekeepers Federation, a body that meets only once a year, a delegate moves to postpone a contentious resolution "to next year's convention." The chair must decide whether such a postponement is permissible. What is the ruling?
gold: The motion is out of order. Where more than a quarterly interval will elapse between meetings (as with an annual convention), a question cannot be postponed beyond the end of the present session. The resolution cannot be pushed to next year's convention; it must be disposed of this session. A permissible postponement within the session would need a second and a majority vote.
keywords: beyond the end of the present session

ref.quote: a question cannot be postponed beyond the end of the present session.

prompt: The Millbrook Ratepayers Association holds regular business meetings monthly. At the June meeting, Member Sofia Nunes moves to postpone a pending assessment proposal to the July regular meeting. A member objects that any postponement to a future meeting is improper. Is postponing to July in order?
gold: Yes. Because no more than a quarterly interval elapses between the monthly sessions, the question may be postponed until, but not beyond, the next regular business session, which here is the July meeting. The postponement is in order; it requires a second, is debatable and amendable, and is adopted by a majority vote. It could not be postponed past that next regular session.
keywords: no more than a quarterly interval

ref.quote: In cases where no more than a quarterly time interval will elapse between sessions, a question can be postponed until, but not beyond, the next regular business session.

conceptName: Postponing to create a special order
prompt: At the Ashford Alumni Association, a resolution is pending that its sponsors fear will be crowded out by other business if simply held over. Member Ted Halloran says, "I move to postpone the resolution to the next meeting and give it priority so it can interrupt whatever else is going on at that time." What kind of postponement is this, and what vote does it need?
gold: Ted is moving to postpone and make the resolution a special order, which gives it priority to interrupt other business at the set time. Because making a question a special order suspends any interfering rules, the motion to Postpone in this form requires a two-thirds vote, not a mere majority. It still needs a second and is debatable and amendable.

ref.quote: To give the question being postponed a special type of priority that will enable it to interrupt the consideration of other business at the specified time, the motion to Postpone may, by a two-thirds vote, be adopted in a form that makes the question a special order. (See Time at Which a Postponed Question Is Taken Up Again, 14:13-17, and the illustrations under Form and Example, 14:20-22.) 14:3 When a motion proposes to postpone a matter that is not pending-for example, the hearing of a committee's report- such a motion is not the subsidiary motion to Postpone, but is an incidental main motion .

conceptName: Postponing to create a special order
prompt: During a meeting of the Northgate Merchants Guild, a resolution is pending and Member Elena Vasquez wants to guarantee it comes up at the next meeting ahead of new business. She says, "I move that the question be postponed to the next meeting and be made a special order." The secretary asks what vote is required to adopt it. Advise the chair.
gold: This is a motion to Postpone and make the question a special order for the next meeting. It requires a two-thirds vote for adoption, because it will suspend any rules that would otherwise interfere with taking the question up at that time. It also requires a second and is debatable and amendable; an ordinary postponement with no special order would need only a majority.

ref.quote: I move that the question be postponed to the next meeting and be made a special order."

conceptName: Postponing to create a special order
prompt: The Brookside Homeowners Association postponed a landscaping resolution "to the next meeting" by a majority vote, without making it a special order. At the next meeting the set time effectively arrives while a different motion is being debated. Member Raj Patel demands that the chair immediately interrupt the pending debate to take up the landscaping resolution. Is he entitled to that interruption?
gold: No. Because the resolution was postponed by majority vote without being made a special order, it became a general order, which cannot interrupt pending business even though its time has arrived. It is taken up only when no other business is pending. To gain the power to interrupt, the assembly would have had to postpone and make it a special order by a two-thirds vote.

ref.quote: If the motion to Postpone does not make the postponed question a special order, it becomes a general order, which cannot interrupt pending business even if the time for which it is set has arrived or passed.

conceptName: Two ways to limit or extend debate
prompt: At the Cedar Valley Homeowners Association meeting, a bylaws amendment is being debated. Member Priya Nair rises and says, "I move that debate on this be limited to one speech of three minutes for each member." The chair, Tom Alvarez, is unsure whether this is a proper way to control debate or whether such a motion must always set a firm cutoff time. How should the chair rule, and what exactly does this motion accomplish?
gold: This is the subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate, and it is in order. That motion can limit debate either by reducing the number or length of speeches, as here, or by fixing an hour or elapsed time after which debate is closed; it need not set a cutoff time. It requires a second and a two-thirds vote, is not debatable, but is amendable. The chair should process it accordingly.
keywords: limit or extend limits of debate

ref.quote: The motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate can limit debate by: (1) reducing the number or length of speeches permitted, without including specific provision for closing debate; or (2) requiring that, at a certain later hour or after debate for a specified length of time, debate shall be closed.

conceptName: Two ways to limit or extend debate
prompt: During a heated discussion at the Riverside Debate League board, member Grace Okafor feels the standard limit of two ten-minute speeches per member is too restrictive for such an important question. She wants each member to be able to speak longer and more often. She asks the presiding officer, Daniel Cho, what motion she should make and what vote it will take. What should Daniel tell her?
gold: Grace should move to Extend the Limits of Debate, the subsidiary motion that allows more and longer speeches than the regular rules permit. It needs a second, is not debatable, is amendable, and requires a two-thirds vote because it modifies members' rights of debate. If two-thirds agree, the extended limits apply.

ref.quote: It can extend the limits of debate by allowing more and longer speeches than under the regular rules .

conceptName: Two ways to limit or extend debate
prompt: At the Fairmont Arts Council, member Luis Herrera is frustrated with a long debate and moves "that debate be closed after ten more minutes." Moments later member Anna Beck interrupts, saying, "That won't work—if you want to stop talking you have to say you want to cut it off right now." The chair, Serena Wall, must decide whether Luis's motion is proper. How should she rule and explain the distinction?
gold: Luis's motion is proper. The motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate may set a later hour or elapsed time after which debate closes; it simply cannot impose an immediate close of debate, which would instead require the Previous Question. Luis's motion needs a second, is undebatable, is amendable, and takes a two-thirds vote. Anna's objection is mistaken.
keywords: limit or extend limits of debate

ref.quote: It cannot impose an immediate closing of debate, which requires a different motion—the Previous Question.

prompt: At the Northgate Toastmasters chapter, the assembly has just adopted, by more than two-thirds, a motion that debate on the pending resolution be limited to twenty minutes. New member Kevin Diaz asks the secretary, Rosa Lin, how this action should be described in the minutes and in the chair's announcement, since it is not itself a resolution. How should Rosa characterize what the assembly has adopted?
gold: By adopting the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate, the assembly has adopted an "order" limiting debate. It should be recorded and announced as an order taking that action, not as a resolution. This term "order" here means the binding directive controlling debate, and it required the two-thirds vote just taken.
keywords: limit or extend limits of debate

ref.quote: When an assembly adopts a motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate, it is said to adopt an "order" taking such action. (The word order as applied in this sense should not be confused with the technical terms order of the day, general order, and special order as used in 3, 14, and 41.) When an order limiting or extending the limits of debate finally ceases to be in force as relates to all the motions it affected, the order is said to be "exhausted" .

prompt: The Millbrook Civic Association adopted an order limiting debate on three pending motions. Over the course of the meeting all three were voted on. Later, a member tries to invoke the same debate limit against a brand-new motion introduced afterward. The chair, Ellen Park, must decide whether the earlier order still governs. On what basis should she rule?
gold: The chair should rule that the earlier order no longer applies. Once all the motions an order limiting debate affected have been disposed of, the order is "exhausted" and ceases to be in force. It cannot be carried over to a new motion; controlling debate on the new motion would require a fresh motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate, adopted by a two-thirds vote.
keywords: limit or extend limits of debate

ref.quote: When an order limiting or extending the limits of debate finally ceases to be in force as relates to all the motions it affected, the order is said to be "exhausted" .

prompt: During a meeting of the Harborview Yacht Club, member Frank Ostrom objects: "The chair keeps saying we adopted an 'order' to limit debate. Isn't an 'order' the same as an order of the day or a special order? This is confusing." The presiding officer, Maya Sun, needs to clear up the terminology. What should she explain?
gold: Maya should explain that when an assembly adopts a motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate it adopts an "order" taking that action, and this use of the word is distinct from the technical terms order of the day, general order, and special order. Here "order" simply means the binding directive controlling debate that the assembly adopted by a two-thirds vote. Frank's concern reflects a confusion of two different uses of the term.
keywords: limit or extend limits of debate

ref.quote: When an assembly adopts a motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate, it is said to adopt an "order" taking such action. (The word order as applied in this sense should not be confused with the technical terms order of the day, general order, and special order as used in 3, 14, and 41.) When an order limiting or extending the limits of debate finally ceases to be in force as relates to all the motions it affected, the order is said to be "exhausted" .

prompt: At the Lakeside Rotary Club, member Ben Foss is speaking on a motion when member Carla Reyes calls out from her seat, "I move we limit each speech to two minutes!" No one seconds it, but the chair, Omar Nassar, is about to state the question anyway and open it for discussion. Identify every procedural error in what just happened and how the chair should have handled it.
gold: Two errors occurred. First, the motion to Limit Debate is out of order while another member has the floor, so Carla could not properly make it while Ben was speaking. Second, the motion must be seconded before it can be stated, and it received no second. The chair should also not open it to discussion, because the motion is not debatable; it is, however, amendable. It requires a two-thirds vote.
keywords: out of order when another has the floor

ref.quote: 3. Is out of order when another has the floor. 4. Must be seconded. 5. Is not debatable. 6. Is amendable, but any amendment, like the motion itself, is undebatable. 7.

prompt: At the Greenfield School Parents' Council, a motion to limit debate to one three-minute speech per member receives 9 votes in favor and 7 against out of 16 voting. The chair, Nadia Bloom, announces, "The majority is in favor, so debate is now limited." Member Paul Grant objects. Was the chair's ruling correct, and why or why not?
gold: The chair's ruling was wrong. The motion to Limit Debate requires a two-thirds vote, not a mere majority, because it takes away members' basic right to full discussion. With 9 in favor of 16 voting, two-thirds (at least 11) was not reached, so the motion fails and debate continues under the regular rules. Paul's objection is well taken.

ref.quote: Requires a two-thirds vote-because it suspends the rules, and because limiting debate takes away the basic rights of all members to full discussion and may restrict a minority's right to present its case. 8.

prompt: At the Summit Cycling Club, member Dana Wu moves "that debate be limited to fifteen minutes." Member Greg Hale immediately says, "I move to amend that to twenty minutes," and then starts arguing at length why twenty minutes is better. The chair, Ivan Petrov, must decide whether the amendment is allowed and whether Greg may keep speaking on it. How should he rule?
gold: The amendment is in order, because the motion to Limit Debate is amendable. However, Greg may not debate his amendment: any amendment to this motion, like the motion itself, is undebatable. The chair should accept the amendment (with a second) but cut off debate on it, then take a two-thirds vote first on the amendment and then on the motion as it stands.

ref.quote: 6. Is amendable, but any amendment, like the motion itself, is undebatable. 7.

conceptName: Applying to one or a series
prompt: At the Ashford Alumni Association, three debatable motions are stacked up: a main resolution, an amendment to it, and a motion to refer the whole matter to a committee (immediately pending). Member Sofia Marin moves "that debate be limited to ten minutes," saying nothing about which of the pending questions she means. The chair, Leo Chang, must decide the scope of her motion if it passes. What is the correct interpretation?
gold: Because Sofia's motion does not specify which pending questions it covers, it applies only to the immediately pending question, the motion to refer to committee. It does not automatically reach the amendment or the main resolution. If adopted by a two-thirds vote, the ten-minute limit governs only that referral motion; to cover the others she would have to say so expressly.

ref.quote: If the motion does not specify to which of these questions it is to apply, then only the immediately pending question is affected.

conceptName: Applying to one or a series
prompt: At the Bramble Creek Land Trust, several debatable motions are pending in a series. Member Hana Cole wants a twenty-minute debate cap to cover the immediately pending motion and the next one behind it, but not the main resolution at the bottom of the stack. The chair, Marcus Reed, is unsure whether a debate limit can be tailored to just part of the pending series. How should he advise her?
gold: The chair should advise that this is permitted. The motion to Limit Debate can be applied to the immediately pending motion alone, to the entire series, or to any consecutive part of the series beginning with the immediately pending question. Hana may therefore move to limit debate on those two consecutive motions, specifying them expressly. The motion needs a second, is undebatable but amendable, and requires a two-thirds vote.

ref.quote: Can be applied to any immediately pending debatable motion, to an entire series of pending debatable motions, or to any consecutive part of such a series beginning with the immediately pending question. (It therefore can be made only while a debatable motion is immediately pending.

conceptName: Applying to one or a series
prompt: At the Windsor Choral Society, the assembly adopts an order limiting debate on the pending main motion. A few minutes later, while that order is still in force, a member offers a debatable motion to postpone the main motion to the next meeting. Member Julia Frost asks the chair, Tomas Reyes, whether the new postponement motion can be freely debated or whether the existing limit reaches it. How should the chair rule?
gold: The chair should rule that the existing limit applies to the new motion. An order limiting debate reaches not only the motion on which it was ordered but also debatable subsidiary motions, motions to Reconsider, or debatable appeals that become pending later while the order is in force. So the debatable motion to postpone is governed by the same limit, unless the assembly adopts a new order by a two-thirds vote.

ref.quote: An order limiting debate applies not only to the motion(s) on which the limitation is ordered, but also to any debatable subsidiary motions, motions to Reconsider, or debatable appeals that may become pending subsequently while the order is in force.

conceptName: Later motion supersedes an earlier one
prompt: At the Pinehurst Garden Club, the assembly earlier adopted an order limiting debate on the pending resolution to fifteen minutes. Now, with the resolution still pending, member Rachel Ames moves "that debate be limited instead to one two-minute speech per member." The chair, Victor Salas, hesitates, thinking the club already settled the debate rules and cannot revisit them. Is Rachel's new motion in order?
gold: Rachel's motion is in order. Unlike main motions and lower-ranking subsidiary motions, adopting one order limiting or extending debate does not bar a later conflicting motion of the same kind. A member may move different limits, or switch to the Previous Question, at any time it is in order while the questions are still pending. The new motion needs a second, is undebatable, and requires a two-thirds vote; if adopted it supersedes the earlier order.
keywords: limit or extend limits of debate

ref.quote: Unlike the case of main motions and lower-ranking subsidiary motions (Postpone Indefinitely, Amend, Commit, Postpone), the adoption of one motion limiting or extending debate in a certain way does not prevent another such conflicting motion from being in order.

conceptName: Later motion supersedes an earlier one
prompt: At the Oakmont Investors Guild, an order extending debate is already in effect on the pending motion. Member Neil Prasad wants to replace it with a stricter order that closes debate at 4 P.M. Another member protests that the club would be "changing a rule it just voted on," which should require reopening the earlier decision first. The chair, Dana Fields, must explain why a fresh conflicting order can simply be adopted. What is the reasoning?
gold: The chair should explain that a new, conflicting order limiting or extending debate can be adopted directly while the questions are still pending. The reason is that the two-thirds vote required to modify the limits of debate simultaneously satisfies the requirement for suspending the rules, so no separate step to reopen the earlier order is needed. Neil's motion needs a second, is undebatable, and if adopted by two-thirds supersedes the earlier order.

ref.quote: The reason is that the two-thirds vote necessary for the adoption of any motion to modify the limits of debate also fulfills the requirement for suspending the rules .

conceptName: Later motion supersedes an earlier one
prompt: At the Cliffside Preservation Society, an order limiting debate to twenty minutes is in effect on a pending motion. Member Owen Blake now moves to close debate immediately by ordering an immediate vote. Member Grace Lund objects that "we already fixed the debate time, so nothing else about cutting off debate can be entertained now." The chair, Rita Voss, must decide whether Owen's motion can be made despite the existing order. How should she rule?
gold: The chair should rule Owen's motion in order. The existence of one order limiting debate does not prevent a later conflicting motion about debate, including the Previous Question, from being made while the questions are still pending. Owen has in effect moved the Previous Question, which needs a second, is undebatable and unamendable, and requires a two-thirds vote; if adopted it supersedes the earlier limiting order. Grace's objection is mistaken.

ref.quote: Unlike the case of main motions and lower-ranking subsidiary motions (Postpone Indefinitely, Amend, Commit, Postpone), the adoption of one motion limiting or extending debate in a certain way does not prevent another such conflicting motion from being in order.

prompt: At the Cedar Grove Homeowners Association, a resolution to repave the parking lot is under debate. A pending amendment would raise the budget cap to $40,000. Delia Marsh gets the floor, moves the motion that brings the assembly to an immediate vote, it is seconded, and two-thirds vote in favor. Immediately afterward, Tom Reyes rises and says he wishes to offer a further amendment lowering the figure to $30,000, and another member asks to speak against the resolution. What should the chair do?
gold: The chair must refuse both requests. Adoption of the Previous Question by the two-thirds vote immediately closed debate and stopped all amendment of the pending questions to which it applied, so Reyes's amendment and the request to speak are out of order. The chair proceeds directly to the vote first on the amendment (if still pending) and then on the resolution, entertaining no further debate or amendments.

ref.quote: immediately closes debate on, and stops amendment of, the immediately pending question and such other pending questions as the motion may specify (in consecutive series; see Standard Characteristic 2); and 2) prevents the making of any other subsidiary motions except the higher-ranking Lay on the Table.

prompt: During a meeting of the Millbrook Arts Council, a main motion to fund a summer concert series is immediately pending with no amendments offered yet. Grace Nkemadu moves the motion to force an immediate vote; it is seconded and adopted by the required margin. Before the chair takes the vote on the concert funding, member Paul Ortiz moves to postpone the whole matter to the next meeting, and member Ruth Lang moves to refer it to the finance committee. How should the chair treat these two motions?
gold: Both motions are out of order and the chair should decline to entertain them. Once the Previous Question is ordered it prevents the making of any other subsidiary motions except the higher-ranking motion to Lay on the Table; Postpone and Commit are lower-ranking subsidiary motions and cannot be made. The chair proceeds at once to the vote on the concert funding.

ref.quote: immediately closes debate on, and stops amendment of, the immediately pending question and such other pending questions as the motion may specify (in consecutive series; see Standard Characteristic 2); and 2) prevents the making of any other subsidiary motions except the higher-ranking Lay on the Table.

prompt: The Lakeside Rowing Club is debating a bylaw change. Members obtain the floor, the motion to close debate and vote at once is moved, seconded, and carried by more than two-thirds. As the chair prepares to take the vote, Nina Castellano rises to raise a question of privilege about the room being too hot to hear, and separately Derek Hobbs rises on a point of order challenging whether a quorum is present. Are these interruptions in order after the assembly has ordered an immediate vote?
gold: Yes, both are in order. Although the Previous Question closes debate and bars lower subsidiary motions, its adoption does not prevent the making of privileged or incidental motions as applicable. Castellano's question of privilege (privileged) and Hobbs's point of order (incidental) may therefore be raised even after the order is adopted; the chair handles them, then returns to taking the vote.

ref.quote: The adoption of an order for the Previous Question does not prevent the making of privileged or incidental motions as applicable, and, strictly speaking, it does not prevent a special order set for a particular hour from interrupting the pending business .

prompt: At the Fairhaven School Board, the following are pending, in the order made: (a) a resolution to adopt a new attendance policy; (b) a primary amendment to that resolution; and (c) an immediately pending motion to refer the whole matter to a committee. Member Alan Whitfield gets the floor and says, "I move the previous question on the motion to refer and on the amendment to the resolution." Is his motion in order as stated, and what would it cover?
gold: The motion is in order. The Previous Question can be applied to a consecutive part of a series of pending questions provided it begins with the immediately pending question, which here is the motion to refer (c). Whitfield's motion covering the referral (c) and the amendment (b) is a valid consecutive series starting from the immediately pending question; it needs a second and a two-thirds vote and would order an immediate vote on (c) then (b), leaving the resolution (a) open.

ref.quote: Can be applied to any immediately pending debatable or amendable motion; to an entire series of pending debatable or amendable motions; and to any consecutive part of such a series, beginning with the immediately pending question. (Under this rule it can be applied to motions that are amendable but not debatable, for the purpose of stopping amendment; see page t47.) It supersedes any earlier order for the closing of debate at a future time and can be applied while such an order is in effect.

prompt: At the Riverside Cycling Federation, a resolution, an amendment to it, and an immediately pending motion to postpone the matter to next month are all on the floor. Carmen Foley obtains the floor and moves, "I move the previous question on the resolution and on the motion to postpone" — skipping over the amendment. The chair hesitates. Is Foley's qualified motion a proper application, and why?
gold: No, it is not a proper application and the chair should rule it out of order as stated. The Previous Question may be applied only to the immediately pending question, to the entire series, or to a consecutive part beginning with the immediately pending question. Foley's motion reaches the resolution while skipping the intervening amendment, so it is not a consecutive series beginning with the immediately pending motion to postpone. She could instead move it on the postponement alone, or on the postponement plus the amendment, or on all pending questions.

ref.quote: Can be applied to any immediately pending debatable or amendable motion; to an entire series of pending debatable or amendable motions; and to any consecutive part of such a series, beginning with the immediately pending question. (Under this rule it can be applied to motions that are amendable but not debatable, for the purpose of stopping amendment; see page t47.) It supersedes any earlier order for the closing of debate at a future time and can be applied while such an order is in effect.

prompt: The Oakwood Choral Society has three motions pending: a main resolution, an amendment to it, and an immediately pending motion to commit it to a subcommittee. Devon Pryce simply gets the floor and says, "I move the previous question," with a second following. Several members assume debate will end on everything. If the motion carries, exactly what will it bring to an immediate vote?
gold: An unqualified motion for the Previous Question applies only to the immediately pending question, which here is the motion to commit. If it is adopted by a two-thirds vote, only the motion to commit is brought to an immediate vote; if that fails, the amendment and the resolution remain open to debate and amendment as before. To reach the amendment and resolution, Pryce would have had to move the Previous Question in qualified form on those as well.

ref.quote: In practice, this motion usually is made in an unqualified form, such as “I move the previous question,” and then it applies only to the immediately pending question.

prompt: At the Glenview Toastmasters chapter, a controversial motion is under debate. Ivy Sandoval moves to end debate and vote now; it is seconded. On the rising vote, 14 members favor closing debate and 12 oppose. The presiding officer announces, "The ayes have it, debate is closed," and starts to put the main question. Was the ruling correct, and what should happen?
gold: The ruling was wrong. The Previous Question requires a two-thirds vote, and 14 to 12 is a bare majority, not two-thirds (two-thirds of 26 is at least 18). Because it failed to gain the necessary two-thirds, debate continues as if the motion had not been made; the chair must reopen debate on the main question rather than proceed to the vote.

ref.quote: Requires a two-thirds vote. (If a motion for the Previous Question fails to gain the necessary two-thirds vote, debate continues as if this motion had not been made.) In ordinary bodies, the requirement of a two-thirds vote for ordering the Previous Question is important in protecting the democratic process.

prompt: The Pinecrest Garden Club is debating a dues increase. Marcus Bell obtains the floor and says, "I move the previous question." No one seconds it, but the chair, eager to move on, states the question and calls for a vote, then also invites two members to argue about whether debate should end before that vote is taken. Identify what the chair got wrong about handling this motion.
gold: The chair made two errors. The motion for the Previous Question must be seconded, so with no second the chair should not have stated or put it. It is also not debatable, so the chair should never have allowed members to argue about whether to close debate. Properly, absent a second the motion falls; if seconded, the chair puts it to an immediate rising vote without debate, requiring two-thirds to adopt.

ref.quote: Requires a two-thirds vote. (If a motion for the Previous Question fails to gain the necessary two-thirds vote, debate continues as if this motion had not been made.) In ordinary bodies, the requirement of a two-thirds vote for ordering the Previous Question is important in protecting the democratic process.

prompt: At the Harbor City Newcomers Association, member Tara Q. is speaking in debate and has the floor. From across the room, Leon Draper stands and interjects, "I move the previous question!" while Tara is mid-sentence. The chair is unsure whether to entertain Draper's motion immediately. How should the chair handle Draper's motion?
gold: The chair should rule Draper's motion out of order at this moment. The motion for the Previous Question is out of order when another member has the floor, and Tara still holds it. Draper must wait until the floor is properly assigned to him; he then must be recognized, move it, obtain a second, and the assembly adopts it only by a two-thirds vote taken without debate.
keywords: out of order when another has the floor

ref.quote: Is out of order when another has the floor. 4.

prompt: At the Summit Trail Conservancy, debate on a land-purchase motion is dragging. Recognized by the chair, Bianca Ferro says, "I move we vote now." The chair responds, "That's not a proper motion," and simply continues debate. Bianca protests that her motion should be handled. Who is right?
gold: Bianca is right. "I move we vote now" is simply the motion for the Previous Question stated in nonstandard form, and it is subject to all the rules governing that motion. The chair must treat it as such: it requires a second, is undebatable and unamendable, and needs a two-thirds vote to close debate. The chair errs by dismissing it as improper.

ref.quote: A motion such as “I call for [or “call”] the question,” “I demand the previous question,” “I move to close [or “end”] debate,” or “I move we vote now” is simply a motion for the Previous Question made in nonstandard form, and it is subject to all of the rules in this section.

prompt: During a heated debate at the Woodland Ratepayers League, several members shout "Question! Question!" from their seats without being recognized. The chair, taking the shouts as the will of the assembly, declares debate closed and begins the vote on the main motion — even though Harold Pryce is on his feet signaling he still wishes to speak and has not yet spoken. Was the chair entitled to close debate this way?
gold: No. Shouts of "Question!" from members' seats do not close debate. Regardless of the wording of any motion or call to close debate, it always requires a second and a two-thirds vote, taken separately from and before the vote on the main motion, to shut off debate against the will of even one member who wishes to speak and has not exhausted his right. Harold has not spoken, so the chair must reopen debate and recognize him.

ref.quote: But regardless of the wording of a motion or "call" seeking to close debate, it always requires a second and a two-thirds vote, taken separately from and before the vote(s) on the motion(s) to which it is applied, to shut off debate against the will of even one member who wishes to speak and has not exhausted his right to debate .

prompt: At the Ashford Debate Guild, member Sofia Grant is recognized and states, "I call for the question." Another member seconds. The chair, believing a single member's call automatically forces a vote, announces that debate is over and proceeds without any vote on Sofia's call. Explain whether one member calling the question can cut off debate this way.
gold: No. "I call for the question" is merely the Previous Question in nonstandard form and is subject to all the same rules. It is a demand that the assembly decide, not a command by one member; a single member cannot cut off debate. The chair must put Sofia's call to the assembly, and only a two-thirds vote (after a second, without debate) closes debate. Absent that vote, debate continues.
keywords: single member cannot cut off debate

ref.quote: A motion such as “I call for [or “call”] the question,” “I demand the previous question,” “I move to close [or “end”] debate,” or “I move we vote now” is simply a motion for the Previous Question made in nonstandard form, and it is subject to all of the rules in this section.

prompt: At the Brightwater Booster Club, the Previous Question was ordered on both a pending amendment and the main motion. The assembly voted on and adopted the amendment, but before voting on the main motion, Renee Alcott moves to reconsider the vote that ordered the Previous Question, arguing debate should reopen. Is her motion to reconsider available at this point?
gold: Yes, but only in this narrow window. An affirmative vote ordering the Previous Question can be reconsidered before any vote has been taken under the order, but not after it has been partly executed. Here the order covered two motions and only the amendment has been voted on, so the order is partly executed and it is now too late to reconsider ordering the Previous Question; the chair must proceed to the vote on the main motion.

ref.quote: When the Previous Question has been ordered on a number of motions, the order is said to be partly executed (or partly carried out) if one or more, but not all, of these motions have been voted on. When all of the motions specified in the order have been voted on, it is fully executed. > 16.

prompt: The Kestrel Historical Society ordered the Previous Question on a single main motion. Right after the chair announces that the Previous Question is ordered but before he puts the main motion to a vote, Owen Blackwood rises and moves to reconsider the vote that ordered the Previous Question. Timing aside, is there any moment in which such a reconsideration is possible here?
gold: Yes. Because the order applies to only one motion, it becomes fully executed the instant that motion is voted on, and it is not partly executed before then. So the sole moment to move to reconsider the ordering of the Previous Question is in the brief interval after the vote ordering it and before the vote is taken on the main motion. Owen has moved in exactly that window, so his motion to reconsider is in order.
keywords: before any vote under the order

ref.quote: When the Previous Question has been ordered on a number of motions, the order is said to be partly executed (or partly carried out) if one or more, but not all, of these motions have been voted on. When all of the motions specified in the order have been voted on, it is fully executed. > 16.

prompt: At the Verde Valley Water District board, the Previous Question was ordered on a series: a primary amendment and the main resolution. Both have now been voted on and disposed of. Later in the same meeting, member Priya Anand moves to reconsider the vote that ordered the Previous Question on that series. Should the chair entertain her motion?
gold: No. The order was ordered on two motions and both have now been voted on, so the order is fully executed. A vote ordering the Previous Question cannot be reconsidered once the order has been executed; it may be reconsidered only before any motion under the order has been voted on. The chair should rule Priya's motion to reconsider out of order.

ref.quote: When the Previous Question has been ordered on a number of motions, the order is said to be partly executed (or partly carried out) if one or more, but not all, of these motions have been voted on. When all of the motions specified in the order have been voted on, it is fully executed. > 16.

prompt: At the Coastline Yacht Club, the Previous Question has been ordered on the pending main motion but the motion has not yet been voted on. As the chair proceeds, member Gwen Hollis takes an appeal from a ruling of the chair. Normally an appeal on such a point could be debated. May the assembly debate this appeal right now?
gold: No. An appeal is undebatable if it is made after the Previous Question has been moved or ordered and before the order is exhausted. Since the order for the Previous Question is still unexhausted, Gwen's appeal cannot be debated; the chair puts it to an immediate vote without debate, then continues executing the order.

ref.quote: An appeal is undebatable if it is made after the Previous Question has been moved or ordered and before the order is exhausted.

prompt: At the Elmridge Alumni Association, the Previous Question has been ordered on a main motion that is still pending and undecided. Before the vote is taken, member Jonah Reeves makes a motion to Reconsider a related vote, which becomes pending. He argues the reconsideration should be fully debatable. Is he correct while the order for the Previous Question is still in effect?
gold: No. While a motion on which the Previous Question has been ordered remains pending, the order also applies to other motions that take precedence over it. So a motion to Reconsider that becomes pending during the unexhausted order is undebatable, and the question to be reconsidered cannot be debated or amended. The chair treats it accordingly and takes the vote without debate.

ref.quote: While one or more motions on which the Previous Question has been ordered remain pending, the order also applies to any other motions that may take precedence over these pending questions. (The rules stated in the two preceding paragraphs-relating to reconsiderations and appeals-are applications of this principle.) - But if a question of privilege is raised and is admitted for immediate consideration , or if a special order set for a particular hour intervenes, these questions are independent of an unexhausted order for the Previous Question applying to business that they interrupt.

prompt: At the Northgate Community Chorus, the Previous Question has been ordered on a pending resolution that has not yet been voted on. Before the chair takes the vote, member Alicia Vaughn rises and raises a question of privilege about a serious disruption in the hall, and the chair admits it for immediate consideration. Alicia asks whether her question of privilege is swept up by the still-pending order for the Previous Question. How should the chair respond?
gold: The chair should explain that her question of privilege is not affected. When a question of privilege is raised and admitted for immediate consideration, or a special order for a particular hour intervenes, those matters are independent of an unexhausted order for the Previous Question applying to the business they interrupt. So the question of privilege is handled under its own rules, and the order for the Previous Question resumes on the resolution afterward.

ref.quote: But if a question of privilege is raised and is admitted for immediate consideration (see [19](#sec-19)), or if a special order set for a particular hour intervenes, these questions are independent of an unexhausted order for the Previous Question applying to business that they interrupt.

prompt: At a meeting of the Cedar Falls Historical Society, president Marta Delgado sees that the projector has frozen mid-report. She says, "Let's just pause a moment while tech sorts this out" and, hearing no objection, waits about ninety seconds while everyone stays seated. She takes no vote and declares nothing formally. A member later insists that because members quieted down and waited, the assembly was in recess and the meeting's continuity was broken. How do you rule on what actually occurred and what the chair did?
gold: The chair did not put the assembly in recess; she permitted it to stand at ease, which she may do without objection and without any motion or vote. There is technically no interruption of the meeting when the assembly stands at ease, members remain in their places, and the pause ends the moment the chair declares business in order or any member objects. The member is incorrect: the meeting's continuity was never broken.

ref.quote: A meeting is said to stand at ease if the chair, without objection, simply permits a brief pause, without a declaration of recess. In such a case there is technically no interruption of the meeting, and members remain in their places.

prompt: During a Riverside Tenants Association meeting, chair Kwame Osei lets the assembly stand at ease so two members can quietly confer at their seats. Partway through the pause, member Lena Fischer says loudly, "I object to standing at ease any longer." Osei is unsure whether he needs a majority vote to bring the meeting back to order, or whether a single member can end the pause. Advise the chair.
gold: No vote is required. Because the assembly is merely standing at ease rather than in a declared recess, any single member's objection ends the pause immediately, and the chair simply declares the meeting again in order. Standing at ease is permitted only so long as there is no objection, so Fischer's objection is decisive on its own.

ref.quote: A meeting is said to stand at ease if the chair, without objection, simply permits a brief pause, without a declaration of recess. In such a case there is technically no interruption of the meeting, and members remain in their places.

prompt: Chair Priya Nair of the Lakeside Camera Club wants a very short breather so a late presenter can plug in her laptop, and she does not want the formality or the interruption of a declared recess. A board member tells her the only proper way to pause is to entertain a motion to recess, have it seconded, and take a vote. Is there a lighter-weight option available to the chair, and what distinguishes it from a recess?
gold: Yes. The chair may simply let the meeting stand at ease: without objection she permits a brief pause with no declaration of recess, no motion, second, or vote. Unlike a recess, standing at ease technically does not interrupt the meeting, members remain in their places, and it ends the instant the chair declares business in order or any member objects. The board member is wrong that a formal motion to recess is the only option.

ref.quote: A meeting is said to stand at ease if the chair, without objection, simply permits a brief pause, without a declaration of recess. In such a case there is technically no interruption of the meeting, and members remain in their places.

conceptName: Meaning of recess; recess vs adjourn
prompt: The Oakmont Garden Club takes a ten-minute recess in the middle of debate on a motion to purchase new greenhouse shelving so members can inspect a sample. When they return, president Hugo Bennett asks the secretary whether they must reopen the meeting with the usual opening ceremonies and re-read the pending motion from scratch. What should you tell him about how business resumes after a recess?
gold: A recess is only a short intermission that does not close the meeting, so there are no opening proceedings afterward. Business is resumed immediately at exactly the point where it was interrupted, meaning debate simply continues on the pending shelving motion with no re-reading and no fresh opening ceremonies. The chair should just call the meeting back to order and return to the pending question.

ref.quote: A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.

conceptName: Meaning of recess; recess vs adjourn
prompt: At a three-day convention of the Statewide Beekeepers Federation, the chair announces "we will recess until tomorrow morning." The next morning the parliamentarian, Dolores Chen, notes that the break sent delegates home overnight and required reassembling the body. A delegate argues it does not matter what you call it, so the assembly should skip minutes and opening ceremonies and just resume. Was this truly a recess, and what follows the next morning?
gold: Despite the label, an overnight break that terminates the meeting and disperses the delegates is properly an adjournment, not a recess. At the conclusion of a genuine recess there are never any opening proceedings and business resumes exactly where it left off; but the start of a new meeting the next day may include opening ceremonies and the reading of minutes. Because this was in substance an adjournment, treating it as a mere recess and skipping those steps is not automatically correct.

ref.quote: at the conclusion of a recess there never are any “opening” proceedings, but business is always immediately resumed where it was left off, just as if there had been no recess.

conceptName: Meaning of recess; recess vs adjourn
prompt: The Fairview Co-op Board is midway through a contested election when the tellers need time to tally paper ballots. Director Aisha Rahman proposes that the board close the meeting entirely and reconvene later that evening once the count is done. Another director thinks a brief break to count would be more appropriate. Which device fits this situation, and what effect does it have on the meeting?
gold: A recess is the fitting device: it is a short intermission, commonly of only a few minutes, and counting ballots is a classic purpose for it. A recess does not close the meeting; when the tellers finish, business resumes immediately at exactly the point where it was interrupted. Closing the meeting entirely (an adjournment) is unnecessary and heavier-handed than the situation requires.

ref.quote: A recess may be taken, for example, to count ballots, to secure information, or to allow for informal consultation.

conceptName: Main vs privileged motion to Recess
prompt: Debate is underway on a pending motion to hire a new auditor at the Brightwater Condo Association. Member Tomas Vidal rises and says, "I move that we take a recess for fifteen minutes right now." Chair Ellen Park is unsure whether this motion may even be entertained while the auditor question is still on the floor, whether it is debatable, and what vote it needs. Rule on the motion.
gold: The motion is in order. Because a question is pending and the recess is to begin immediately, this is the privileged motion to Recess. It requires a second, is not debatable, is amendable as to the length of the recess, and needs only a majority vote. If adopted, the chair declares the recess at once and debate on the auditor motion resumes afterward where it left off.

ref.quote: The privileged motion to Recess (or to Take a Recess) is a motion that a recess begin immediately, made while another question is pending.

conceptName: Main vs privileged motion to Recess
prompt: At the Northgate Rotary, no motion is currently pending. Member Grace Liu moves "that we recess for ten minutes so the caterers can reset the room." Someone objects that since nothing is on the floor, this cannot be the privileged motion to recess and therefore is out of order entirely. Chair Sam Whitfield asks how to classify and handle the motion. Advise him.
gold: The motion is in order, but it is not the privileged motion to Recess; because no question is pending it is an incidental main motion to recess. As a main motion it is debatable and fully amendable, requires a second, and is adopted by majority vote. The objector is wrong that it is out of order simply because nothing else is pending.

ref.quote: a motion to recess is privileged only when another question is pending; and a motion to take a recess at a future time is in order only when no question is pending.

conceptName: Main vs privileged motion to Recess
prompt: During debate on a pending bylaws amendment at the Harborview Yacht Club, commodore Rex Alton wants to schedule a recess to begin two hours later, at the lunch hour. He moves "that the club recess at noon." The parliamentarian frowns. Is a motion to recess at a future time in order at this moment, and if not, when would it be proper?
gold: No. A motion to take a recess at a future time is in order only when no question is pending, so it cannot be moved now while the bylaws amendment is pending. The privileged motion to Recess must call for the recess to begin immediately. To schedule the noon recess, the commodore must wait until no question is pending and move it as a main motion, which would be debatable and amendable by majority vote.

ref.quote: A motion to recess that is made when no question is pending (whether the recess is to begin immediately or at a future time) is a main motion, and the eight characteristics given below do not apply to it.

prompt: A motion to recess for fifteen minutes is pending (a question was on the floor when it was moved) at the Millbrook School Council. Member Farah Idris moves to amend it to make the recess thirty minutes, and then a member tries to debate the merits of the longer break. Chair Owen Blake asks whether the amendment is allowed and whether members may debate it. Rule.
gold: The amendment is proper: the privileged motion to Recess is amendable as to the length of the recess. However, any such amendment is itself undebatable, so the chair should not permit debate on it. The amendment is decided by majority vote, after which the recess motion as amended is put to a vote.

ref.quote: Is amendable as to the length of the recess; any such amendment is undebatable. 7.

prompt: Delegate Nils Aaberg of the Prairie Wind Chorale has the floor and is speaking on a pending motion. From across the room, member Cora Yee calls out, "I move we recess for ten minutes," without waiting to be recognized and with no one seconding her. Chair Bianca Rossi wonders whether she must process this recess motion. What is your ruling?
gold: The chair should not entertain it. The privileged motion to Recess is out of order when another member has the floor, and Aaberg currently holds the floor, so Yee's interruption is improper. Additionally, the motion must be seconded before it can be considered. The chair rules the attempted motion out of order and lets Aaberg continue.
keywords: out of order when another has the floor

ref.quote: Is out of order when another has the floor. 4. Must be seconded. 5. Is not debatable. (But see 43:31-32 regarding allowable explanation of an undebatable motion.) 6.

prompt: With a main motion pending at the Glenview Quilters Guild, a properly seconded motion to recess for twenty minutes is on the floor. Member Ruth Ellison rises and begins arguing at length about why the guild really needs the break to reorganize its fabric tables. Chair Devon Marsh is unsure whether to let her continue and what vote the recess needs. Advise the chair.
gold: The privileged motion to Recess is not debatable, so the chair should stop the extended argument, though a very brief explanation of the motion may be tolerated. The motion requires only a majority vote and cannot be reconsidered. The chair should promptly put the recess motion to a vote rather than allow debate on its merits.

ref.quote: Is not debatable. (But see 43:31-32 regarding allowable explanation of an undebatable motion.) 6.

conceptName: Declaring and postponing a scheduled recess
prompt: The adopted program for the Southshore Educators' Convention schedules a recess at 10:30 a.m. When the clock reaches 10:30, chair Mona Reyes is midway through routine announcements and wonders whether she must first entertain and pass a motion to recess before the assembly can take the scheduled break. Must she, or may she simply proceed? Explain.
gold: No motion or vote is needed. Because the recess is provided for in the adopted program, the chair, without further action by the assembly, simply announces the fact and declares the assembly in recess when the scheduled time arrives. She should conclude the immediate item and then declare the recess; if she failed to do so, a member could call for the orders of the day to demand it.
keywords: call for orders of the day

ref.quote: If a recess is provided for in the adopted agenda or program of a convention or other meeting, the chair, without further action by the assembly, announces the fact and simply declares the assembly in recess when the specified time arrives.

conceptName: Declaring and postponing a scheduled recess
prompt: At the Valley Artisans Convention, the adopted agenda calls for a recess at 3:00 p.m., but at 3:10 chair Leon Pratt is still letting a speaker run long and has said nothing about the break. Member Hana Kim wants the scheduled recess enforced but is not sure she is allowed to interrupt to insist on it. What can she do, and what must the chair then do?
gold: Because the recess was provided for in the adopted agenda and its time has passed unannounced, any member may call for the orders of the day, which demands that the scheduled recess be declared. On that call, the chair must proceed to declare the assembly in recess as scheduled unless the assembly sets the orders aside. Kim's call does not require a second or a vote to compel the chair to take up the order.
keywords: call for the orders of the day

ref.quote: If the chair does not announce the recess at the scheduled time, a member can call for the orders of the day ([18](#sec-18)), thereby demanding that the recess be declared.

conceptName: Declaring and postponing a scheduled recess
prompt: The adopted program of the Delta Historical Congress sets a recess for 2:00 p.m., but as that time arrives the assembly is deep in a productive debate and most members would rather keep working. Chair Ida Nkemelu asks how the body can put off the scheduled recess and what level of support that requires. Advise her.
gold: The scheduled recess is an order of the day, so it can be postponed by a two-thirds vote if, when the time arrives, the assembly does not wish to recess. The chair may put the question of postponing the recess (or a member may move to extend/continue rather than recess), and a two-thirds vote is required to set aside the scheduled order. Absent that two-thirds vote, the recess must be declared at the appointed time.

ref.quote: The time for taking a scheduled recess can be postponed by a twothirds vote if, when that time arrives, the assembly does not wish to recess.

prompt: At a regular monthly meeting of the Cedar Hollow Garden Club, President Lorna Whitfield has a resolution on native plantings still under discussion when member Doug Pak is recognized and says, "I move that we adjourn." The motion is seconded and carries. Doug turns to the treasurer and says the club can finish the plantings resolution "in about ten minutes, once we tidy up." What is the effect of the adopted motion, and is Doug right that the meeting continues?
gold: Doug is wrong. The unqualified motion to Adjourn, once adopted, closes the meeting immediately; adjournment does not merely pause the meeting for cleanup. Because the motion named no later time, it is the privileged motion to Adjourn (majority vote, second required, not debatable, not amendable, takes effect at once). The pending plantings resolution simply drops and is resumed at the next regular meeting.

ref.quote: The adoption of any motion to adjourn closes the meeting immediately unless the motion specifies a later time for adjourning (but if it does specify such a time it is not a privileged motion).

prompt: The Riverside Camera Society meets monthly under bylaws that schedule several regular meetings each year and set no fixed hour to end. During debate on a dues increase, member Tanya Bloor rises and moves "to adjourn," without naming any time. A newer member objects that "you can't just adjourn in the middle of business — that has to wait until we finish." Rule on whether the chair should entertain Tanya's motion, and describe its handling.
gold: The chair should entertain the motion. In an ordinary society with regular meetings and no set adjournment hour, an unqualified motion to adjourn is the privileged motion to Adjourn and is in order even while business is pending. It requires a second, is not debatable and not amendable, needs only a majority vote, and if adopted closes the meeting immediately; the dues question would carry over to the next meeting.
keywords: in order while business is pending

ref.quote: The motion to adjourn that commonly occurs in meetings of ordinary societies is the privileged motion.

prompt: At the closing session of the one-time Delta Founders Convention, delegate Marcus Iyer moves "that we adjourn," and a colleague immediately moves "that we adjourn to reconvene next spring." A delegate insists both are the same privileged motion and neither is debatable. The convention has made no provision to meet again. How should the chair classify and handle each motion?
gold: Neither is the privileged motion to Adjourn here. Because adjourning this final convention meeting would dissolve the assembly with no provision for another meeting, Marcus's motion is an incidental main motion (adjourn sine die), and the qualified motion naming a future time is likewise a main motion. Both are therefore debatable and amendable, need a second, and are adopted by majority vote; whether adjourn means privileged or main depends on these conditions.

ref.quote: A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.

conceptName: Adjourn privileged with no question pending
prompt: The Fairview Historical Association has finished all business at its regular meeting; nothing is on the floor. Member Gail Ostrander moves "to adjourn." Another member says, "Since no motion is pending, this is just an ordinary main motion — so it's debatable, and I'd like to argue we should stay longer." The society meets regularly and has set no hour for ending. How should the chair treat Gail's motion?
gold: The chair should treat it as the privileged motion to Adjourn. Because there is provision for future regular meetings and no adjournment time has been set, an unqualified motion to adjourn keeps its privileged status even with no question pending; it is not debatable or amendable. It needs a second and a majority vote, and on adoption closes the meeting immediately, so the objector may not debate it.
keywords: even when no question is pending

ref.quote: Under the conditions just described, a motion to Adjourn is therefore said to be “privileged” or to be “a privileged motion” even when no question is pending.

conceptName: Adjourn privileged with no question pending
prompt: At the Northgate Homeowners Association, with the floor clear between agenda items, member Priya Anand moves "to adjourn." The secretary wants to add "and that we thank the caterers" to the motion, and another member asks to amend it to "adjourn in fifteen minutes." The association holds regular meetings and has fixed no closing hour. May the members amend Priya's motion?
gold: No. Even though no question was pending, Priya's unqualified motion to adjourn is the privileged motion to Adjourn and is neither debatable nor amendable, so neither proposed amendment is in order. The motion needs a second and a majority vote and takes effect immediately on adoption. (Adding a specified time would instead have to be moved as a separate main motion.)

ref.quote: even if it is made while no question is pending, it is not debatable or amendable and it remains subject to all of the rules governing the privileged motion to Adjourn (except those that relate to making the motion while business is pending; see Standard Descriptive Characteristics).

conceptName: Adjourn privileged with no question pending
