prompt: The Lakeshore Rotary chapter has completed its agenda, and the floor is clear. Member Ken Duarte moves "to adjourn," it is seconded, and a member calls out that he wants five minutes to debate whether members would rather keep socializing in session. The chapter meets weekly with no set adjournment hour. The chair asks you whether debate should be allowed before the vote. Advise the chair.
gold: No debate is allowed. The privileged motion to Adjourn is undebatable even with no business pending, precisely so a decision to close does not consume the assembly's time and a majority is not forced to remain in session longer than it wishes. The chair should put it to an immediate voice vote; a majority carries it, and on adoption the meeting closes at once.

ref.quote: a majority should not be forced to continue in session substantially longer than it desires, and even if no business is pending, a decision as to whether to close the meeting should not be allowed to consume time.

conceptName: Main vs privileged motion to Adjourn
prompt: During debate on a budget at the Pinecrest Booster Club, member Alice Reynoso is recognized and moves "that we adjourn to meet at 7 p.m. next Thursday." The chair is unsure whether to treat this like the usual quick, undebatable adjournment motion. The club meets regularly and has no fixed closing hour. How should the chair classify and handle Alice's motion while the budget is pending?
gold: Because Alice's motion is qualified by naming a future time, it is not the privileged motion to Adjourn but an incidental main motion. A main motion to adjourn to a future time is out of order while business is pending, so the chair should rule it out of order until the budget is disposed of. Offered when nothing is pending, it would be debatable, amendable, need a second, and take a majority vote.
keywords: out of order while business is pending

ref.quote: A motion to adjourn is always a privileged motion except in the following cases: 1) When the motion is qualified in any way, as in the case of a motion to adjourn at, or to, a future time. 2) When a time for adjourning is already established, either because the assembly has adopted a motion or a program setting such a time, or because the order of business, the bylaws, or other governing rules prescribe it. 3) When the effect of the motion to adjourn, if adopted, would be to dissolve the assembly with no provision for another meeting, as is usually the case in a mass meeting or the last meeting of a convention.

conceptName: Main vs privileged motion to Adjourn
prompt: The Harborview Society long ago adopted a standing rule fixing that every meeting ends at 9:00 p.m. At 8:20, while a motion is pending, member Sy Feldman moves "to adjourn" now, unqualified. Another member says any motion to adjourn is always privileged and must be voted on without debate. Given the fixed hour, how should the chair rule while business is pending?
gold: With a closing hour already established, a motion to adjourn is not privileged and is treated as an incidental main motion, and such a motion is out of order while business is pending. The chair should rule Sy's motion out of order until the pending question is disposed of. If made when nothing is pending, it would be a debatable, amendable main motion requiring a second and a majority vote.
keywords: out of order while business is pending

ref.quote: Under any of conditions (1) through (3) above, a motion to adjourn is not privileged and is treated just as any other incidental main motion.

conceptName: Main vs privileged motion to Adjourn
prompt: At the Ashford Debate League's regular meeting, a resolution is under discussion when member Nora Klein moves "that the meeting adjourn to reconvene tomorrow at noon." She argues that because adjournment is a privileged motion, it may interrupt the pending resolution. The league meets regularly with no fixed adjournment time. Is Nora correct that her motion may interrupt the pending business?
gold: No. A motion to adjourn to a future time is qualified, making it an incidental main motion rather than the privileged motion to Adjourn, and such a motion is always out of order while business is pending. The chair should rule it out of order until the resolution is disposed of. Only an unqualified motion "to adjourn" would be privileged and able to interrupt.
keywords: out of order while business is pending

ref.quote: a motion to adjourn at or to a future time is always out of order while business is pending in any assembly; and any motion to adjourn at all is out of order while business is pending under either of conditions or -which, however, do not commonly apply to meetings of ordinary societies.

prompt: At the Summit Trail Club meeting, member Owen Basto has been assigned the floor and is speaking on a trail-maintenance report. From his seat, member Rhea Colter blurts out, "I move to adjourn!" No one seconds it, and the chair asks you whether the motion is properly before the assembly. The club meets regularly with no set adjournment hour. Advise the chair.
gold: The motion is not properly before the assembly on two counts. The privileged motion to Adjourn is out of order when another member has the floor, and Rhea interrupted Owen, so the chair should not entertain it. It also must be seconded, and none was given. The chair should let Owen continue; Rhea may move to adjourn later after obtaining the floor, whereupon a second and a majority vote would be needed.
keywords: out of order when another has the floor

ref.quote: Is out of order when another has the floor. 4. Must be seconded. 5.

prompt: The Glenwood Choral Society is between agenda items when member Dev Ramachandran moves "to adjourn," and it is seconded. Another member moves to amend it to "adjourn after we schedule the spring concert," and a third insists the adjournment needs a two-thirds vote because it cuts off the meeting. The society meets regularly with no fixed closing time. Rule on the amendment and the vote required.
gold: The privileged motion to Adjourn is not amendable, so the proposed amendment is out of order. It requires only a majority vote, not two-thirds. The chair should put the unamended motion to an immediate vote; if a majority favors it, the meeting closes at once. Any wish to set a concert time must be handled by a separate motion.

ref.quote: Is not amendable. 7. Requires a majority vote. 8.

prompt: At the Brookline Arts Council, a grant proposal is pending when member Selina Vaught obtains the floor, moves "to adjourn," and it is seconded. Before the chair can put the question, member Paul Noh says, "Point of information — I'd like to speak against adjourning; we're so close to finishing the grant." The council meets regularly with no fixed closing hour. May members debate the motion to adjourn before it is voted on?
gold: No. The privileged motion to Adjourn is not debatable, so Paul may not argue against it before the vote. The chair should put the seconded motion to an immediate vote, where a majority decides it; if adopted it closes the meeting at once. If it fails, the grant proposal is restated and debate on that resumes.

ref.quote: Must be seconded. 5. Is not debatable . 6.

conceptName: What is in order before a directed adjournment
prompt: At the Maple Ridge Association, the motion to adjourn has just carried on a voice vote. Before the chair, President Hal Muntz, has said anything further, member Cara Lindqvist rises to make an urgent safety announcement about a flooded parking lot. Hal is unsure whether it is "too late" because the vote already passed. May Cara's announcement still be made, and when is the meeting actually closed?
gold: Cara's announcement is still in order. Even though the vote to adjourn carried, the meeting is not closed until the chair declares it "adjourned" (or "stands adjourned"), and making important announcements is among the steps in order after the vote but before that declaration. Hal should recognize Cara for the announcement and only then declare the meeting adjourned.
keywords: not closed until the chair has declared

ref.quote: the meeting is not closed until the chair has declared that the meeting “is adjourned” (or “stands adjourned”), and members should not leave their seats until this declaration has been made.

conceptName: What is in order before a directed adjournment
prompt: During the Westbrook Guild meeting, member Tomas Errera moves "to adjourn" and it is seconded, but the chair recalls that a required previous notice of a bylaw amendment still needs to be given tonight, and a member also wants to give that notice. The chair worries this must wait until the next meeting. What may properly happen before the vote on the motion to adjourn is taken?
gold: These matters may be handled before the vote on adjourning. While the motion to Adjourn is pending, the chair may state the facts briefly, or a member may rise and give the required previous notice, before the adjournment vote is taken. Only after such necessary steps are handled should the chair put the undebatable motion to Adjourn to a majority vote.
keywords: while the motion to adjourn is pending

ref.quote: If any matters of the types listed above arise after it has been moved to adjourn, the chair should state the facts briefly, or a member who rises and addresses the chair for the purpose may do so—or make the necessary motion or give the desired notice—before the vote is taken on the motion to Adjourn.

conceptName: What is in order before a directed adjournment
prompt: At the Oakmont Service League, member Grace Uhl moves "to adjourn," seconded, but then the treasurer realizes a time-sensitive bill must be authorized for payment tonight or a late penalty will hit. The chair asks how the league can act on the bill without simply ignoring the pending adjournment motion. What is the proper course before adjournment?
gold: Because action is required before adjournment, Grace, who moved to adjourn, can be requested to withdraw her motion so the assembly can authorize the payment. If she withdraws it, the bill can be taken up and decided; a fresh motion to adjourn may then be made. Alternatively, the necessary facts could be stated before the vote, but withdrawal is the clean way to allow substantive action.

ref.quote: If something requires action before adjournment, the member who moved to adjourn can be requested to withdraw his motion.

conceptName: Declaring or setting aside scheduled adjournment
prompt: The Statewide Educators Convention adopted a program setting each day's business session to end at 5:00 p.m. As 5:00 arrives on the second day, the presiding officer, Chair Delia Ford, waits for someone to move to adjourn, and no one does. A delegate insists the session cannot end without a formal motion and vote. Is a motion to adjourn required, and what should Chair Ford do?
gold: No motion is required. Because the program fixed 5:00 p.m. as the hour for adjourning, when that hour arrives the chair simply announces the fact and declares the meeting adjourned, without any motion or vote. Chair Ford should so declare. (If the assembly wished to continue past 5:00, that would be handled as setting aside the orders of the day; to adjourn earlier would take a main motion by majority vote.)
keywords: no motion to adjourn is necessary

ref.quote: If an hour for adjourning a meeting within a convention or other session of more than one meeting has been scheduled—either in an agenda or program or by the adoption of a motion setting a time—no motion to adjourn is necessary when that hour arrives.

conceptName: Declaring or setting aside scheduled adjournment
prompt: At the regular meeting of the Elmwood Neighborhood Society, an ordinary local society that runs a full order of business each meeting, the agenda appears complete and the room has gone quiet. President Ivan Reese does not want to just sit waiting, but a member says the meeting can only end if someone formally moves to adjourn. Must the chair wait for a motion, and how may President Reese properly close the meeting?
gold: The chair need not wait for a motion. In an ordinary local society that completes a full order of business, when it appears there is no further business the chair may ask, "Is there any further business?" and, hearing no response, declare, "Since there is no further business, the meeting is adjourned." President Reese may close the meeting this way without any motion or vote.

ref.quote: If there is no response, the chair can then say, “Since there is no further business, the meeting is adjourned.”

conceptName: Declaring or setting aside scheduled adjournment
prompt: Midway through the Harbor District Council meeting, a fire alarm sounds and smoke is visible at the rear of the hall. Chair Bianca Voss knows the normal path to end a meeting is a seconded motion to adjourn decided by majority vote, and she hesitates to skip that. Given the emergency, what is she permitted to do, and how should she set up the next meeting?
gold: In a fire or other extreme emergency, if the chair believes taking time for a vote would endanger those present, she may declare the meeting adjourned on her own, without any motion or vote. Chair Voss should declare it adjourned to a suitable time and place for an adjourned meeting if she can, or to meet at the call of the chair. Ordinary rules requiring a seconded, majority-vote motion are set aside here.

ref.quote: In the event of fire, riot, or other extreme emergency, if the chair believes taking time for a vote on adjourning would be dangerous to those present, he should declare the meeting adjourned—to a suitable time and place for an adjourned meeting (if he is able), or to meet at the call of the chair.

prompt: At a meeting of the Lakeside Homeowners Association, member Dana rises and says, "Point of order! The pending amendment isn't germane." President Alvarez immediately announces, "The chair rules that the point is not well taken; the amendment is germane." Another member protests that the assembly should have been allowed to vote on Dana's point before the chair decided it. Was the president's handling proper?
gold: The president acted properly. A Point of Order is normally ruled upon by the chair alone, and no vote of the assembly is taken unless the chair is in doubt or the ruling is appealed. The point required no second and was not put to a vote; a member who disagrees may appeal the decision.

ref.quote: Is normally ruled upon by the chair. No vote is taken unless the chair is in doubt or his ruling is appealed. 8.

prompt: During the treasurer's report at the Rotary Club of Fairview, member Kwan is mid-sentence when member Ruiz stands without being recognized and calls out, "Point of order!" The treasurer objects that Ruiz interrupted him and had not obtained the floor. Chair Bello asks you whether Ruiz was out of order in interrupting. What do you advise?
gold: Ruiz was in order. A Point of Order is in order even when another member has the floor, and may interrupt a person who is speaking or reading a report when the point genuinely requires attention at that time. It requires no recognition and no second, so the chair should hear the point at once.
keywords: in order when another has the floor

ref.quote: Is in order when another has the floor, even interrupting a person speaking or reading a report if the point genuinely requires attention at such a time . 4.

prompt: At the Delta Choral Society, member Ito raises a point of order and, after stating it, several members begin arguing back and forth about whether it is well taken. Chair Nguyen wonders whether she must open the floor to full debate on the point before ruling. How should she proceed?
gold: A Point of Order is not debatable, so the chair should not open it to general debate. With the chair's consent she may permit the member to explain his point and may hear knowledgeable or interested members by way of explanation, but this is not debate. She then rules on the point without a vote unless she is in doubt.

ref.quote: Is not debatable—but, with the chair’s consent, a member may be permitted to explain his point and knowledgeable or interested members can be heard by way of explanation.

conceptName: Purpose and handling of a Point of Order
prompt: A newer member of the Green Valley Garden Club, Priya, notices that the chair let a main motion be stated without a second, but she hesitates, assuming only officers may raise such matters. She asks whether an ordinary member is even allowed to insist that the rules be followed. How would you answer, and what is the mechanism she would use?
gold: Any member has the right to insist on enforcement of the rules; it is the right of every member who notices a breach to demand it. The mechanism is a Point of Order, which requires no second and is not debatable, calling on the chair for a ruling. She may raise it even though she is not an officer.

ref.quote: It is the right of every member who notices a breach of the rules to insist on their enforcement.

conceptName: Purpose and handling of a Point of Order
prompt: After member Ford raises a point of order at the Harborview Yacht Club, President Shaw simply says, "Overruled," and moves on without any explanation. The secretary asks what, if anything, she should enter in the minutes about the ruling. What was deficient in the chair's handling, and what belongs in the minutes?
gold: The chair should rule that the point of order is either "well taken" or "not well taken" and briefly state his reasons. Those reasons are recorded in the minutes, so a bare "Overruled" is deficient. The secretary should record both the ruling and the chair's stated reasons.

ref.quote: The chair then rules whether “the point of order is well taken” or “is not well taken,” stating briefly his reasons, which are recorded in the minutes.

conceptName: Purpose and handling of a Point of Order
prompt: At the Midtown Book Club, member Osei keeps rising on tiny technical slips—an out-of-order word choice by the chair, a slightly informal way of stating a motion—though no one's rights are affected and business is flowing smoothly. The chair asks you whether such points must always be entertained. Advise the chair.
gold: While any member may raise a Point of Order for a genuine breach, in ordinary meetings it is undesirable to raise points of order on minor irregularities of a purely technical character when no one's rights are infringed and no real harm is done to the transaction of business. The chair may note this and, if the conduct becomes obstructive or dilatory, decline to entertain such points.

ref.quote: In ordinary meetings it is undesirable to raise points of order on minor irregularities of a purely technical character, if it is clear that no one’s rights are being infringed upon and no real harm is being done to the proper transaction of business.

prompt: At the Ashford Alumni Association, a motion was stated by the chair even though it had never been seconded. Debate proceeded for several minutes, and only then did member Cole rise to say, "Point of order—that motion was never seconded." Chair Byrne asks you whether Cole's point is good. What is your ruling?
gold: Cole's point is too late. The general rule is that a question of order must be raised promptly at the time the breach occurs; the time to object to a missing second was when the chair stated the motion. Because debate had already begun, the lack-of-second point of order is not timely and the chair should rule it not well taken.

ref.quote: The general rule is that if a question of order is to be raised, it must be raised promptly at the time the breach occurs.

prompt: At the Northgate Civic League, the chair mistakenly stated the question on a motion that was not in order in the existing parliamentary situation. Two speakers have already debated it. Member Larkin now says the motion should never have been admitted and wants it thrown out on a point of order. Is a point of order still available on that ground?
gold: No. Once debate on the motion has begun, a point of order is too late—no matter how clearly the chair erred in stating the question. The objection had to be raised promptly when the chair stated the motion, so the point is now untimely and should be ruled not well taken.

ref.quote: After debate on such a motion has begun—no matter how clear it is that the chair should not have stated the question on the motion—a point of order is too late.

prompt: At the Summit Ridge Condo Association, the chair announced the result of a voice vote as carried. Business continued to the next item, and about ten minutes later member Pace wants to raise a point of order that the vote was conducted improperly. Chair Ellison asks whether this is timely. What do you advise?
gold: It is too late. A point of order regarding the conduct of a vote must be raised immediately following the chair's announcement of the voting result. Because subsequent business intervened, Pace's point is untimely and should be ruled not well taken.

ref.quote: Points of order regarding the conduct of a vote must be raised immediately following the announcement of the voting result .

prompt: Three months ago the Riverside Arts Guild adopted a motion that directly conflicts with a provision of its bylaws. Member Tran only now realizes the problem and wants to challenge it, but another member says it is far too late. Chair Duval asks whether a point of order can still be raised. What is your ruling?
gold: A point of order can still be raised. The timeliness requirement does not apply to breaches of a continuing nature that render the action null and void, and a motion adopted in conflict with the bylaws is such a case. The point may be raised at any time the action has continuing force and effect, so it is not barred by the delay.

ref.quote: The only exceptions to the requirement that a point of order must be made promptly at the time of the breach arise in connection with breaches that are of a continuing nature, whereby the action taken in violation of the rules is null and void.

prompt: Last year the Cedar Falls Athletic Club adopted a standing policy by a procedure that violated a fundamental principle of parliamentary law. The measure is still in force. A member argues that because a full year has passed, nothing can be done. President Okafor asks you to explain whether elapsed time bars a point of order here. How do you respond?
gold: Elapsed time does not bar the point. When a breach is of a continuing nature—such as action taken in violation of a fundamental principle of parliamentary law—a point of order can be raised at any time during the continuance of the breach, regardless of how much time has elapsed. If sustained, the action is null and void.

ref.quote: In such cases, a point of order can be made at any time during the continuance of the breach—that is, at any time that the action has continuing force and effect—regardless of how much time has elapsed.

prompt: At the Pinehurst Neighborhood Council, a member insists that any error can be fixed later and asks the chair to identify a situation where a point of order survives past the moment of the breach. Chair Meadows turns to you for a concrete example of a continuing breach. Give one and explain why it qualifies.
gold: One clear example is where a main motion has been adopted that conflicts with the bylaws (or constitution) of the organization. Such action is a continuing breach that is null and void, so a point of order against it may be raised at any time the action remains in force, not merely when the breach occurred.

ref.quote: a main motion has been adopted that conflicts with the bylaws (or constitution) of the organization or assembly

prompt: President Halvorsen of the Westbrook Service Club ruled on a contested point of order and stated her reasons, which the secretary recorded. A member asks whether that ruling has any effect beyond the immediate meeting. How would you explain the significance of the recorded ruling?
gold: The ruling and its stated rationale, recorded in the minutes, serve as a precedent for future reference by the chair and the assembly, unless it is overturned on appeal. If it is appealed and reversed, that result is likewise recorded and may create a contrary precedent. Such precedents are persuasive but not binding.

ref.quote: The ruling and its rationale serve as a precedent for future reference by the chair and the assembly, unless overturned on appeal, the result of which is also recorded in the minutes and may create a contrary precedent.

prompt: At the Fairmont Debate Society, a member cites a single ruling the chair made two meetings ago as though it settles a current dispute conclusively. Another member cites a line of rulings the society has followed consistently for years. Chair Reyes asks how much weight each carries. Explain.
gold: Neither precedent is strictly binding, but their weight differs. The weight given to a precedent increases with the number of times the same or similar rulings have been repeated and with how long the assembly has consistently adhered to them. The long-followed line of rulings is therefore far more persuasive than the single recent ruling.

ref.quote: The weight given to precedent increases with the number of times the same or similar rulings have been repeated and with the length of time during which the assembly has consistently adhered to them.

prompt: The secretary of the Oakmont Historical Society, Ms. Pruitt, asks you what she must capture when the chair rules on a point of order, since she usually just writes "point of order overruled." Why is that entry incomplete, and what should the minutes contain?
gold: The entry is incomplete because the minutes must include the reasons the chair gave for the ruling, not merely the outcome. Recording the rationale is what allows the ruling to serve as a precedent for future reference. The secretary should record both the ruling and the chair's stated reasons.

ref.quote: The minutes include the reasons given by the chair for his or her ruling ).

conceptName: Precedential nature and superseding a ruling
prompt: The Brookdale Alumni Chapter has followed a particular precedent for years, but a majority now think it was wrong. A member claims the chapter is permanently stuck with it. Chair Vance asks how, procedurally, an unsatisfactory precedent can be displaced. What do you advise?
gold: The chapter is not stuck with it. A precedent may be overruled, in whole or in part, by a later ruling of the chair or by a decision of the assembly on an appeal in a similar situation, which then creates a new precedent. Precedents are persuasive, not binding, so a fresh ruling or a sustained appeal can supersede the old one.

ref.quote: If an assembly is or becomes dissatisfied with a precedent, it may be overruled, in whole or in part, by a later ruling of the chair or a decision of the assembly in an appeal in a similar situation, which will then create a new precedent.

conceptName: Precedential nature and superseding a ruling
prompt: The Glenwood Foundation dislikes a standing precedent, but its members would rather change the underlying rule than wait for a similar situation to re-litigate the point. President Sato asks whether there is a way to attack the basis of the precedent directly. Explain the alternative route.
gold: Yes. Instead of waiting to overrule the precedent through a new ruling or appeal, the assembly may alter the rule or policy on which the precedent was based—by adopting, rescinding, or amending the relevant bylaw provision, special rule of order, standing rule, or other motion. Changing the underlying rule removes the foundation for the unsatisfactory precedent.

ref.quote: Alternatively, adoption, rescission, or amendment of a bylaw provision, special rule of order, standing rule, or other motion may alter the rule or policy on which the unsatisfactory precedent was based.

conceptName: Precedential nature and superseding a ruling
prompt: At the Trailhead Outdoors Club, a member insists the chair is absolutely bound to follow a prior ruling and has no discretion. Chair Iverson asks you to clarify the true status of a precedent. How binding is it?
gold: A precedent is persuasive, not binding. Prior rulings carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course, but they do not bind the chair or the assembly. The chair may depart from a precedent for good reason, and the assembly can create a contrary precedent on appeal.

ref.quote: they carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the chair or the assembly.

conceptName: Two exceptions to the right to Appeal
prompt: At the Maplewood Community Theater, an appeal from a chair's ruling is pending before the assembly when member Ndiaye raises a new point of order about how the appeal is being handled. The chair rules on that point, and Ndiaye immediately tries to appeal this second ruling too. Chair Lund asks whether that second appeal is allowed. Rule.
gold: The second appeal is not allowed. When a point of order is raised while an appeal is already pending, there is no appeal from the chair's decision on that point of order. The correctness of the chair's ruling can be raised later by a motion covering the case, but a member cannot stack an appeal on top of the pending one.
keywords: point of order while appeal pending

ref.quote: if a point of order is raised while an appeal is pending, there is no appeal from the chair's decision on this point of order, although the correctness of the ruling can be brought up later by a motion covering the case; and b) when the chair rules on a question about which there cannot possibly be two reasonable opinions, an appeal would be dilatory and is not allowed.

conceptName: Two exceptions to the right to Appeal
prompt: At the Sunnyvale Toastmasters, the chair ruled on a matter so clear-cut that no reasonable person could differ, yet member Bragg immediately appeals, apparently to stall. Chair Whitfield asks whether she must entertain the appeal. What is your advice?
gold: She need not entertain it. Although an Appeal can normally be applied to any ruling of the chair, when the chair rules on a question about which there cannot possibly be two reasonable opinions, an appeal would be dilatory and is not allowed. The chair may decline to accept it as an improper, dilatory motion.

ref.quote: when the chair rules on a question about which there cannot possibly be two reasonable opinions, an appeal would be dilatory and is not allowed.

conceptName: Two exceptions to the right to Appeal
prompt: A member of the Bayside Rowing Club asserts that the right to appeal is absolutely unlimited—any ruling, in any circumstance, may be appealed. Chair Estrada asks you whether that is accurate, and if not, what the boundaries are. Explain.
gold: It is not unlimited. An Appeal can be applied to any ruling by the presiding officer except in two situations: there is no appeal from the chair's decision on a point of order raised while an appeal is already pending, and no appeal is allowed when the chair rules on a question about which there cannot possibly be two reasonable opinions. Outside those exceptions, a ruling may be appealed if timely.
keywords: point of order while appeal pending

ref.quote: Can be applied to any ruling by the presiding officer except that: a) if a point of order is raised while an appeal is pending, there is no appeal from the chair's decision on this point of order, although the correctness of the ruling can be brought up later by a motion covering the case; and b) when the chair rules on a question about which there cannot possibly be two reasonable opinions, an appeal would be dilatory and is not allowed.

conceptName: Standard descriptive characteristics, undebatable cases, timeliness
prompt: At the Kingsport Choral Union, an appeal is taken from the chair's ruling that a member's remarks in debate were out of order for indecorum. A member wants to debate the appeal at length. Chair Marsh asks whether this appeal is debatable. Rule and explain.
gold: This appeal is not debatable. An Appeal is normally debatable, but not when it relates to indecorum or a transgression of the rules of speaking, to the priority of business, or when an undebatable question is immediately pending. Since this appeal concerns indecorum in debate, the chair should put it to a vote without debate.

ref.quote: Is debatable, unless it (a) relates to indecorum or a transgression of the rules of speaking; (b) relates to the priority of business; or (c) is made when an undebatable question is immediately pending or involved in the appeal.

conceptName: Standard descriptive characteristics, undebatable cases, timeliness
prompt: At the Hollow Creek Preservation Society, an appeal from the chair's ruling produces an exact tie vote. A member says the tie means the ruling is overturned. Chair Delgado asks you what the tie actually accomplishes. What is the effect of the vote?
gold: The tie sustains the chair. On an appeal, a majority or a tie vote sustains the decision of the chair, on the principle that the chair's decision stands until reversed by a majority. Because it takes a majority against the ruling to overturn it, a tie leaves the ruling in force.

ref.quote: A majority or a tie vote sustains the decision of the chair on the principle that the chair’s decision stands until reversed by a majority.

conceptName: Standard descriptive characteristics, undebatable cases, timeliness
prompt: At the Riverbend Quilters Guild, the chair made a ruling, the assembly debated the underlying motion for several minutes, and now member Fenwick wants to appeal that earlier ruling. Chair Osborne asks whether the appeal is timely. Rule.
gold: The appeal is too late. An appeal must be made at the time of the chair's ruling; once any debate or business has intervened, it is too late to appeal. Because debate proceeded after the ruling, Fenwick's appeal is untimely and the chair should not entertain it.
keywords: at the time of the ruling

ref.quote: If an appeal is to be made, it must be made at the time of the chair’s ruling. If any debate or business has intervened, it is too late to appeal.

prompt: After a voice vote at the Ferndale Booster Club, the chair declared, "The ayes have it." Member Salas doubts that and immediately says, "I appeal the chair's judgment on the count." Chair Pryor asks whether an appeal is the right tool. Advise, and state the correct remedy.
gold: An appeal is not available. The chair's judgment as to the more numerous side in a vote, or whether there is a two-thirds vote, is not a ruling and is not subject to appeal. The proper remedy for a member who doubts the announced result is to call for a Division or move that the vote be counted.

ref.quote: The chair’s judgment as to the more numerous side in a vote, or whether there are two thirds in the affirmative, also is not a ruling and is not subject to appeal.

prompt: At the Clearwater Sailing Association, member Voss made a parliamentary inquiry, and the chair answered that a particular motion would not be in order right then. Voss tries to appeal that answer. Chair Ackerman asks whether the chair's answer can be appealed. Explain.
gold: The answer cannot be appealed. A reply to a parliamentary inquiry is an opinion, not a ruling on a question that has actually arisen, and is not subject to appeal. If Voss disagrees, he may make the motion despite the chair's opinion; then, if the chair rules it out of order, that ruling can be appealed.

ref.quote: For example, if, in answer to a parliamentary inquiry, the chair states that a certain motion would not be in order at the time, this reply is not subject to appeal.

prompt: A member of the Highland Curling Club insists that any statement the chair makes—advice, an opinion, an informal comment—can be appealed to the assembly. Chair Whitlock asks you to state precisely what an appeal may reach. Clarify.
gold: An appeal is applicable only to a ruling by the chair. Opinions, such as answers to parliamentary inquiries or informal advice, are not rulings and cannot be appealed. Only when the chair actually rules on a question that has arisen may a member, timely, appeal that decision.

ref.quote: As explained in Standard Characteristic 2, an appeal is applicable only to a ruling by the chair.

prompt: At the Prairie Wind Grange, the assembly has just voted on an appeal from the chair's ruling and the result has been announced. The chair is unsure what to do next. Chair Boone asks you how the meeting should proceed after the appeal is decided. Advise.
gold: Once the result of the vote on the appeal is announced, business is resumed in accordance with the parliamentary situation existing after the action on the appeal. In other words, the chair takes up the pending business as governed by whether the ruling was sustained or reversed. No further vote on the appeal is needed.

ref.quote: After the result of the vote is announced, business is resumed in accordance with the situation existing after the action on the appeal.

prompt: At the Cascade Athletic Boosters, an appeal has been seconded, and the chair is about to state the question as, "Shall the chair be sustained?" A member objects to that wording. Chair Ramsey asks you how the question on an appeal should properly be stated and why. Explain.
gold: The question should be stated on the decision, not the officer—for example, "Shall the decision of the chair stand as the judgment of the assembly?" The question is not on "sustaining the chair," because it is the decision, not the presiding officer, that is in question. Framing it around the person is incorrect.

ref.quote: The question is not on “sustaining the chair,” because the decision, not the presiding officer, is in question.

prompt: At the Fox Hollow Riding Club, a member is confused about which way to vote on a pending appeal—he wants the chair's ruling upheld but isn't sure whether "aye" supports it. Chair Alcott asks how the vote on an appeal is framed so members know what a yes vote means. Explain.
gold: On an appeal, the vote is taken so that the affirmative is in favor of sustaining the chair's decision. A member who wants the ruling upheld votes "aye"; one who wants it reversed votes "no." A majority or tie sustains the chair, so it takes a majority in the negative to overturn the ruling.

ref.quote: The vote is taken so that the affirmative will be in favor of sustaining the chair's decision, as follows: CHAIR: Those in favor of sustaining the chair's decision, say aye.… Those opposed to sustaining this decision, say no.…

conceptName: Seven categories that cannot be suspended
prompt: At a poorly attended meeting of the Elmhurst Ratepayers Association, a member moves to suspend the rules to conduct business not listed in the call of this special meeting, arguing that everyone present agrees. Chair Devi asks whether the rules can be suspended for that purpose. Rule.
gold: They cannot. The rule restricting business at a special meeting to matters in the call protects absentees and cannot be suspended when any member is absent—even by unanimous consent—because the absentees do not consent. The motion to Suspend the Rules is out of order for this purpose, and any action taken would be null and void.

ref.quote: For example, the rules requiring the presence of a quorum, restricting business transacted at a special meeting to that mentioned in the call of the meeting, and requiring previous notice of a proposed amendment to the bylaws protect absentees, if there are any, and cannot be suspended when any member is absent.

conceptName: Seven categories that cannot be suspended
prompt: The Aspen Grove Conservancy has a standing policy capping annual donations to any one charity. Tonight a member moves to suspend the rules so the group can exceed that cap with a single large gift. Chair Holloway asks whether suspension is proper here. Advise.
gold: Suspension is not proper. Rules that have their application outside of the session in progress cannot be suspended, and a policy limiting yearly contributions operates outside the meeting context. The motion to Suspend the Rules is out of order; if the group wants to exceed the cap, it must instead rescind or amend the policy.

ref.quote: Rules that have their application outside of the session which is in progress cannot be suspended.

conceptName: Seven categories that cannot be suspended
prompt: At the Whitmore Cooperative, a member moves to suspend the rules to permit cumulative voting on a pending question, claiming a two-thirds vote can authorize anything. Chair Bianchi asks whether even a large vote can accomplish this. Rule.
gold: No vote can accomplish it. Cumulative voting cannot be authorized by suspending the rules, because it is a fundamental principle of parliamentary law that each member is entitled to one—and only one—vote on a question. Rules embodying fundamental principles cannot be suspended even by a unanimous vote, so the motion is out of order.

ref.quote: since it is a fundamental principle that each member of a deliberative assembly is entitled to one-and only one-vote on a question, the rules may not be suspended so as to authorize cumulative voting .

prompt: At the Thornbury Literary Society, a member moves to suspend the rules and postpone a question all the way to a session six months out, past the next regular business session. Chair Ephron asks whether suspension can authorize that postponement. Rule.
gold: It cannot. The rules cannot be suspended to permit postponing a motion to a future session held after the next regular business session, or after more than a quarterly time interval has elapsed. This limit protects the ordinary control of business and cannot be waived by Suspend the Rules, so the motion is out of order.

ref.quote: Likewise, the rules cannot be suspended in order to permit postponement of a motion to a future session that will be held after the next regular business session or that will be held after more than a quarterly time interval has elapsed.

prompt: At the Marlborough Women's Club, the assembly adopted a motion to dispense with the regular order of business and proceed at once to a certain report. After that report is finished, a member claims the meeting is over. Chair Sinclair asks what she must do next. Advise.
gold: The meeting is not over. Voting to dispense with the order of business and proceed to a subject only suspends the rules to pass the intervening classes; once the matter taken up out of order is disposed of, the chair must return to the regular order of business and call for the items in sequence, unless the assembly then votes to adjourn. The chair should resume the order of business.

ref.quote: In such a case, when the matter taken up out of its proper order has been disposed of, even if it has consumed as much time as the usual meeting, the chair must return to the regular order of business and call for the items in sequence, unless the assembly then votes to adjourn .

prompt: The Beaumont Historical Trust is incorporated. A member moves to suspend the rules to act contrary to a requirement in the organization's corporate charter, arguing the members can waive their own rules. Chair Loomis asks whether the charter provision can be suspended. Rule.
gold: It cannot. Nothing in a corporate charter can be suspended unless the charter or applicable law so provides. The motion to Suspend the Rules is out of order as to a charter requirement, and any action contrary to the charter would be null and void.

ref.quote: Nothing in a corporate charter can be suspended unless the charter or applicable law so provides.

conceptName: Standard descriptive characteristics and vote required
prompt: The Ridgeline Alpine Club has a special rule requiring a certain matter to be decided by a protected process. A member moves to suspend that rule; when the vote is taken, the number opposing suspension is as large as the minority the rule protects. Chair Navarro asks whether the suspension can pass. Rule.
gold: The suspension fails. Although Suspend the Rules generally requires a two-thirds vote, no rule protecting a minority of a particular size can be suspended in the face of a negative vote as large as the minority the rule protects. Since the opposition equals that protected minority, the motion cannot be adopted regardless of a two-thirds tally.
keywords: negative vote as large as the minority

ref.quote: In any case, no rule protecting a minority of a particular size can be suspended in the face of a negative vote as large as the minority protected by the rule. 8.

conceptName: Standard descriptive characteristics and vote required
prompt: At the Danforth Camera Club, a member argues that because the club labels all its rules "standing rules," any of them can be suspended by a bare majority. One such rule plainly governs parliamentary procedure. Chair Whitby asks what vote is required to suspend it. Advise.
gold: A two-thirds vote is required. The label does not control—by whatever name a rule is called, if it relates to parliamentary procedure, its suspension requires a two-thirds vote. Calling it a "standing rule" does not reduce the requirement to a majority.

ref.quote: But by whatever name a rule is called, if it relates to parliamentary procedure, it requires a two-thirds vote for its suspension.

conceptName: Standard descriptive characteristics and vote required
prompt: The Oakridge Model Railroad Society has an ordinary standing rule barring recording devices at meetings—a rule that does not relate to parliamentary procedure. A member wants it set aside for tonight's session. Chair Tuck asks what vote is needed and whether it can be lifted for the whole session. Advise.
gold: Because it is an ordinary standing rule not relating to parliamentary procedure, it can be suspended by a majority vote, unlike rules of order that require two-thirds. Moreover, through an incidental main motion adopted by a majority vote, the standing rule can be suspended for the duration of the current session, not just a single instance.

ref.quote: Standing rules can be suspended by a majority vote. Through an incidental main motion adopted by a majority vote, a standing rule can be suspended for the duration of the current session.

conceptName: Purpose and manner of suspending rules
prompt: A new officer of the Stonegate Homeowners Association, Mr. Paulsen, asks what the motion to Suspend the Rules is actually for and which kinds of rules it can reach. Explain its object.
gold: The object of Suspend the Rules is to set aside one or more rules that interfere with a proposed action during a meeting. It can reach rules such as those in the parliamentary authority, special rules of order, and standing rules. It cannot reach the bylaws, procedural law, or fundamental principles of parliamentary law.

ref.quote: The object of this motion is to suspend one or more rules applicable to the assembly— such as rules contained in the parliamentary authority, special rules of order, or standing rules—that interfere with proposed action during a meeting.

conceptName: Purpose and manner of suspending rules
prompt: At the Cliffside Ratepayers meeting, President Aziz wants to take up a courtesy resolution slightly out of order, and the matter is plainly uncontroversial. She wonders whether she must have a formal motion to suspend the rules and a two-thirds vote. What is the simpler route?
gold: For an uncontroversial matter, she can save time by asking unanimous consent rather than making a formal motion to suspend the rules. She simply asks if there is objection; if none, the rules are effectively suspended. If any member objects, she proceeds to take a vote on suspending the rules, just as if a formal motion had been made.

ref.quote: Frequently, when the matter is clearly not controversial, time may be saved by asking unanimous consent rather than by making a formal motion to suspend the rules.

conceptName: Purpose and manner of suspending rules
prompt: At the Brentwood Chess Federation, a member moves to suspend the rules and wants to explain his reason, but another member objects that the motion is undebatable so no one may say anything about it. Chair Marconi asks whether the mover may offer any explanation. Advise.
gold: The mover may offer a brief explanation. Although the motion to Suspend the Rules is undebatable, the member moving it can briefly give sufficient information to enable the members to vote intelligently. This is not debate, and the motion still requires a second and generally a two-thirds vote.

ref.quote: A member moving to suspend the rules can briefly give sufficient information to enable the members to vote intelligently on his undebatable motion. (For the manner of taking a two-thirds vote, see 4:38-39 and provisions (d), (e), and (g) in 4:49.) In announcing an affirmative result, the chair says, for example, CHAIR: There are two thirds in the affirmative and the rules are suspended for the purpose of… The chair recognizes Mrs. Watkins.

prompt: During a member's speech at the Templeton Debate Club, member Faris rises to make a parliamentary inquiry that genuinely needs an immediate answer to know whether a motion is in order. Another member complains that Faris cannot speak while someone else has the floor. Chair Odell asks whether the inquiry is in order now. Rule.
gold: The inquiry is in order. A Parliamentary Inquiry is in order even when another member has the floor if it requires immediate attention. It requires no second and no vote, so the chair should permit Faris to state the inquiry.
keywords: in order when another has the floor

ref.quote: All are in order when another has the floor if they require immediate attention. 4.

prompt: At the Harwich Sailing Club, a member makes a parliamentary inquiry, and several members start to argue about it as though it were a debatable motion. Chair Reinholt asks whether a parliamentary inquiry is subject to debate. Explain.
gold: A Parliamentary Inquiry is not debatable, so the chair should not allow members to argue it. Brief explanations may accompany it as necessary, but that is not debate. The chair answers the inquiry directly.

ref.quote: All are not debatable, although they may be accompanied by brief explanations as necessary (cf. 43:31-32). 6.

prompt: At the Coventry Historical Society, a member tries to "amend" another member's pending parliamentary inquiry to add a second question. Chair Byrd asks whether a parliamentary inquiry can be amended. Rule.
gold: It cannot. A Parliamentary Inquiry is not amendable. A member wishing to raise a further question simply makes his own separate inquiry; there is no amending of another member's inquiry.

ref.quote: All are not amendable. 7. No vote is taken on a Parliamentary Inquiry and a Request for Information.

prompt: A member of the Aldergrove Cycling Club rises to a parliamentary inquiry, unsure how to bring a matter before the assembly. The chair is inclined to wave the question off as not his job. President Salih asks whether the chair must respond. Advise.
gold: The chair should respond. It is the chair's duty to answer a Parliamentary Inquiry when doing so may assist a member to make an appropriate motion, raise a proper point of order, or understand the parliamentary situation or effect of a motion. The inquiry needs no second and no vote.

ref.quote: It is the chair’s duty to answer such questions when it may assist a member to make an appropriate motion, raise a proper point of order, or understand the parliamentary situation or the effect of a motion.

prompt: At the Fairhaven Astronomy Society, a member poses a parliamentary inquiry that is purely hypothetical—"What would happen if, at some future meeting, someone did such-and-such?" Chair Nakamura asks whether he must answer. Advise.
gold: He is not obliged to answer. While the chair has a duty to answer inquiries that help a member act in the present situation, the chair is not obliged to answer hypothetical questions. He may decline to respond to a purely speculative inquiry.

ref.quote: The chair is not obliged to answer hypothetical questions.

prompt: At the Kestrel Valley Grange, the chair answered a parliamentary inquiry, and a member disagrees and moves to appeal the chair's answer. Chair Ibarra asks whether the answer can be appealed, and what the member's real recourse is. Advise.
gold: The answer cannot be appealed, because a reply to a parliamentary inquiry is an opinion, not a ruling. The member's recourse is to act contrary to the opinion—make the motion anyway—and if the chair then rules adversely, appeal from that ruling.

ref.quote: The chair’s reply to a parliamentary inquiry is not subject to an appeal, since it is an opinion, not a ruling.

conceptName: Preventing misuse of Request for Information
prompt: At the Groveland Debate League, during a speech member Cortez uses a "request for information" to launch into a mini-speech rebutting the speaker, never actually asking anything. Chair Delacroix asks how to keep this device from becoming a back-door speech. Advise.
gold: A Request for Information, even when meant to rebut or remind the speaker, must always be put in the form of a question. The chair should require Cortez to phrase his point as an actual question rather than a statement; if he merely makes a speech, the chair rules him out of order. This form requirement is what prevents misuse of the device.

ref.quote: An inquiry of this kind may also be for the purpose of reminding a speaker of a point to be made in argument, or it may be intended to rebut his position; but it must always be put in the form of a question.

conceptName: Preventing misuse of Request for Information
prompt: At the Sandhill Ratepayers meeting, a request for information turns into two members debating each other back and forth directly, ignoring the chair. President Vogel asks how such an exchange must be conducted. Advise.
gold: The exchange must go through the chair. To protect decorum, members are not allowed to carry on discussion directly with one another; the inquiry, reply, and any resulting colloquy are made in the third person through the chair. The chair should redirect the two members to address the chair rather than each other.

ref.quote: To protect decorum, members are not allowed to carry on discussion directly with one another.

conceptName: Preventing misuse of Request for Information
prompt: At the Belmont Investors Club, a member asks a question of a speaker who has consented to yield. The two want to converse directly and informally. Chair Rourke asks what his role is and how the exchange should be framed. Advise.
gold: Even though the chair generally stays silent during the exchange, the inquiry, the reply, and any resulting colloquy must be made in the third person through the chair. The members may not converse directly with one another. The chair should ensure the exchange is routed through him to preserve decorum.

ref.quote: Although the presiding officer generally remains silent during the ensuing exchange, the inquiry, the reply, and any resulting colloquy are made in the third person through the chair.

prompt: During a speech at the Westford Investors Guild, member Alonzo rises for a request for information that genuinely needs answering before the assembly can proceed. A member objects that Alonzo cannot speak while someone holds the floor. Chair Petrov asks whether the request is in order now. Rule.
gold: The request is in order. A Request for Information is in order even when another member has the floor if it requires immediate attention. It requires no second and no vote, so the chair should allow Alonzo to state it.
keywords: in order when another has the floor

ref.quote: All are in order when another has the floor if they require immediate attention. 4.

prompt: At the Ravenna Photography Club, a member attempts to amend a pending request for information to broaden it. Chair Sallis asks whether a request for information can be amended. Rule.
gold: It cannot. A Request for Information is not amendable. A member who wants different information simply makes his own request; there is no amending another member's request.

ref.quote: All are not amendable. 7. No vote is taken on a Parliamentary Inquiry and a Request for Information.

prompt: At the Lindenwood Civic Forum, a request for information is made and members begin debating it as if it were a motion. Chair Whitcomb asks whether debate is proper and whether any explanation at all may be given. Advise.
gold: A Request for Information is not debatable, so the chair should not allow members to debate it. It may be accompanied by brief explanations as necessary, but that is not debate. No vote is taken on the request.

ref.quote: All are not debatable, although they may be accompanied by brief explanations as necessary (cf. 43:31-32). 6.

prompt: A member of the Carrington Foundation wants to know the current treasury balance before voting on a costly motion, and asks the chair how to get that information properly. President Mendez asks you to explain what device the member should use and how it differs from a parliamentary inquiry. Explain.
gold: The member should use a Request for Information (also called a Point of Information), directed to the chair or through the chair to another officer or member, for information relevant to the business at hand but not related to parliamentary procedure. It differs from a Parliamentary Inquiry, which concerns parliamentary law or the rules. Here, the treasurer can be asked through the chair to state the balance; the request needs no second and no vote.

ref.quote: A Request for Information (also called a Point of Information) is a request directed to the chair, or through the chair to another officer or member, for information relevant to the business at hand but not related to parliamentary procedure.

prompt: At the Ellsworth Rowing Association, member Paz wishes to ask the current speaker a question. Chair Blythe wonders whether the question can be forced on the speaker and, if the speaker agrees, whose debate time is used. Advise.
gold: The question cannot be forced on the speaker; the chair asks whether the speaker is willing to be interrupted. If the speaker consents to the interruption, the time consumed is taken out of his allowed speaking time. The exchange is conducted through the chair.

ref.quote: If the speaker consents to the interruption, the time consumed will be taken out of his allowed time.

prompt: During debate at the Hartwell Community Band, a member rises wanting to put a question to the member who is speaking. Chair Ostrander is unsure of the exact procedure for allowing the interruption. Walk the chair through the correct steps.
gold: The chair asks whether the speaker is willing to be interrupted; if the speaker consents, the chair directs the inquirer to proceed. The question and reply are made through the chair, and the time consumed comes out of the speaker's allotted time. If the speaker declines, no interruption occurs.

ref.quote: The chair therefore asks if the speaker is willing to be interrupted, and if he consents, directs the inquirer to proceed.

prompt: Last spring the Fairview Arts Council adopted a resolution creating a summer grant program. The council now wishes to cancel the whole program outright. A member asks which motion accomplishes a complete cancellation and what it does. Explain.
gold: The council should use the motion to Rescind. Its effect is to strike out an entire main motion, resolution, order, or rule adopted at some previous time—here, the whole grant resolution. Rescind and Amend Something Previously Adopted are two forms of one incidental main motion; Rescind cancels the entire action, while Amend Something Previously Adopted changes only part.

ref.quote: The effect of Rescind is to strike out an entire main motion, resolution, order, or rule that has been adopted at some previous time.

prompt: A new secretary at the Kingston Heritage Society, Mr. Vale, sees the terms "repeal" and "annul" used loosely in old minutes and asks what motion they refer to and what it does. Clarify.
gold: They refer to the motion to Rescind, also known as Repeal or Annul. It is the motion by which a previous action or order is canceled or countermanded. It is one form of the incidental main motion Rescind/Amend Something Previously Adopted, used to undo a prior action.

ref.quote: Rescind—also known as Repeal or Annul—is the motion by which a previous action or order can be canceled or countermanded.

prompt: The Brookhaven Trail Alliance adopted a policy months ago but now wants to change just one clause, not scrap the whole thing. A member asks whether they must rescind and re-adopt the entire policy. Advise.
gold: They need not rescind the whole policy. Amend Something Previously Adopted is the motion used when only part of the text is to be changed, or to substitute a different version. It changes the single clause while leaving the rest of the previously adopted policy in force.

ref.quote: Amend Something Previously Adopted is the motion that can be used if it is desired to change only a part of the text, or to substitute a different version.

prompt: At the Colton Ridge Association, member Dax rises while another member has just been assigned the floor but has not yet begun speaking, and states that at the next meeting he will move to rescind a prior action. A member objects that Dax interrupted. Chair Fenn asks whether giving that notice was proper. Rule.
gold: It was proper. The motions to Rescind and Amend Something Previously Adopted are out of order when another has the floor, but previous notice of intent to offer one of them at the next meeting may be given after a member has been assigned the floor, provided that member has not begun to speak. Since the assigned member had not yet started speaking, Dax's notice was in order.
keywords: out of order when another has the floor

ref.quote: Are out of order when another has the floor; but previous notice of intent to offer one of these motions at the next meeting can be given after another member has been assigned the floor, provided that he has not begun to speak. 4.

prompt: At the Deerfield Booster Club, a motion to rescind a previously adopted spending decision is pending. A member claims debate must be limited to whether to rescind and may not touch the wisdom of the original decision. Chair Halloran asks how far debate may range. Advise.
gold: Debate may range into the merits of the underlying question. The motions to Rescind and Amend Something Previously Adopted are debatable, and debate can go into the merits of the question proposed to be rescinded or amended. Members may therefore argue the wisdom of the original spending decision, not merely the procedural question.

ref.quote: Are debatable; debate can go into the merits of the question which it is proposed to rescind or amend. 6.

prompt: At the Wexford Camera Club, member Ojo alone stands and moves to rescind a prior resolution; no one else says anything. The chair is about to state the question. Chair Ainsley asks whether anything is missing before she can state it. Advise.
gold: A second is missing. The motion to Rescind must be seconded. Without a second, the chair should not state the question; she should ask if there is a second, and if none is offered, the motion is not before the assembly.

ref.quote: Must be seconded. 5. Are debatable; debate can go into the merits of the question which it is proposed to rescind or amend. 6.

prompt: At the Northshore Yacht Club, no previous notice was given, but a member moves to rescind an ordinary resolution adopted last month, and only a simple majority favors it. Chair Reyes asks whether that vote is enough to rescind. Rule.
gold: A bare majority without notice is not enough. To rescind an ordinary motion, adoption requires any one of the following: a two-thirds vote, a majority vote with previous notice, or a vote of a majority of the entire membership. With no notice given, a simple majority of those present does not meet any of these thresholds, so the motion fails unless it reaches two-thirds or a majority of the entire membership.

ref.quote: In an assembly, except when applied to a constitution, bylaws, or special rules of order, require (a) a two-thirds vote, (b) a majority vote when notice of intent to make the motion, stating the complete substance of the proposed change, has been given at the previous meeting within a quarterly time interval or in the call of the present meeting, or (c) a vote of a majority of the entire membership—any one of which will suffice.

prompt: The membership assembly of the Glenmoor Society wants to overturn a decision made by its executive board, which was empowered to act on the assembly's behalf. A member asks what vote the assembly needs to rescind the board's action. Advise.
gold: The assembly can rescind or amend the board's action, and the same vote is required as for rescinding its own action: a two-thirds vote, a majority with previous notice, or a majority of the entire membership—any one of which suffices. Because the board acted on the assembly's behalf, the assembly may countermand it under these thresholds.
keywords: two-thirds or notice or majority of entire membership

ref.quote: The same vote is required for the assembly to rescind or amend an action taken by subordinate bodies, such as some executive boards, empowered to act on behalf of the assembly.

prompt: At the Ashby Historical Society, notice was given to amend a special rule of order in a specific way. On the floor, a member moves a secondary amendment that would expand the change well beyond what the notice described. Chair Bello asks whether that amendment is in order. Rule.
gold: It is out of order. When these motions require previous notice—as with amending a special rule of order—the motion cannot be amended so as to make the proposed change greater than that for which notice was given. An amendment exceeding the scope of the notice is not permitted, because it would destroy the effect of the notice.

ref.quote: When these motions require previous notice (as may be the case with respect to a motion to rescind or amend a provision of the bylaws or a special rule of order), such a motion cannot be amended so as to make the proposed change greater than that for which notice has been given. 7.

conceptName: Previous notice and making the motion
prompt: At the last meeting of the Pemberton Society, member Cho gave notice that she would move to rescind a resolution. At this meeting, the moment arrives but Cho stays silent. Another member wants to make the motion instead. Chair Lyle asks whether anyone else may move it. Advise.
gold: Yes. When previous notice has been given, it is usual to wait for the member who gave the notice to move it, but if that member does not, any member can make the motion. So another member may move to rescind, and because notice was given, adoption requires only a majority vote.

ref.quote: When previous notice has been given, it is usual to wait for the member who gave notice of these motions to move them; but if he does not, any member can do so.

conceptName: Previous notice and making the motion
prompt: At the Rockport Guild, with no prior notice, a member moves to rescind a resolution adopted at the last meeting. A member asks what it will take to pass it tonight. Chair Ferris asks you to state the threshold. Advise.
gold: Without previous notice, the motion to rescind requires a two-thirds vote or a vote of a majority of the entire membership for adoption. A simple majority of those present is insufficient unless one of those higher thresholds is met.

ref.quote: Without previous notice, the motion requires a two-thirds vote or a majority of the entire membership for its adoption.

conceptName: Previous notice and making the motion
prompt: At the Havenwood Association, member Ruiz begins, "In accordance with notice given at the last meeting, I move to rescind the resolution that authorized additional landscaping." A member asks why this can pass more easily than a rescission made without warning. Chair Delaney asks you to explain the effect of the prior notice. Advise.
gold: Because previous notice of the motion was given at the last meeting, a majority vote is sufficient to adopt it. Notice lowers the threshold: without notice, rescission would require a two-thirds vote or a majority of the entire membership, but with proper notice a simple majority carries.

ref.quote: In such a case, a majority vote is sufficient.

prompt: The Wentworth Society wants to abolish the office of Historian, which requires striking several scattered references throughout the bylaws. A member proposes filing each change as a separate motion. President Amara asks whether that is the right approach. Advise.
gold: They should not be filed separately. When a series of isolated changes to the bylaws are needed to achieve one end—such as abolishing an office and eliminating all references to it—the changes should be offered in a single motion. Bundling them ensures the bylaws remain coherent as the change is made.

ref.quote: When a series of isolated changes to the bylaws are needed to achieve one end—such as abolishing the office of “Historian” and eliminating all references to it—the changes should be offered in a single motion.

prompt: At the Danby Association, a single motion bundles several interdependent bylaw amendments that only make sense together. A member demands the question be divided so each amendment can be voted on separately. Chair Vine asks whether division is allowed here. Rule.
gold: Division is not allowed here. Where the changes are so related that all of the individual amendments must be made together for the bylaws to be coherent, the motion cannot be divided. The chair should deny the demand for division and keep the interdependent amendments as one question.

ref.quote: If the changes are related in such a way that all of the individual amendments must be made, if any one of them is made, in order for the bylaws to be coherent, then the motion cannot be divided .

prompt: At the Meridian Club, a member offers a single motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted that makes several distinct, independent changes to a prior policy, and another member wants some parts separated out for individual votes. Chair Poole asks where the rules on dividing such a motion come from and whether independent parts can be split. Explain.
gold: Several amendments to previously adopted text may be offered in one incidental main motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted, and whether and how it may be divided is governed by the rules on Division of a Question. Independent parts, each capable of standing alone, may be divided on demand; but parts so related that they must stand or fall together cannot be separated.

ref.quote: See 10:25 and 27:5 for the rules governing whether and how such a motion may be divided. (For an example, see 57:3.) 35:6 Actions That Cannot Be Rescinded or Amended.

prompt: Right after the Fairlawn Club adopted a motion, new information surfaced showing the decision was hasty. A member asks what motion lets the assembly revisit the vote it just took and why such a device exists. Explain.
gold: The motion to Reconsider serves this purpose. Its purpose is to permit correction of hasty, ill-advised, or erroneous action, or to take account of added information or a changed situation arising since the vote. It lets a majority reopen a question already voted on, within a limited time and without notice.

ref.quote: The purpose of reconsidering a vote is to permit correction of hasty, ill-advised, or erroneous action, or to take into account added information or a changed situation that has developed since the taking of the vote.

prompt: At the Oakmont Society, a motion was adopted and member Reilly, who voted against it, now wants to move to reconsider it. Chair Sato asks whether Reilly is eligible to make the motion. Rule.
gold: Reilly is not eligible. Except in committees, the motion to Reconsider can be made only by a member who voted on the prevailing side—here, someone who voted for the adopted motion. Reilly, having voted against it, cannot move to reconsider; he must persuade a prevailing-side member to do so. The motion still requires a second, which any member may give.

ref.quote: Except in committees, it can be made only by a member who voted on the prevailing side. b) Except in committees, it must be moved either on the same day the original vote was taken or on the next succeeding day within the same session on which a business meeting is held. c) It can be made even when its consideration would not be in order, in which case it may be "called up" for consideration at a later time whose limits are longer than those for its making.

prompt: At a one-day meeting of the Bellhaven Club, a member wants to move to reconsider a vote taken earlier in the very same meeting. Another member insists reconsideration always has to wait until a future meeting. Chair Nunez asks about the timing rule. Advise.
gold: Reconsideration does not wait for a future meeting. Except in committees, it must be moved either on the same day the original vote was taken or, in a session of more than one day, on the next succeeding day within the same session on which a business meeting is held. In this one-day meeting, moving to reconsider the same day is proper and timely.

ref.quote: Except in committees, it must be moved either on the same day the original vote was taken or on the next succeeding day within the same session on which a business meeting is held. c) It can be made even when its consideration would not be in order, in which case it may be "called up" for consideration at a later time whose limits are longer than those for its making.

prompt: At the Crestwood Guild, prevailing-side member Iman wants to reconsider a main-motion vote taken minutes ago. She could raise it now, while nothing else is pending, or wait. Chair Boyle asks you to advise on the better timing. What do you say?
gold: It is usually better to make the motion to Reconsider when no other business is pending, so it can be taken up immediately—unless there may be no later opportunity or there is an important reason to do otherwise. Since nothing else is pending, Iman should move it now; the chair will then state the question on the motion to Reconsider at once.

ref.quote: In proposing a reconsideration of the vote on a main motion, it is usually better to make the motion to Reconsider when no other business is pending and the motion can be taken up immediately—unless it appears that there may be no such opportunity or there is an important reason for doing otherwise.

prompt: At the Marlowe Association, a resolution was adopted earlier by unanimous consent. Member Pike now moves to reconsider it. Because no formal vote was recorded, the chair is unsure how to verify Pike's standing to move it. Chair Renata asks how to proceed. Advise.
gold: Because the resolution was adopted by unanimous consent, the chair asks whether the member was present at the time. All members present at an adoption by unanimous consent are treated as having voted on the prevailing side, so if Pike was present he qualifies to move to reconsider. If he was not, another member who was present must make the motion.

ref.quote: If the resolution was adopted by unanimous consent, the chair asks whether the member was present at the time.

prompt: At the Sutton Club, member Faye moves to reconsider a vote, but it turns out she did not vote with the prevailing side, so she is disqualified from making the motion. A colleague who did vote with the prevailing side is willing to step in. Chair Odom asks whether the reconsideration can still be brought. Advise.
gold: It can still be brought. Since Faye did not vote with the prevailing side, she cannot make the motion, but another member who did vote with the prevailing side may make the motion to Reconsider if he desires. The motion must be seconded, which any member may do.

ref.quote: If the member did not vote with the prevailing side, another member who did so can make the motion to Reconsider, if he desires.

prompt: The Harborlight Society voted to authorize demolishing an old shed, and the shed has already been torn down. A member now moves to reconsider that vote. Chair Espinoza asks whether reconsideration is available. Rule.
gold: Reconsideration is not available. Reconsider cannot be applied to any vote which has caused something to be done that it is impossible to undo. Because the shed has already been demolished, the vote cannot be reconsidered; the motion is out of order.

ref.quote: any vote which has caused something to be done that it is impossible to undo; f) a vote on a motion to Reconsider; g) an election that has become final as provided in 46:46; or h) when practically the same result as desired can be obtained by some other parliamentary motion that can be adopted by a majority vote without previous notice.

prompt: The Brookline Association voted down a motion to spend its funds on library books, then adopted a motion to spend the same funds on athletic equipment. A member now wants to reconsider the rejected library-books vote. Chair Cutler asks whether that reconsideration is in order. Rule.
gold: It is not in order. Reconsidering the rejected library-books vote would, if adopted, place before the assembly a motion conflicting with the athletic-equipment motion previously adopted and still in force. To reconsider the library-books vote, the assembly must first rescind or amend the conflicting adopted motion (or reconsider and reverse it).

ref.quote: No motion to reconsider the vote on the motion to purchase library books will thereafter be in order because, if adopted, it would place before the assembly a motion which conflicts with a motion previously adopted and still in force .

prompt: The Fenwick Club voted to hire a caterer, and the caterer has already been notified that the contract is accepted. A member wants to reconsider the vote. Chair Iverson asks whether reconsideration is proper now. Rule.
gold: Reconsideration is not proper. Reconsider cannot be applied to an affirmative vote in the nature of a contract once the party to the contract has been notified of the outcome. Because the caterer has been notified of the acceptance, the vote can no longer be reconsidered.

ref.quote: an affirmative vote in the nature of a contract when the party to the contract has been notified of the outcome; e) any vote which has caused something to be done that it is impossible to undo; f) a vote on a motion to Reconsider; g) an election that has become final as provided in 46:46; or h) when practically the same result as desired can be obtained by some other parliamentary motion that can be adopted by a majority vote without previous notice.

conceptName: When Reconsider cannot be taken up at once
prompt: At the Weston Society, a motion to reconsider a main-motion vote was made but could not be taken up before the meeting adjourned. The next regular meeting is three weeks away. A member fears the reconsideration has lapsed. Chair Alcott asks until when it may be called up. Advise.
