gold: The reconsideration has not lapsed. A motion to Reconsider that could not be taken up may be called up at any later meeting of the same session, and—since the next regular business session falls within a quarterly time interval—at any meeting of that next session or at any intervening special meeting called for that purpose. It may be called up when no question is pending and no one else has the floor.

ref.quote: If the next regular business session will be held within a quarterly time interval , it may also be called up at any meeting of that next session, or at any intervening special meeting called for that purpose.

conceptName: When Reconsider cannot be taken up at once
prompt: At the Danvers Club, member Roper moved to reconsider a vote on the same day but wants to wait until a later meeting-day to call it up so he can gather information and allow fuller debate. Another member demands it be taken up immediately. Chair Selby asks whether Roper may control the timing. Advise.
gold: Roper may generally control the timing. So long as business is not unreasonably delayed and he acts in good faith, the mover is entitled to have the reconsideration take place at a time he feels will allow the fullest and fairest reexamination of the question. He need not have it taken up immediately, though the chair should watch that business is not unduly delayed.

ref.quote: So long as business is not unreasonably delayed and the mover of the reconsideration acts in good faith, he is entitled to have it take place at a time he feels will make for the fullest and fairest reexamination of the question.

conceptName: When Reconsider cannot be taken up at once
prompt: At a three-day convention of the Statewide Educators League, a delegate wants to move to reconsider a vote that was taken on the first day, but it is now the third day. Chair Molina asks whether the motion can still be made. Rule.
gold: It is too late. In a convention or session of more than one day, a reconsideration can be moved only on the same day the original vote was taken or on the next succeeding business-meeting day within the session. Because the vote was taken on the first day and it is now the third, the time to move to reconsider has passed.
keywords: same day or next succeeding day

ref.quote: In a convention or session of more than one day, a reconsideration can be moved only on the same day the original vote was taken or on the next succeeding day within the session on which a business meeting is held.

prompt: The Ashcroft Society adopted a bylaws-related measure that had required a two-thirds vote. A member now moves to reconsider that vote and argues the reconsideration itself must also reach two-thirds. Chair Dubois asks what vote is needed to adopt the motion to Reconsider. Rule.
gold: The motion to Reconsider requires only a majority vote, regardless of the vote necessary to adopt the motion being reconsidered. So even though the original measure needed two-thirds, a simple majority adopts the motion to Reconsider. It must have been made by a prevailing-side member and seconded.

ref.quote: Requires only a majority vote, regardless of the vote necessary to adopt the motion to be reconsidered. (But see 37:35 for a different rule in the case of standing and special committees.) 8.

prompt: At the Larkspur Club, prevailing-side member Voss moves to reconsider, and member Kane—who voted on the losing side—offers the second. A member protests that only another prevailing-side member may second it. Chair Prewitt asks whether Kane's second is valid. Rule.
gold: Kane's second is valid. The motion to Reconsider must be seconded at the time it is made, but while the maker must have voted with the prevailing side, the second may be given by any member regardless of how he voted. Kane, though he voted on the losing side, may properly second the motion.

ref.quote: Must be seconded at the time it is made. Unlike the making of the motion, which must be done by a person who voted with the prevailing side, the seconding can be done by any member regardless of how he voted on the motion to be reconsidered.

prompt: At the Ferncliff Association, a member moves to reconsider the vote on a debatable main motion. Another member insists the motion to Reconsider is never debatable and that, even if it were, debate could not touch the underlying question. Chair Osei asks you to clarify. Advise.
gold: Both assertions are wrong. The motion to Reconsider is debatable whenever the motion proposed to be reconsidered would be debatable, and when debatable it opens to debate the merits of the question whose reconsideration is proposed. Since the underlying main motion was debatable, debate on the motion to Reconsider may go into that question's merits.

ref.quote: Is debatable whenever the motion proposed to be reconsidered would be debatable, and when debatable, opens to debate the merits of the question whose reconsideration is proposed. (See 37:29-32, however, regarding a series of motions proposed to be reconsidered, and the question that is opened to debate in such a case.) When the motion proposed to be reconsidered is not debatable-either because of its nature or because it is subject to an unexhausted order for the Previous Question -the motion to Reconsider is undebatable (but see 43:31-32 regarding allowable explanation of an undebatable motion).

prompt: At the Halewood Society, a motion authorizing an immediate payment was adopted, and moments later a prevailing-side member moved to reconsider that vote. The treasurer asks whether he should go ahead and issue the payment now. Chair Barrett asks you what effect the pending reconsideration has. Advise.
gold: The treasurer should not issue the payment yet. The mere making of a motion to Reconsider suspends all action that depends on the result of the vote proposed to be reconsidered. The authorized payment is held up until the motion to Reconsider is disposed of.

ref.quote: The effect of making a motion to Reconsider is the suspension of all action that depends on the result of the vote proposed to be reconsidered.

prompt: At the Ridgeway Club, the assembly has just adopted the motion to Reconsider a prior vote. A member asks what happens to the original vote now. Chair Amell asks you to explain the effect of adopting the reconsideration. Advise.
gold: Adopting the motion to Reconsider cancels the original vote altogether. The question then comes back before the assembly in the position it occupied just before it was originally voted on, open again to debate, amendment, and a new vote. The prior decision no longer stands.

ref.quote: If the motion to Reconsider is adopted, the original vote on the motion being reconsidered is thereby canceled altogether; but if the suspending effect is terminated by a vote rejecting the motion to Reconsider or in any other way, the situation becomes the same as if there had been no such motion, and the vote which it was proposed to reconsider-and any other action held up because of the proposed reconsideration- comes into full force, as if in effect, so far as applicable, from the time the vote was originally taken.

prompt: At the Colby Association, a motion to Reconsider was made—suspending action on an adopted measure—but the assembly then voted the reconsideration down. A member asks whether the original measure is now dead or back in force. Chair Nye asks you to explain. Advise.
gold: The original measure is back in full force. When the suspending effect is terminated by rejecting the motion to Reconsider (or in any other way), the situation becomes the same as if no such motion had been made. The vote proposed to be reconsidered, and any action held up, comes into effect as if from the time it was originally taken.

ref.quote: if the suspending effect is terminated by a vote rejecting the motion to Reconsider or in any other way, the situation becomes the same as if there had been no such motion, and the vote which it was proposed to reconsider-and any other action held up because of the proposed reconsideration- comes into full force, as if in effect, so far as applicable, from the time the vote was originally taken.

prompt: At the Aldous Club, the vote on a motion is reconsidered later the same day, and the motion is again before the assembly. Member Poe, who already used up his debate on it earlier that day, wants to speak again on the reconsidered question. Chair Vasquez asks whether he may. Rule.
gold: He may not, without the assembly's permission. When the reconsideration takes place on the same day as the first consideration, anyone who exhausted his right to debate in the first consideration cannot speak again on it during the reconsideration without permission of the assembly. So Poe needs leave of the assembly to speak once more.

ref.quote: If the reconsideration takes place on the same day as the first consideration, however, anyone who exhausted his right to debate in the first consideration will not be able to speak on it again during the reconsideration, without permission of the assembly. (But such a member can pursue an equivalent purpose while the motion to Reconsider is pending, since the motion proposed to be reconsidered is also open to discussion in debate on the motion to Reconsider.) 37:22 Reconsideration of a vote on a later day.

prompt: At a two-day session of the Braxton League, a vote taken yesterday is reconsidered today. Member Hale, who used all her debate on the question yesterday, wants to speak on it again during the reconsideration. Chair Ono asks whether her earlier speeches limit her now. Rule.
gold: Her earlier speeches do not limit her. Because the reconsideration takes place on a different day than the vote being reconsidered, every member's right to debate begins over again, regardless of speeches made previously. Hale may debate the reconsidered question anew under the regular limits.

ref.quote: Every member’s right to debate in the reconsideration of a question begins over again, regardless of speeches made previously, if reconsideration takes place on a day other than that on which the vote to be reconsidered was taken.

prompt: At the Kimball Society, the motion to Reconsider a debatable main motion is pending. Member Trell exhausted his debate on the main motion earlier the same day and is told he therefore cannot speak at all on the pending motion to Reconsider. Chair Beaumont asks whether that is correct. Advise.
gold: That is not correct. The right of each member to debate the motion to Reconsider is separate from the original consideration of the motion proposed to be reconsidered. So even though Trell exhausted his debate on the main motion, he still has the right to speak the regular number of times in debate on the motion to Reconsider itself.

ref.quote: The right of each member to debate the motion to Reconsider is separate from the original consideration of the motion proposed to be reconsidered.

conceptName: Need for and effect of a quorum
prompt: At the Lakeside Rowing Club, whose bylaws set a quorum well above the four members who showed up, member Dana Ruiz argues, "We four are here and willing to act, so let's just approve the $9,000 dock repair now." What is the effect of acting on this expenditure with only four members present, and why does the rule exist?
gold: With only four members present the required quorum is not met, so any substantive action such as approving the $9,000 expenditure is null and void. The quorum requirement exists to protect the organization against unrepresentative action taken in its name by an unduly small number of members. The club should not adopt the expenditure; it may only take the limited procedural steps permitted in the absence of a quorum.

ref.quote: The requirement of a quorum is a protection against totally unrepresentative action in the name of the body by an unduly small number of persons.

conceptName: Need for and effect of a quorum
prompt: The Ashford Historical Society held its regular monthly meeting at the scheduled time, but too few members attended to make a quorum, so it adjourned at once. Secretary Owen Pratt asks whether the society can be said to have "held" its required regular meeting even though no business was done. How should the parliamentarian answer?
gold: Yes. Because the meeting was convened at a regular or properly called time, the society's rule requiring the meeting to be held was satisfied, even though the absence of a quorum meant no business could be transacted and the meeting had to adjourn immediately. The lack of a quorum does not undo the fact that the meeting was validly convened.

ref.quote: But if a quorum fails to appear at a regular or properly called meeting, the inability to transact business does not detract from the fact that the society’s rules requiring the meeting to be held were complied with and the meeting was convened—even though it had to adjourn immediately.

conceptName: Need for and effect of a quorum
prompt: At the Bell County Booster Club, with no quorum present, chair Marta Vinson says, "There's an urgent grant deadline, so if there is no objection, we'll adopt the application by unanimous consent." No one objects. Is the action valid?
gold: No. The prohibition on transacting business without a quorum cannot be waived, even by unanimous consent, so adopting the grant application is invalid. A valid notice likewise cannot be given at such a meeting. If the members act anyway they do so at their own risk, and the action would need later ratification at a meeting with a quorum to have effect.

ref.quote: The prohibition against transacting business in the absence of a quorum cannot be waived even by unanimous consent, and a notice cannot be validly given.

prompt: The Fairview Choral Association has 80 enrolled members, and its bylaws say nothing about a quorum. Treasurer Lena Cho asks how many members must be present for the association to conduct business. What is the answer?
gold: Because the bylaws are silent and the enrolled membership can be accurately determined, the default quorum under the common parliamentary law is a majority of the entire membership. A majority of 80 is 41, so at least 41 members must be present to transact business. The association should consider adopting a bylaw setting a more workable figure.

ref.quote: In the absence of such a provision in a society or assembly whose real membership can be accurately determined at any time—that is, in a body having an enrolled membership composed only of persons who maintain their status as members in a prescribed manner—the quorum is a majority of the entire membership, by the common parliamentary law.

prompt: The Northgate Alliance has 200 members but rarely gets more than 30 to a meeting, and its bylaws fix the quorum at a majority of the entire membership. President Sam Ortega complains that meetings routinely fail for lack of a quorum. What do you advise?
gold: The bylaw quorum is set unrealistically high. A quorum provision should specify a number that approximates the largest attendance that can dependably be expected except under very unfavorable conditions. The Alliance should amend its bylaws to set a realistic figure so that meetings can validly transact business.

ref.quote: In an ordinary society, therefore, a provision of the bylaws should specify the number of members that shall constitute a quorum, which should approximate the largest number that can be depended on to attend any meeting except in very bad weather or other extremely unfavorable conditions.

prompt: A newly formed civic group, the Rivertown Neighbors, copied Congress and set its quorum at "a majority of the members," and now struggles to meet. Founder Priya Nair asks whether a majority quorum is the right model. How do you respond?
gold: A majority quorum is appropriate for a legislative body like Congress but is usually too large for a voluntary society, whose attendance is far lower and less predictable. Rivertown Neighbors should instead set its quorum at a number approximating the largest attendance it can dependably expect, rather than a majority of all members.

ref.quote: In both houses of Congress, the quorum is a majority of the members, by the United States Constitution. Such a quorum is appropriate in legislative bodies but too large in most voluntary societies.

conceptName: What may be done absent a quorum
prompt: At the Cedar Valley Grange, no quorum is present. Member Hal Berg insists, "With no quorum we can do absolutely nothing, so we must all just go home." Is he correct about what the assembly may do?
gold: He is not entirely correct. Although no substantive business may be transacted, even without a quorum the assembly may fix the time to which to adjourn, adjourn, recess, or take measures to obtain a quorum. So the members are not limited to simply leaving; they may, for example, recess or set an adjourned meeting.

ref.quote: Even in the absence of a quorum, the assembly may fix the time to which to adjourn , adjourn , recess , or take measures to obtain a quorum.

conceptName: What may be done absent a quorum
prompt: The Milbrook Foundation board lacks a quorum, but a contract must be approved before the next regular meeting a month away. Chair Ines Falk asks how the board can preserve its ability to act on the contract soon. What should it do?
gold: Since there is important business that cannot wait for the next regular meeting, the board should fix the time for an adjourned meeting and then adjourn. It can then take up the contract at that adjourned meeting if a quorum is present. It cannot validly approve the contract now, and any informal action taken without a quorum would be at the members' own risk.

ref.quote: If there is important business that should not be delayed until the next regular meeting, the assembly should fix the time for an adjourned meeting and then adjourn.

conceptName: What may be done absent a quorum
prompt: With no quorum present at the Harborview Association, member Guy Larsen moves "that we recess for fifteen minutes and phone the absent members to come in." Is such a motion in order despite the lack of a quorum?
gold: Yes. A motion to contact absent members during a recess is a measure to obtain a quorum, which is one of the actions expressly permitted even in the absence of a quorum. The motion is in order, and the assembly may adopt it although it cannot transact substantive business.

ref.quote: A motion that absent members be contacted during a recess would represent a measure to obtain a quorum.

conceptName: Order of business, orders of day, agenda, program
prompt: Parliamentarian Ruth Emory is asked by the Glenwood Club board to explain whether "order of business," "orders of the day," "agenda," and "program" all mean the same thing. How should she distinguish them?
gold: These terms are closely related and their meanings often coincide, but each has its own application. An order of business is the established sequence in which business is taken up; an order of the day is a particular item scheduled for a given time; and an agenda or program is a sequence of business adopted for a specific session, with a program possibly also including nonbusiness events. They are related but not simply interchangeable.
keywords: order of the day = scheduled item

ref.quote: The meaning of these terms often coincides, although each has its own applications in common usage.

conceptName: Order of business, orders of day, agenda, program
prompt: At the Summit Society, member Cal Ivey uses "the order of the day" to mean the whole list of everything the meeting will do. Chair Nadia Roy asks whether that usage is correct. What is the proper meaning?
gold: That usage is inaccurate. An order of the day is a single item of business scheduled to be taken up during a given session, day, meeting, or hour, not the entire sequence of the meeting's business. The full established sequence is the order of business; an order of the day is one scheduled item within it.

ref.quote: An order of the day is an item of business that is scheduled to be taken up during a given session, day, or meeting, or at a given hour (unless there is business having precedence over it that interferes).

conceptName: Order of business, orders of day, agenda, program
prompt: The Delegates of the Statewide Educators Convention have a meeting whose every item was fixed in sequence in advance by an adopted agenda. Chair Marcus Vane asks whether, in that meeting, the "orders of the day" and the "order of business" are two different things. How do you rule?
gold: In that meeting they are the same thing. When the only items in order have been specified and set in sequence in advance, as by an adopted agenda for a convention meeting, the orders of the day are identical with the order of business, which is itself in the form of an agenda or program.
keywords: orders of the day = order of business

ref.quote: Within a meeting in which the only items of business that are in order have been specified and set in sequence in advance—as might occur, for example, in a particular meeting of a convention—the orders of the day are identical with the order of business (which, in such a case, is in the form of an agenda or program).

prompt: At the Oak Ridge Society, which uses the standard order of business, chair Bill Hovey wants to begin the meeting with New Business "to keep energy high" and read the minutes last. Member Joan Pike objects. What is the correct sequence?
gold: The standard order of business is fixed in sequence: Reading and Approval of Minutes; Reports of Officers, Boards, and Standing Committees; Reports of Special Committees; Special Orders; Unfinished Business and General Orders; and New Business. Minutes come first and New Business last, so the chair cannot begin with New Business except by suspending the rules. Joan Pike's objection is well taken.

ref.quote: 1) Reading and Approval of Minutes 2) Reports of Officers, Boards, and Standing Committees 3) Reports of Special (Select or Ad Hoc) Committees 4) Special Orders 5) Unfinished Business and General Orders 6) New Business

prompt: At the Pinehurst Association, a matter that was made a special order and a matter of unfinished business both await consideration, and someone also wants to raise a new topic. Chair Elsa Munn asks in what order these three classes come under the standard order of business. How do you advise?
gold: Under the standard order of business, Special Orders (heading 4) are taken up before Unfinished Business and General Orders (heading 5), and New Business (heading 6) comes last. So the special order is considered first, then the unfinished business, and only afterward may the new topic be introduced under New Business.

ref.quote: 4) Special Orders 5) Unfinished Business and General Orders 6) New Business 41:6 In organizations that have adopted this book as parliamentary authority and that have not adopted a special order of business, this series of headings is the prescribed order of business for regular meetings, unless the periods intervening between consecutive regular meetings are usually more than a quarterly time interval .

prompt: New secretary Tom Ackley of the Westbrook League has been asked to list the standard order of business headings so the chair can follow them. What are the subdivisions of the standard order of business?
gold: The standard order of business comprises six headings, in this order: Reading and Approval of Minutes; Reports of Officers, Boards, and Standing Committees; Reports of Special Committees; Special Orders; Unfinished Business and General Orders; and New Business. These prescribe the sequence in which business is taken up at a regular meeting.

ref.quote: The customary or "standard" order of business comprises the following subdivisions: 1) Reading and Approval of Minutes 2) Reports of Officers, Boards, and Standing Committees 3) Reports of Special (Select or Ad Hoc) Committees 4) Special Orders 5) Unfinished Business and General Orders 6) New Business 41:6 In organizations that have adopted this book as parliamentary authority and that have not adopted a special order of business, this series of headings is the prescribed order of business for regular meetings, unless the periods intervening between consecutive regular meetings are usually more than a quarterly time interval .

prompt: The Brookside Club has adopted Robert's Rules as its parliamentary authority, meets monthly, and has never adopted its own special order of business. Chair Dee Salas asks whether the standard order of business applies to its regular meetings. What is the answer?
gold: Yes. Because the club has adopted this book as its parliamentary authority, has not adopted a special order of business, and meets more often than a quarterly interval, the standard series of headings is its prescribed order of business for regular meetings. The chair should follow that sequence.

ref.quote: In organizations that have adopted this book as parliamentary authority and that have not adopted a special order of business, this series of headings is the prescribed order of business for regular meetings, unless the periods intervening between consecutive regular meetings are usually more than a quarterly time interval .

prompt: The Coastal Preservation Trust meets only once a year, more than a quarterly interval apart, and has adopted Robert's Rules. Chair Owen Frey assumes the standard order of business automatically governs its annual meeting. Is that assumption correct?
gold: Not automatically. The standard order of business is the prescribed order for regular meetings only when meetings are not usually separated by more than a quarterly time interval. Because the Trust meets only annually, the standard order does not automatically apply, and it should adopt an agenda or its own order of business for the session.

ref.quote: In organizations that have adopted this book as parliamentary authority and that have not adopted a special order of business, this series of headings is the prescribed order of business for regular meetings, unless the periods intervening between consecutive regular meetings are usually more than a quarterly time interval .

prompt: At the Hillcrest Society, chair Gene Poll believes the standard order of business dictates how many minutes must be spent under each heading. Member Ada Voss disagrees. What does the standard order of business actually control?
gold: The standard order of business prescribes only the sequence of the headings, not the amount of time allotted to each. The time under each heading may vary from meeting to meeting. Gene Poll is mistaken; the order fixes sequence, not timing.

ref.quote: This standard order of business prescribes only the sequence of the headings, not the time to be allotted to each—which may vary with every meeting.

conceptName: Prepare a memorandum of order of business
prompt: Newly elected chair Rosa Kim of the Elmwood Association wants to prepare for her first meeting. She asks what kind of working memorandum she should have at hand and what it should list. What do you advise?
gold: She should prepare a memorandum of the complete order of business. Under the reports headings (2) and (3) it should list all known reports expected to be presented, and under Special Orders (4) and Unfinished Business and General Orders (5) it should list all matters the minutes show are due to come up, arranged in proper sequence or with any times for which they were set. This lets her move through the meeting in correct order.

ref.quote: The presiding officer may find it helpful to have at hand a memorandum of the complete order of business, listing, under headings (2) and (3) as explained below, all known reports which are expected to be presented, and under headings (4) and (5), all matters which the minutes show are due to come up, arranged in proper sequence or, where applicable, listed with the times for which they have been set.

conceptName: Prepare a memorandum of order of business
prompt: At the Fenwick Club, chair Leo Marsh thinks he alone must compile the order-of-business memorandum and has no help. He asks whether anyone may assist. Who may prepare it?
gold: The secretary can prepare the memorandum, or assist the presiding officer in preparing it. The chair need not compile it entirely on his own; because the secretary keeps the minutes showing what matters are due to come up, the secretary is well placed to prepare or help prepare the memorandum.

ref.quote: The secretary can prepare, or assist the presiding officer to prepare, such a memorandum.

conceptName: Prepare a memorandum of order of business
prompt: Chair Nita Bram of the Sunrise Society is drafting her meeting memorandum and is unsure under which headings to place matters that the minutes show are due to come up, such as a postponed motion and an item made a special order. Where do those belong?
gold: Matters that the minutes show are due to come up are listed on the memorandum under headings (4) Special Orders and (5) Unfinished Business and General Orders, arranged in proper sequence or with any set times. A postponed motion made a special order goes under Special Orders; other carried-over matters go under Unfinished Business and General Orders. Expected reports, by contrast, are listed under headings (2) and (3).

ref.quote: The presiding officer may find it helpful to have at hand a memorandum of the complete order of business, listing, under headings (2) and (3) as explained below, all known reports which are expected to be presented, and under headings (4) and (5), all matters which the minutes show are due to come up, arranged in proper sequence or, where applicable, listed with the times for which they have been set.

prompt: At the start of the Ridgeview Homeowners meeting, chair Paul Mott has called the meeting to order and reached the first heading. Copies of the minutes were not sent out in advance. What should he say and do to handle the minutes?
gold: Under the first heading he should say, "The Secretary will read the minutes," and have them read aloud, since copies were not distributed in advance. After the reading he asks whether there are any corrections, handles any by unanimous consent, and then declares the minutes approved. No formal motion or vote to approve is required.

ref.quote: The chair says, “The Secretary will read the minutes.”

prompt: Reaching the Unfinished Business heading, chair Vera Lund of the Maplewood Society announces, "Is there any unfinished business?" and waits for members to speak up. Is that the correct way to handle this heading?
gold: No. The chair should not ask an open-ended "Is there any unfinished business?" Instead she must state the question on the first item due to come up under that heading, as shown by the minutes, and after it is disposed of proceed through the remaining items in their proper order. Unfinished business is brought up by the chair from the minutes, not solicited from the floor.
keywords: do not ask if there is any unfinished business

ref.quote: He should not ask, “Is there any unfinished business?” but should state the question on the first item of business that is due to come up under this heading; and when it has been disposed of, he should proceed through the remaining subjects in their proper order.

prompt: Chair Dana Isley of the Brightwater Club has finished all unfinished business and general orders and wants to open the floor for members to raise new matters. What is the proper wording and procedure at this point?
gold: After unfinished business and general orders are disposed of, the chair asks, "Is there any new business?" Members then obtain the floor in turn to introduce new items or move to take matters from the table. So long as members are reasonably prompt in claiming the floor, the chair may not rush past legitimate business.

ref.quote: After unfinished business and general orders have been disposed of, the chair asks, “Is there any new business?”

conceptName: Taking business up out of order
prompt: At the Grandview Association, an important committee report is scheduled late in the meeting, but member Ray Sol wants it considered now because key members must leave early. Chair Amy Beck asks how the assembly may take it up out of order. What do you advise?
gold: A particular item may be taken up out of its proper order by adopting a motion to Suspend the Rules, which requires a two-thirds vote, though this is usually arranged more simply by unanimous consent. So the assembly can advance the committee report either by unanimous consent or, if anyone objects, by a two-thirds vote to suspend the rules.

ref.quote: Any particular item of business can be taken up out of its proper order by adopting a motion to Suspend the Rules by a two-thirds vote, although this is usually arranged by unanimous consent .

conceptName: Taking business up out of order
prompt: Wanting to skip ahead, member Kurt Ames of the Fairhaven Society moves "to lay all of tonight's committee reports on the table so we can get to new business." Chair Lou Diaz is unsure. Is the motion in order?
gold: No. It is not in order to lay on the table, or to postpone, an entire class of questions such as committee reports, or anything other than the question actually before the assembly. The motion to table all reports at once is improper; only the specific pending question may be laid on the table or postponed.
keywords: cannot table a class of questions

ref.quote: It is not in order to lay on the table or postpone a class of questions, like committee reports, or anything but the question that is actually before the assembly. (See 14:10-11, 17:3, 17:14.) 41:39 The chair himself cannot depart from the prescribed order of business, which only the assembly can do by at least a twothirds vote.

conceptName: Taking business up out of order
prompt: At the Cliffside Club, chair Nolan Pace announces, "On my own authority I'm moving New Business ahead of the committee reports tonight." Member Gail Ruiz questions whether the chair may reorder business unilaterally. How do you rule, and why does the rule matter?
gold: The chair cannot depart from the prescribed order of business on his own; only the assembly can do so, by at least a two-thirds vote (or by unanimous consent). This rule is an important protection for members principally involved in a question who may be unable to stay for an entire meeting. Nolan Pace's unilateral reordering is out of order unless the assembly authorizes it.

ref.quote: This is an important protection in cases where some of the members principally involved in a particular question may be unable to be present through an entire meeting.

prompt: The Woodland Society postponed a motion to 4:00 p.m. by majority vote without making it a special order. At 4:00 another motion is being debated. Member Joy Kern demands the postponed motion be taken up immediately, interrupting the debate. How do you rule?
gold: The postponed motion is only a general order, because it was postponed by a majority vote without being made a special order. A general order cannot interrupt pending business even after its set time has arrived, so it cannot cut off the current debate. It will be taken up when the pending question has been disposed of.

ref.quote: If the motion to Postpone does not make the postponed question a special order, it becomes a general order, which cannot interrupt pending business even if the time for which it is set has arrived or passed.

prompt: At the Harbor Council, members want a postponed budget motion to actually interrupt whatever is being debated when its 4:15 time arrives. Chair Iris Lang asks what is required to give it that power. What do you advise?
gold: To let the postponed motion interrupt pending business at its set time, it must be made a special order, which requires a two-thirds vote when postponed. As a special order it gains priority over general orders and the ability to interrupt the pending question when 4:15 arrives. A mere majority postponement would make it only a general order, which cannot interrupt.

ref.quote: By a two-thirds vote, however, a question can be postponed and made a special order, giving it priority over general orders as well as the ability to interrupt pending business.

prompt: The Bayview Club postponed a motion to 3:00 p.m. At 2:00 p.m., with nothing else pressing, member Dean Roe wants to take that motion up early. Chair Fern Ott asks whether that is permitted before the set time. How do you rule?
gold: A postponed question generally cannot be taken up before the time for which it is set. It can be reached early only by reconsidering the vote on the motion to Postpone, or by suspending the rules by a two-thirds vote. So the motion cannot be taken up at 2:00 unless one of those steps is taken.

ref.quote: It cannot be taken up before the time for which it is set, except by reconsidering the vote on the motion to Postpone, or by suspending the rules by a two-thirds vote.

conceptName: Nature, adoption, and change of an agenda
prompt: At the opening of the annual convention of the State Beekeepers, which has no prescribed order of business, chair Vic Hale presents a proposed agenda that includes several items set as special orders for particular hours. Member Sara Poe asks what vote is needed to adopt it. How do you rule?
gold: Because this session has no prescribed or adopted order of business, the agenda may be adopted by a majority vote, even though it contains special orders. Once adopted it becomes the order of business for that session. A two-thirds vote is not required in these circumstances.

ref.quote: At a session having no prescribed or adopted order of business, such an agenda is followed as a guide by the chair pending its formal adoption and can be adopted by majority vote, even if it contains special orders; it is then the order of business for that session.

conceptName: Nature, adoption, and change of an agenda
prompt: Midway through the Lakemont convention, after the agenda has already been adopted, delegate Omar Reyes moves to rearrange two items. Chair Tess Blum asks what vote is needed to change an already-adopted agenda. What is the answer?
gold: Once the assembly has adopted the agenda, it can be changed only by a two-thirds vote, a vote of a majority of the entire membership, or unanimous consent. A simple majority is not enough to alter an adopted agenda. So Omar Reyes's motion needs one of those higher thresholds to succeed.

ref.quote: After an agenda has been adopted by the assembly, no change can be made in it except by a two-thirds vote, a vote of a majority of the entire membership, or unanimous consent. (See also Taking Up Business out of Its Proper Order, 41:37- 39; cf. 59:59.) An affirmative vote to adopt an agenda may not be reconsidered.

conceptName: Nature, adoption, and change of an agenda
prompt: At the Northfield Association's session, a proposed agenda has been offered but not yet adopted. Member Hana Cole moves to amend it by adding an item before it is adopted. Chair Reed Vann asks what vote her amendment requires. How do you rule?
gold: While the adoption of the proposed agenda is still pending, the agenda is subject to amendment by a majority vote. So Hana Cole's amendment to add an item requires only a majority to be adopted. The higher thresholds apply only after the agenda has actually been adopted.

ref.quote: When the adoption of a proposed agenda is pending, it is subject to amendment by majority vote.

prompt: Secretary Cora Biggs of the Westland Society drafts minutes that summarize each speaker's debate remarks in detail. President Neil Ash questions whether that is proper. What should the minutes contain?
gold: In an ordinary society the minutes should record mainly what was done, not what was said. Detailed summaries of members' debate remarks do not belong in the minutes. Cora Biggs should record the motions and actions taken and omit the summaries of what individual members said.

ref.quote: In an ordinary society, the minutes should contain mainly a record of what was done at the meeting, not what was said by the members.

prompt: In the draft minutes of the Kingsley Club, secretary Blake Foss wrote, "The treasurer gave an excellent and thorough report." Chair Lena Mott asks whether that sentence belongs in the minutes. How do you advise?
gold: That sentence should be removed. The minutes must never reflect the secretary's opinion, favorable or otherwise, on anything said or done. The secretary may record that the treasurer's report was given and its disposition, but not a personal evaluation such as "excellent and thorough."

ref.quote: The minutes must never reflect the secretary’s opinion, favorable or otherwise, on anything said or done.

prompt: Recording a main motion at the Ferndale League, secretary Gus Reed asks whether he should write down both the member who moved it and the member who seconded it. What is the rule?
gold: The name of the member who made the main motion should be entered in the minutes, but the name of the seconder should not be entered unless the assembly orders it. So Gus Reed should record the maker's name and omit the seconder's name absent such an order.

ref.quote: The name of the maker of a main motion should be entered in the minutes, but the name of the seconder should not be entered unless ordered by the assembly. 2) a) When a count has been ordered, the number of votes on each side is entered, unless the vote was on a motion that would not otherwise be entered in the minutes. b) When the voting is by ballot, the full tellers' report is entered. c) When the voting is by roll call, the names of those voting on each side and those answering "present," as well as the total number in each category, are entered.

prompt: New secretary Mona Krebs of the Silverton Club is drafting the opening paragraph of the minutes and asks what basic facts it must state. What should the first paragraph contain?
gold: The first paragraph should state the kind of meeting (regular, special, adjourned regular, or adjourned special), the name of the society, and the date, time, and (if it varies) the place. It should also note that the regular chairman and secretary were present or name their substitutes, and whether the previous minutes were approved, as read or as corrected. These items need not be numbered or separated to match that list.

ref.quote: 1) the kind of meeting: regular, special, adjourned regular, or adjourned special; 2) the name of the society or assembly; 3) the date and time of the meeting, and the place, if it is not always the same; 4) the fact that the regular chairman and secretary were present or, in their absence, the names of the persons who substituted for them; and 5) whether the minutes of the previous meeting were read and approved-as read, or as corrected-and the date of that meeting if it was other than a regular business meeting.

prompt: Finishing the minutes of the Oakmont Society meeting, secretary Ivan Pell asks what the final paragraph of the minutes should contain. What is the answer?
gold: The last paragraph of the minutes should state the hour of adjournment. So Ivan Pell should close the minutes by recording the time at which the meeting adjourned.

ref.quote: The last paragraph should state: 12) the hour of adjournment.

prompt: Secretary Rae Lund of the Brookfield Association is unsure how to organize the body of the minutes when several distinct motions were considered. How should the body of the minutes be structured?
gold: The body of the minutes should contain a separate paragraph for each subject matter. For each main motion it should state the wording in which the motion was adopted or otherwise disposed of and its disposition. Rae Lund should give each distinct matter its own paragraph rather than running them together.

ref.quote: The body of the minutes should contain a separate paragraph for each subject matter

prompt: At the Crestline Society, after the minutes are read, member Hugo Vance insists, "We must have a formal motion and a vote to approve the minutes, or they aren't official." Chair Bea Toll asks whether that is required. How do you rule?
gold: A formal motion to approve the minutes is not necessary, though making one is not out of order. After the reading, the chair asks for corrections, handles any by unanimous consent, and then declares the minutes approved without any formal vote. Hugo Vance is incorrect that a motion and vote are required.

ref.quote: A formal motion to approve the minutes is not necessary, although such a motion is not out of order.

prompt: During approval of the minutes at the Ashland Club, member Tia Boyd proposes a correction, but another member protests, "You weren't even at that meeting, so you can't touch the minutes." Chair Sol Frye asks whether an absent member may take part. How do you rule?
gold: A member's absence from the meeting whose minutes are being approved does not bar the member from participating in their correction or approval. Tia Boyd may propose and speak to corrections and take part in approving the minutes despite having been absent. The protest is not well founded.

ref.quote: It should be noted that a member’s absence from the meeting for which minutes are being approved does not prevent the member from participating in their correction or approval.

prompt: The Fairmont Association board wants the minutes to routinely summarize members' debate on every question going forward. Chair Peg Innis asks what action is required to change what the minutes regularly contain. What do you advise?
gold: To change what the minutes regularly include, the association must adopt a special rule of order. A mere majority vote can only direct the inclusion of specific additional information in the minutes of a particular meeting, not permanently change the standard content. So a routine, ongoing change requires a special rule of order.
keywords: majority for a particular meeting only

ref.quote: To modify the rules governing what is regularly to be included in the minutes requires adoption of a special rule of order, although a majority vote may direct the inclusion of specific additional information in the minutes of a particular meeting.

prompt: At the Hollow Creek Society, member Jae Park begins speaking on a pending motion without waiting to be recognized, while chair Ann Cobb had not yet assigned anyone the floor. Ann asks how she should handle members who wish to speak. What is the rule?
gold: Before speaking or making a motion, a member must obtain the floor by rising, addressing the chair, and being recognized. The chair must recognize any member who seeks the floor while entitled to it. Jae Park should not have begun speaking without recognition; Ann should require members to be recognized first, then assign the floor to those entitled to it.

ref.quote: The chair must recognize any member who seeks the floor while entitled to it.

prompt: At the Devon Club, two members rise at nearly the same moment after the previous speaker sat down, and both want the floor. Chair Ken Bly asks who is entitled to be recognized. How do you rule?
gold: All other things being equal, the member who rose and addressed the chair first after the floor was yielded is entitled to recognition. A member cannot gain a prior claim by rising before the floor was actually yielded. So Ken should recognize whichever of the two first rose and addressed him after the previous speaker sat down.

ref.quote: If two or more rise at about the same time, the general rule is that, all other things being equal, the member who rose and addressed the chair first after the floor was yielded is entitled to be recognized.

prompt: After chair Rita Voss of the Marlowe Society has already recognized member A, member B jumps up claiming he had preference in recognition as the maker of the motion. What is the correct handling?
gold: A member cannot claim preference in recognition after the chair has already recognized another member, so B's claim comes too late once A was recognized. If, however, the chair actually erred by overlooking a timely preference claim, a member may raise a Point of Order and the chair must correct the error. Absent such a timely claim, member A keeps the floor.

ref.quote: A member cannot rise for the purpose of claiming preference in being recognized (as this right is called in all of the above cases) after the chair has recognized another member.

prompt: At the Glenmoor Association, while member Dana Fox is speaking in debate, member Roy Sims stands and interrupts to move to refer the question to a committee. Chair Uma Beck asks whether Roy may interrupt for that purpose. How do you rule?
gold: A member who has been assigned the floor and begun speaking generally cannot be interrupted, except for a short list of specific purposes and then only when urgency justifies it. Moving to Commit is not one of those permitted interrupting motions, so Roy is out of order. He must wait until Dana yields the floor to make his motion.

ref.quote: When a member has been assigned the floor and has begun to speak-unless he begins to discuss a subject when no motion is pending or speaks longer in debate than the rules of the assembly allow-he cannot be interrupted by another member or by the chair except for one of the following purposes, and then only when the urgency of the situation justifies it: a) a Call for the Orders of the Day when they are not being conformed to, b) the raising of a question of privilege , c) a Point of Order or the calling of the member who has the floor to order -or the chair's calling this member's attention to the fact that he is failing to observe the rules of speaking , d) a call for a separate vote on one or more of a set of independent resolutions on different subjects, or a divisible set of amendments, that have been offered by a single motion , e) a request or inquiry that requires an immediate response; or, in certain special circumstances, these additional purposes: f) an Appeal , g) an Objection to the Consideration of a Question , or h) a Division of the Assembly .

prompt: While member Cy Rand is speaking at the Trenton Club, another member properly interrupts with a point of order, which is then decided. Cy fears he has lost his place. Chair Vi Sloan asks what happens to Cy's floor. How do you rule?
gold: When a permitted interruption occurs, the speaker does not lose the floor; he simply sits while the interrupting matter is handled. Once the point of order has been disposed of, the chair directs Cy to resume, for example by saying, "Mr. Rand has the floor." Cy keeps his right to continue his speech.

ref.quote: If an interruption occurs for any of the reasons listed above, the member who had the floor does not lose it, although he takes his seat while the interrupting matter is being attended to.

prompt: As member Lia Bond rises and is assigned the floor at the Parkside Society, member Jed Cole calls out, "I move we adjourn!" and others shout "Question!" Chair Ora Penn asks how to handle this. How do you rule?
gold: It is out of order for another member to call out a motion to adjourn, or to lay the pending question on the table, when a member has risen or been assigned the floor. Jed's shouted motion and the calls of "Question!" are improper. It is the chair's duty to restore order and protect Lia's right to the floor.

ref.quote: When a member has risen to claim the floor or has been assigned the floor, it is out of order for another to call out a motion to adjourn, or a motion to lay the pending question on the table.

prompt: At the Larkspur Society, the member speaking makes a motion that is clearly out of order, and member Gwen Ash rises to interrupt with a point of order. Chair Ted Rowe asks whether interrupting the speaker with a point of order is permitted. How do you rule?
gold: Yes. Raising a Point of Order, or calling the member who has the floor to order, is one of the purposes for which a speaker may be interrupted. Because a point of order must be raised at the time of the breach, Gwen may interrupt to raise it, and the chair should attend to it before the speaker continues.

ref.quote: a Call for the Orders of the Day when they are not being conformed to, b) the raising of a question of privilege , c) a Point of Order or the calling of the member who has the floor to order -or the chair's calling this member's attention to the fact that he is failing to observe the rules of speaking , d) a call for a separate vote on one or more of a set of independent resolutions on different subjects, or a divisible set of amendments, that have been offered by a single motion , e) a request or inquiry that requires an immediate response; or, in certain special circumstances, these additional purposes: f) an Appeal , g) an Objection to the Consideration of a Question , or h) a Division of the Assembly .

prompt: During a speech at the Ember Falls Club, member Hal Voss cannot hear because of loud noise from an adjoining room and needs it addressed immediately. May he interrupt the speaker, and on what basis? Chair Nan Ely asks for a ruling.
gold: Yes. Raising a question of privilege is one of the purposes for which a member may interrupt a speaker, and it must be urgent to justify the interruption. An inability to hear because of noise is a proper question of privilege affecting the assembly, so Hal may interrupt to raise it so the chair can act.

ref.quote: a Call for the Orders of the Day when they are not being conformed to, b) the raising of a question of privilege , c) a Point of Order or the calling of the member who has the floor to order -or the chair's calling this member's attention to the fact that he is failing to observe the rules of speaking , d) a call for a separate vote on one or more of a set of independent resolutions on different subjects, or a divisible set of amendments, that have been offered by a single motion , e) a request or inquiry that requires an immediate response; or, in certain special circumstances, these additional purposes: f) an Appeal , g) an Objection to the Consideration of a Question , or h) a Division of the Assembly .

prompt: After a voice vote at the Hartwell Association, member Ida Cross doubts the chair's announced result and, as the next speaker begins, calls out "Division!" Chair Amos Reid asks whether a Division may be demanded even at such a moment. How do you rule?
gold: A Division of the Assembly is among the purposes for which a member may interrupt, so Ida's demand is permitted. It requires no second and no vote; on the demand the chair must retake the doubted vote by a rising vote. Amos should honor the Division and verify the result by having the members rise.

ref.quote: a Call for the Orders of the Day when they are not being conformed to, b) the raising of a question of privilege , c) a Point of Order or the calling of the member who has the floor to order -or the chair's calling this member's attention to the fact that he is failing to observe the rules of speaking , d) a call for a separate vote on one or more of a set of independent resolutions on different subjects, or a divisible set of amendments, that have been offered by a single motion , e) a request or inquiry that requires an immediate response; or, in certain special circumstances, these additional purposes: f) an Appeal , g) an Objection to the Consideration of a Question , or h) a Division of the Assembly .

prompt: At the Brambury Club, chair Rex Hall has just assigned member Sue Lamb the floor, but before she begins speaking, member Guy Poe rises, saying it may be his only chance to give notice of a bylaw amendment he intends to introduce. Chair Rex asks whether Guy may interrupt at that instant. How do you rule?
gold: After the floor has been assigned but before the assigned member has begun to speak, it is in order for another member to rise, if there may be no other opportunity, to give notice of intent to introduce a motion requiring such notice. So Guy may be recognized to give his previous notice before Sue starts speaking. Once she has begun, the interruption would not be allowed for this purpose.

ref.quote: giving notice of intent to introduce a motion requiring such notice ; or b) making a motion to Reconsider or to Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes .

prompt: At the Cranford Society, the floor has just been assigned to another member but she has not yet begun. Member Dot Vey rises, saying she must move to Reconsider a vote taken earlier before the chance passes. Chair Ivo Lund asks whether this interruption is in order. How do you rule?
gold: Yes. After the floor is assigned but before the member has begun speaking, it is in order for another member to rise, if there may be no other opportunity, to make a motion to Reconsider or to Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes. So Dot may be recognized to make her motion to Reconsider at that point.
keywords: or Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes

ref.quote: making a motion to Reconsider or to Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes .

prompt: While member Cal Ives is speaking at the Rosedale Club, the chair makes a ruling, and member Fay Orr wishes to interrupt to appeal it. Chair Lena Boyd asks whether an Appeal may interrupt a speaker. How do you rule?
gold: An Appeal may interrupt a speaker, but only in certain special circumstances, since it is among the additional purposes permitted beyond the routine interrupting motions. Because an appeal generally must be taken immediately after the ruling, Fay may interrupt to appeal so that it is timely. The chair should then handle the appeal before the speaker continues.

ref.quote: or, in certain special circumstances, these additional purposes: f) an Appeal , g) an Objection to the Consideration of a Question , or h) a Division of the Assembly .

prompt: At the Windham League, member Joe Tate calls out a main motion from his seat without being recognized, and chair Amy Ross starts debating it with no second. Member Kay Lund objects that the proper steps were skipped. What are the first steps for handling a motion?
gold: A main motion must be made by a member who has obtained the floor while no question is pending, must then be seconded by another member, and must be stated by the chair before it can be debated. Here Joe did not obtain the floor, there was no second, and the chair had not stated the question, so the steps were skipped. Amy should not treat it as pending; it must be properly moved, seconded, and stated first.

ref.quote: the motion must be made by a member who has obtained the floor while no question is pending (or while the motion is in order, if it is not a main motion), after which it must be seconded by another member (unless it is made by direction of a board or committee), and must be stated by the chair.

prompt: At the Thornbury Club, member Vic Doss shouts out a motion without being recognized, and chair Bea Ono is unsure whether it is now pending because no one else immediately speaks. Another member then promptly rises to claim the floor. How do you rule?
gold: Except by unanimous consent, a motion can be made only by a member who has been recognized as having the floor. Because another member promptly rose to claim the floor, unanimous consent was not given, so Vic's called-out motion must not be treated as properly made. The chair should recognize a member in the regular way before any motion is entertained.

ref.quote: Except by unanimous consent , a motion can be made only by one who has been recognized by the chair as having the floor.

prompt: Debate on a motion at the Ferngate Society appears finished, and chair Rob Kell asks, "Are you ready for the question?" After a reasonable pause no one rises. What is the next step in handling the motion?
gold: When, after a reasonable pause, no one rises to claim the floor, the chair may assume no member wishes to speak and, standing, proceed to put the question. So Rob should now put the motion to a vote, calling for the affirmative and then the negative, and afterward announce the result. He must give a genuine opportunity to speak before doing so.

ref.quote: the chair may assume that no member wishes to speak and, standing, may proceed to put the question.

conceptName: Terminology and tables for handling motions
prompt: At the Halcyon Club, member Nora Pace, recognized to debate a pending motion, finishes her remarks by moving the Previous Question. Member Tom Ash objects that she was recognized only to debate, not to make a motion. Chair Gil Rowe asks for a ruling.
gold: A member assigned the floor may use it for any proper purpose or a combination of purposes; having begun by debating, she may conclude by moving any secondary motion in order at the time, including the Previous Question. Nora's motion is therefore proper. A member recognized for one legitimate purpose has the floor for all legitimate purposes.

ref.quote: When assigned the floor, a member may use it for any proper purpose, or a combination of purposes; for example, although a member may have begun by debating a pending motion, he may conclude by moving any secondary motion, including the Previous Question , that is in order at the time.

conceptName: Terminology and tables for handling motions
prompt: At the Sterling Association, chair Van Ott states a motion and instantly bangs the gavel declaring it adopted before anyone can speak, saying that's how business moves quickly. Member Sal Ives protests. How do you rule?
gold: There is no such thing as "gaveling through" a measure under legitimate procedure. The chair cannot cut off the right to debate or to offer secondary motions by putting a question so fast that no one can get the floor. If a member sought the floor with reasonable promptness, the hasty vote must be disregarded and debate allowed. Van's action is improper.

ref.quote: It should be noted that, under legitimate parliamentary procedure, there is no such thing as “gaveling through” a measure.

conceptName: Terminology and tables for handling motions
prompt: After stating a debatable main motion at the Whitmore Society, chair Ida Nunez is unsure what standard wording to use to open the floor for debate. What may she say?
gold: After stating the question on a debatable motion, the chair may open debate by asking, "Are you ready for the question?" or, less formally, "Is there any debate?" She may also simply pause and turn toward the maker to see if he wishes to speak first. Any of these properly opens the motion to debate.
keywords: Are you ready for the question?

ref.quote: The chair may conclude his statement of the question on the debatable motion by asking, “Are you ready for the question?” or, less formally, “Is there any debate?”

conceptName: Unanimous consent and assuming a motion
prompt: At the Kentbridge Club, chair Rex Ames says, "If there is no objection, the bill will be paid," and quickly announces, "There being no objection, it is so ordered." Immediately member Dot Vane calls out, "I object." How should the chair proceed?
gold: If an objection is made with reasonable promptness, the chair must disregard his "no objection" announcement and state the question in the usual manner. Because Dot objected right away, unanimous consent was not obtained, so Rex must put the motion to pay the bill to a regular vote after any debate. The earlier announcement has no effect.

ref.quote: If an objection is made with reasonable promptness, even though the chair may have already announced the result as one of “no objection,” he must disregard such an announcement and proceed to state the question in the usual manner.

conceptName: Unanimous consent and assuming a motion
prompt: At the Rosslyn Society, a speaker's time has expired but everyone is plainly absorbed and wants him to finish his last sentence. Chair Bea Foss lets him conclude without asking for a vote. Member Cy Penn questions whether she may do that. How do you rule?
gold: Where unanimous consent is already apparent, the chair may simply assume it rather than formally requesting it. Since everyone obviously wished the speaker to finish, Bea properly allowed him to conclude by assuming consent. Assuming consent is appropriate only when agreement is genuinely evident; if it is not, she should ask, "Is there any objection?"

ref.quote: In cases where unanimous consent is already apparent, the chair may sometimes assume it.

conceptName: Unanimous consent and assuming a motion
prompt: At a sparsely attended meeting of the Ivywood League, chair Sol Rhee proposes to handle several matters by unanimous consent to save time, and member Ann Poe asks whether unanimous consent still requires a quorum. How do you rule?
gold: Yes. Action by unanimous consent requires the presence of a quorum, just as any other method of transacting business does. If no quorum is present, matters cannot be validly decided even by unanimous consent. So Sol may use unanimous consent only if a quorum is present.

ref.quote: Action by unanimous consent requires the presence of a quorum, just as for the transaction of business by any other method.

conceptName: Concept and basic procedures of debate
prompt: At the Cranview Society, a new member asks chair Lee Voss what "debate" actually means in a meeting and whether it includes any general conversation members might have. How should the chair explain it?
gold: Debate is discussion on the merits of a pending question, that is, on whether the proposal under consideration should or should not be agreed to. It is not general conversation; it is confined to argument for or against a motion that is before the assembly. The right of debate is inherent in a deliberative assembly.
keywords: should or should not be agreed to

ref.quote: In a deliberative assembly, this term applies to discussion on the merits of a pending question—that is, whether the proposal under consideration should, or should not, be agreed to.

conceptName: Concept and basic procedures of debate
prompt: At the Delmar Club, chair Ray Ott wants to move straight to a vote and tells members, "You've heard enough; no more speeches." Member Uma Poe protests that she has not yet spoken. What is the rule on the right to debate?
gold: Every member has the right to speak to every debatable motion before it is finally acted upon, and this right cannot be interfered with except by a two-thirds vote. The chair cannot simply cut off debate on his own. Uma is entitled to be recognized, and debate can be closed only by the assembly, ordinarily through the Previous Question adopted by a two-thirds vote.
keywords: cannot cut off except by two-thirds

ref.quote: every member of the assembly has the right to speak to every debatable motion before it is finally acted upon; and subject only to general limitations on debate established by parliamentary law or the rules of the body as explained below, this right cannot be interfered with except by a twothirds vote.

conceptName: Concept and basic procedures of debate
prompt: At the Fenmore Association, member Guy Sale wants a long discussion about "our parking problems" though no motion has been made. Chair Ivy Rand asks whether the assembly may debate the topic. How do you rule?
gold: Until a matter is brought before the assembly as a motion proposing a specific action, it cannot be debated. So the assembly cannot open general debate on "parking problems" with nothing pending. Guy should obtain the floor and make a specific motion, which, once seconded and stated, may then be debated.
keywords: must be brought before the assembly

ref.quote: Until a matter has been brought before the assembly in the form of a motion proposing a specific action, it cannot be debated.

prompt: At the Glenpark Society, while a motion about a fence is pending, member Hal Ord launches into a speech about last year's picnic and the club newsletter. Chair Sue Vane asks whether she may restrain him. How do you rule?
gold: In debate a member's remarks must be germane to the pending question, meaning they must bear on whether the immediately pending motion should be adopted. Hal's remarks about the picnic and newsletter are not germane to the fence motion, so the chair may require him to confine his comments to the pending question. She should call him to order if he persists.

ref.quote: In debate a member's remarks must be germane to the question before the assembly-that is, his statements must have bearing on whether the immediately pending motion should be adopted .

prompt: During debate at the Ridgeway Club, member Cal Poe says another member "is a liar who only wants this for personal gain." Chair Nan Ott must respond. How do you rule?
gold: This is out of order. In debate the measure, not the member, is the subject, and a member may never attack or question another member's motives, nor use words like "liar." The chair must act immediately and decisively to stop such personal attacks and prevent their repetition. Cal should confine his remarks to the merits of the measure.

ref.quote: The measure, not the member, is the subject of debate.

prompt: At the Marlow Association, two members turn to face each other and begin arguing back and forth directly across the room. Chair Ivo Sell asks how debate should be conducted. How do you rule?
gold: Members cannot address one another directly; all remarks must be addressed through the chair. The two members should stop speaking to each other and direct their comments to the chair. If one wishes to ask the other a question, he rises, addresses the chair, and proceeds as a Request for Information, which the other may allow or decline.

ref.quote: Members of an assembly cannot address one another directly, but must address all remarks through the chair.

prompt: At the Belmont Society, presiding officer Rita Cole feels strongly about a pending motion and wants to argue for it from the chair. Member Sam Ott questions whether she may debate while presiding. How do you rule?
gold: Although the presiding officer has the same rights in debate as any member, the impartiality required of the chair precludes her exercising them while presiding. To take part in debate she must relinquish the chair to a qualified substitute. She may then argue as a member, but she may not debate from the chair.

ref.quote: the impartiality required of the chair in an assembly precludes his exercising these rights while he is presiding.

prompt: At the Oakhurst Club, chair Ned Poe steps down, speaks in favor of a pending main motion, and then wants to immediately resume presiding while the motion is still being debated. Member Ada Voss objects. How do you rule?
gold: Having left the chair to debate, the presiding officer may not return to it until the pending main question has been disposed of, because he has shown himself to be a partisan on that matter. So Ned cannot resume presiding while the motion is still pending; the substitute continues to preside until the question is disposed of.
keywords: may not return to the chair

ref.quote: The presiding officer who relinquished the chair then may not return to it until the pending main question has been disposed of, since he has shown himself to be a partisan as far as that particular matter is concerned.

prompt: At the Fairlawn Society, member Guy Ross appeals a ruling of chair Bea Lund, and the appeal is debatable. Bea wants to speak on the appeal but wonders whether she must step down as she would to debate a main motion. How do you rule?
gold: On a debatable appeal, or a point of order the chair has submitted to the assembly, the rule against participating from the chair does not apply, and the presiding officer does not leave the chair. Because her participation relates to the function of presiding, Bea may speak on the appeal without stepping down. In fact she may speak first and again at the close of debate.

ref.quote: In debate on an appeal or a point of order that the chair has submitted to the judgment of the assembly , the foregoing rule does not apply, and the presiding officer does not leave the chair, since his participation in the debate relates to the function of presiding.

prompt: At the Stonegate Club, member Ivy Poe rises to make a motion and offers a brief sentence explaining why before stating it. Member Cal Ott objects that no discussion is allowed before a motion is pending. Chair Ruth Vane asks for a ruling.
gold: When making a motion, a member may preface it with a few words of explanation, whether the motion is debatable or not, provided this does not become a speech. Ivy's brief explanation is permissible. The chair should only intervene if the explanation expands into actual debate before the motion is stated.

ref.quote: the making of a motion of any kind -whether debatable or undebatable-may be prefaced, when necessary, by a few words of explanation, which must not become a speech; or a member can first request information, or briefly indicate the substance of a desired proposal and ask for the chair's assistance in wording an appropriate motion.

prompt: At the Redwood Association, chair Leo Sant allows brief informal discussion to help a member frame a motion, but it drifts into a lengthy back-and-forth. Member Nia Fox asks how long the chair may let this continue. How do you rule?
gold: When the chair permits informal discussion to help frame a motion, he must not allow it to continue more than a few moments, or longer than is reasonably necessary to arrive at a motion embodying the member's ideas. Leo should cut off the drifting discussion once a workable motion can be stated. It cannot be allowed to become extended debate with nothing pending.
keywords: reasonably necessary to frame a motion

ref.quote: If the chair permits such discussion, he must not allow it to continue more than a few moments or longer than is reasonably necessary to arrive at a motion embodying the member’s ideas.

prompt: At the Cliffwood Society, member Ray Ott wants to address the assembly at length on a subject without making any motion, while nothing is pending. Chair Uma Best asks whether he may simply do so. How do you rule?
gold: Generally, for a member to speak when no question is pending without promptly leading to a motion implies an unusual circumstance and requires the permission of the assembly. Ray cannot simply hold forth on his own; he needs the assembly's permission (or, in a very small body and without objection, leave to explain and then offer a motion). Otherwise he should frame and make a motion so the matter can be debated.

ref.quote: In general, for a member to speak when no question is pending, without promptly leading to a motion, implies an unusual circumstance and requires permission of the assembly.

prompt: At the Brookhaven Club, chair Ted Voss tells the members that a particular main motion "is not open to debate; we'll just vote." Member Ada Poe disagrees. Is a main motion debatable?
gold: Yes. Every main motion is debatable, from the very nature of a deliberative assembly. The chair cannot declare a main motion undebatable and move straight to a vote. Members are entitled to debate it, and debate can be closed only by the assembly, ordinarily by the Previous Question adopted by a two-thirds vote.

ref.quote: Every main motion is debatable, from the nature of the deliberative assembly itself.

prompt: At the Elmcrest Society, a member moves the Previous Question, and another member rises to argue at length against it. Chair Sy Rand asks whether the motion for the Previous Question is itself debatable. How do you rule?
gold: No. The Previous Question, like the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate, is undebatable, because its very object is to alter the debatability of pending questions and allowing debate would defeat that purpose. So the chair should not permit debate on it; he takes it to an immediate vote, requiring a two-thirds vote to adopt.
keywords: limit or extend limits of debate

ref.quote: Motions to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate and for the Previous Question are undebatable inasmuch as their very object is to alter the debatability of pending question(s), and their purpose would be defeated if they were debatable; they are also in the nature of specialized motions to suspend the rules, and any such motion made while business is pending is undebatable. 5) The motion to Lay on the Table is undebatable because its legitimate purpose would be defeated if it were debatable, and because its adoption in no way interferes with the right of the majority to take the question from the table and resume debate.

prompt: At the Hazelton Association, while a motion to Recess is pending, member Kip Voss begins arguing the merits of recessing at length. Chair Nell Ord asks whether the privileged motion to Recess is debatable. How do you rule?
gold: Privileged motions such as Recess are all undebatable, because their high privilege would let them interfere with business if debate were allowed. So the chair should not permit debate on the motion to Recess; brief factual explanation aside, it is put to an immediate vote. Kip may not make a debate speech on it.

ref.quote: The privileged motions are all undebatable because, if they were debatable, their high privilege would allow them to interfere with business.

prompt: At the Fairmont Civic League, President Dana Ruiz puts an ordinary main motion to a rising vote. The tellers report 15 votes in the affirmative, 12 in the negative, and 4 members who abstained. A member asks, "How many votes did we need, and did the motion carry?" How should President Ruiz rule?
gold: A majority vote is more than half of the votes cast, counting only members who actually voted; the 4 abstentions are excluded. With 27 votes cast, more than half is 14. The affirmative has 15, which meets that threshold, so the motion is adopted.

ref.quote: The word majority means “more than half”; and when the term majority vote is used without qualification—as in the case of the basic requirement—it means more than half of the votes cast by persons entitled to vote, excluding blanks or abstentions, at a regular or properly called meeting.

prompt: At the Del Rio Historical Society, a motion to limit debate is pending, which requires a two-thirds vote. Chair Marcus Vane takes a counted rising vote: 30 in the affirmative and 16 in the negative. He asks the parliamentarian whether the motion is adopted. What is the correct ruling?
gold: A two-thirds vote means at least two thirds of the votes cast. With 46 votes cast, two thirds is 30.67, so at least 31 affirmative votes are required. Only 30 voted in the affirmative, which is less than two thirds, so the motion is lost.

ref.quote: A two-thirds vote—when the term is unqualified—means at least two thirds of the votes cast by persons entitled to vote, excluding blanks or abstentions, at a regular or properly called meeting.

prompt: The Westside Foundation board has a fixed membership of 12, but 2 seats are currently vacant, leaving 10 directors in office. A bylaw requires "a majority of the fixed membership" to approve the annual budget. On the vote, 6 directors are in favor and 4 opposed. Treasurer Lena Park asks whether the budget is approved. How do you rule?
gold: A majority of the fixed membership is computed on the 12 authorized positions, not on the seats currently filled; vacancies do not reduce it. A majority of 12 is 7. Only 6 voted in favor, so the budget is not approved.
