ref.quote: Thus, in a board whose membership is fixed at 12, if 2 members have died and their successors have not been named, a majority of the entire membership is 6, and a majority of the fixed membership is 7.

conceptName: Two elements defining voting bases; modifying defaults
prompt: At the Ridgeline Alumni Club, a bylaw says an assessment "shall require a two-thirds vote," with nothing more. Member Omar Faye argues this means two thirds of the entire membership, not just of those voting. President Grace Yun turns to you for a ruling. What do you advise?
gold: A voting requirement has two elements: the proportion and the set of members it applies to. When the set is not stated, it always means the members present and voting. So "a two-thirds vote" here means two thirds of those actually voting, not two thirds of the entire membership. Mr. Faye's reading is incorrect.

ref.quote: Two elements enter into the definition of such bases for decision: (1) the proportion that must concur—as a majority, two thirds, three fourths, etc.; and (2) the set of members to which the proportion applies—which (a) when not stated, is always the number of members present and voting, but (b) can be specified by rule as the number of members present, the total membership, or some other grouping.

conceptName: Two elements defining voting bases; modifying defaults
prompt: The Lakeside Association has 150 members total; 30 attend a meeting and 25 vote. A bylaw requires "a majority of the entire membership" to sell association property. The vote is 25 in favor and 0 against. Chair Priya Nair asks whether the sale is approved. How should she rule?
gold: A majority of the entire membership means more than half of all 150 members, which is 76. Even a unanimous 25 affirmative votes cannot reach 76. The motion is therefore lost, no matter how one-sided the vote of those present was.

ref.quote: Assume, for example, that at a meeting of a society with a total membership of 150 and a quorum of 10, there are 30 members present, of whom 25 participate in a given counted vote (taken by rising, by show of hands, by roll call, or by ballot).

conceptName: Two elements defining voting bases; modifying defaults
prompt: At the Cedar Township Board, an ordinary main motion is pending. Chair Ben Ortiz announces, "Because this is important, I'll require a three-fourths vote to pass it." There is no bylaw or rule setting any special threshold for such motions. Member Sofia Reyes objects. How do you rule?
gold: A basis higher than a majority vote must be precisely defined in the bylaws or a special rule of order; the chair cannot impose one on his own initiative. With no such rule, this ordinary main motion requires only a majority vote. The chair's three-fourths requirement is out of order.

ref.quote: Whenever it is desired that the basis for decision be other than a majority vote or (where the normal rules of parliamentary law require it) a two-thirds vote or a vote of a majority of the entire membership, the desired basis should be precisely defined in the bylaws or in a special rule of order.

prompt: At the Harborview Guild election, three candidates run for president. On the ballot, Alvarez gets 40 votes, Chen 35, and Doyle 25. Member Ruth Kline moves that Alvarez, having the most votes, be declared elected. The bylaws say nothing about plurality. How should the chair rule?
gold: A plurality that is not a majority does not elect anyone unless a special rule previously adopted so provides. Alvarez has the largest number of votes but not a majority (which would be 51 of 100), and no such rule exists. No one is elected, and balloting must be repeated.

ref.quote: A plurality that is not a majority never chooses a proposition or elects anyone to office except by virtue of a special rule previously adopted.

prompt: During the Brookfield Society's election, no candidate reaches a majority. Member Karl Vint says, "We've always let the top vote-getter win when this happens—that's our custom." The custom is not written in the bylaws. President Nadia Coe asks whether the leading candidate can be seated. What is your ruling?
gold: A plurality can elect only by virtue of a special rule previously adopted, and to apply to the election of officers that rule must be prescribed in the bylaws. An unwritten custom is not sufficient. The leading candidate cannot be seated on a plurality; balloting must be repeated until someone obtains a majority.

ref.quote: A plurality that is not a majority never chooses a proposition or elects anyone to office except by virtue of a special rule previously adopted. If such a rule is to apply to the election of officers, it must be prescribed in the bylaws.

prompt: At the Pinecrest Club, three propositions are voted on: Plan A receives 40, Plan B 35, and Plan C 25 of 100 votes cast. Member Dee Marsh states, "Plan A has a plurality, so Plan A is adopted." Chair Leo Sant asks you to explain what a plurality is and whether Plan A is chosen. How do you respond?
gold: A plurality is simply the largest number of votes among three or more choices; Plan A does have a plurality with 40 votes. But a plurality that is not a majority does not decide a question absent a special rule. Plan A does not reach a majority (51), so it is not adopted, and further balloting is needed.

ref.quote: A plurality vote is the largest number of votes to be given any candidate or proposition when three or more choices are possible; the candidate or proposition receiving the largest number of votes has a plurality.

prompt: At the Maple Grove Association, a main motion is put to a counted vote and results in 20 in favor and 20 opposed. President Iris Bell, who is a member and has not yet voted, asks whether the tie means the motion fails or whether she must break it. What is the correct ruling?
gold: A tie vote defeats a motion requiring a majority, because a tie is not a majority; the chair is not obliged to vote. President Bell may, as a member, vote in the affirmative to make it 21-20 and adopt the motion, or she may abstain and let it fail. She is not required to break the tie.

ref.quote: On a tie vote, a motion requiring a majority vote for adoption is lost, since a tie is not a majority.

prompt: At the Sunset Rotary, a main motion is put to a counted rising vote: without the chair, there are 41 in favor and 40 opposed. Chair Paul Genn is a member and strongly opposes the motion. He asks whether he can do anything to defeat it. How do you advise him?
gold: Because his vote would affect the result, the chair may vote even though he is presiding. By voting in the negative he creates a 41-41 tie, and since a tie is not a majority the motion is then lost. He is permitted, but not obliged, to vote this way.

ref.quote: In all other cases the presiding officer, if a member of the assembly, can (but is not obliged to) vote whenever his vote will affect the result—that is, he can vote either to break or to cause a tie; or, in a case where a two-thirds vote is required, he can vote either to cause or to block the attainment of the necessary two thirds.

prompt: At the Glenwood Council, a member appeals from the ruling of Chair Tom Hale. The counted vote on sustaining the chair is 15 to 15, and the chair, a member, cast one of the votes to make it a tie. A member insists a tie means the appeal succeeds and the ruling is overturned. What is your ruling?
gold: On an appeal, a tie vote sustains the chair's decision, even where the chair's own vote created the tie, because a ruling can be reversed only by a majority against it. The 15-15 tie does not reach that majority, so the chair's decision stands and the appeal fails.

ref.quote: In an appeal from the decision of the chair, a tie vote sustains the chair’s decision, even though his vote created the tie, on the principle that the decision of the chair can be reversed only by a majority.

conceptName: Voting rights, obligations, and straw polls
prompt: At the Oakdale Association's annual meeting, member Hugo Vance is behind on his dues. He has not been dropped from the rolls and is not under any disciplinary suspension. The bylaws are silent on voting by members in arrears. Chair Ellen Frost wants to bar him from voting. Is that proper?
gold: A member who is merely in arrears, but who has not been formally dropped from the membership rolls and is not under disciplinary suspension, retains the full rights of a voting member unless the bylaws provide otherwise. Since the bylaws are silent, Mr. Vance is entitled to vote and may not be barred.

ref.quote: A member of a society who is in arrears in payment of his dues, but who has not been formally dropped from the membership rolls and is not under a disciplinary suspension, retains the full rights of a voting member and is entitled to vote except as the bylaws may otherwise provide. (See also 1:13n3, 56:19.) 45:2 One Person, One Vote.

conceptName: Voting rights, obligations, and straw polls
prompt: At the State Federation convention, delegate Maria Sol was selected as a delegate by two different constituent chapters. She asks Chair Devon Poe whether she may therefore cast two votes on a pending question. How should the chair rule?
gold: A fundamental principle of parliamentary law is that each member is entitled to one and only one vote on a question, even a person holding more than one qualifying position. As a delegate selected by two bodies, Ms. Sol may cast only a single vote. Her voting right cannot be doubled or transferred.

ref.quote: It is a fundamental principle of parliamentary law that each person who is a member of a deliberative assembly is entitled to one—and only one—vote on a question.

conceptName: Voting rights, obligations, and straw polls
prompt: During debate at the Valleyview Club, member Josh Reed moves "that we take an informal straw poll just to see where members stand" before the real vote. Chair Anita Bose asks whether that motion is in order. What do you advise?
gold: A motion for an informal straw poll is not in order, because it neither adopts nor rejects a measure and is therefore meaningless and dilatory. The chair should rule it out of order. If the club wants a nonbinding sense of the members, it may instead go into a committee of the whole or quasi committee of the whole.

ref.quote: A motion to take an informal straw poll to “test the water” is not in order because it neither adopts nor rejects a measure and hence is meaningless and dilatory.

conceptName: Abstention and voting on questions affecting oneself
prompt: At the Riverside Guild, a contentious motion is put to a vote and member Clara No sits silent, declining to vote either way. Chair Sam Ott says, "Every member present must vote; you have to pick a side." Clara looks to the parliamentarian. Is the chair correct?
gold: No. Although a member with an opinion has a duty to express it by voting, a member cannot be compelled to vote and always has the right to abstain. Clara may decline to vote, and the chair may not force her to choose a side.

ref.quote: Although it is the duty of every member who has an opinion on a question to express it by his vote, he can abstain, since he cannot be compelled to vote.

conceptName: Abstention and voting on questions affecting oneself
prompt: At the Brighton Society, a motion proposes awarding a service contract to a firm of which member Grant Ely is an officer and from which he would personally profit. Grant intends to vote in favor. President Val Cho asks how this should be handled. What is your ruling?
gold: A member should not vote on a question in which he has a direct personal or pecuniary interest not common to other members, so Mr. Ely ought to abstain on this contract. However, no member can be compelled to refrain from voting, so he cannot be forced to abstain; the proper course is to urge him to do so.

ref.quote: No member should vote on a question in which he has a direct personal or pecuniary interest not common to other members of the organization.

conceptName: Abstention and voting on questions affecting oneself
prompt: During the Cedarhurst Club election, member Tia Lund is a candidate for treasurer and casts a ballot for herself. Another member objects that she has a personal interest and must not vote for her own candidacy. Chair Roy Beck asks whether the objection is valid. How do you rule?
gold: The objection is not valid. The rule against voting on a matter of direct personal interest does not bar a member from voting for herself for an office to which members generally are eligible, or from voting when included with others in a motion. Ms. Lund may properly vote for herself.

ref.quote: The rule on abstaining from voting on a question of direct personal interest does not mean that a member should not vote for himself for an office or other position to which members generally are eligible, or should not vote when other members are included with him in a motion.

prompt: At the Northgate Club, a routine main motion needing only a majority is ready to be put. New chair Bea Frost asks whether she must use a rising vote or a ballot, or whether a simple voice vote is proper. What do you advise?
gold: A voice vote is the regular method of voting on any motion that requires no more than a majority for adoption. This routine main motion needs only a majority, so a voice vote is entirely proper; a rising vote or ballot is not required. The chair calls for the ayes, then the noes, and announces the result.

ref.quote: A vote by voice is the regular method of voting on any motion that does not require more than a majority vote for its adoption .

prompt: At the Fairhaven Society, the previous question (closing debate) is pending, which requires a two-thirds vote. Chair Neil Ash is about to take it by voice. The parliamentarian is asked whether a voice vote is the appropriate method here. What is your advice?
gold: A motion requiring a two-thirds vote is normally taken by a rising vote, not by voice, so the chair can judge whether two thirds are in the affirmative. The chair should put the previous question by a rising vote rather than a voice vote, obtaining a count if the result is in doubt.

ref.quote: The simple rising vote (in which the number of members voting on each side is not counted) is used principally in cases where a voice vote has been taken with an inconclusive result, and as the normal method of voting on motions requiring a two-thirds vote for adoption .

prompt: At a convention of the Statewide League with about 300 delegates present, Chair Owen Tate proposes to conduct all votes by show of hands "to keep things quick." A delegate questions whether that is appropriate for a body this large. How do you rule?
gold: Voting by show of hands should generally be limited to very small meetings where every member can clearly see every other member present. A 300-delegate convention is far too large for that method. The chair should use voice votes, with rising (counted if needed) for verification and for two-thirds votes, rather than a show of hands.

ref.quote: For either of these purposes, the use of voting by show of hands in assemblies should generally be limited to very small meetings where every member can clearly see every other member present.

conceptName: Verifying and counting a rising vote
prompt: At the Hillside Association, Chair Rae Munn takes a voice vote and says, "The ayes have it." Immediately, member Gus Poll calls out, "Division!" without a second and without being recognized. The chair asks whether she must honor it. What is your ruling?
gold: A single member may demand a division from the time the negative votes are cast until just after the result is announced, and it requires no second and no recognition. Mr. Poll's demand is timely, so the chair must immediately retake the vote as a rising vote. No single member can compel it to be counted, however.

ref.quote: A member can demand a division from the moment the negative votes have been cast until the result of the vote has been announced or immediately thereafter .

conceptName: Verifying and counting a rising vote
prompt: At the Ashford Club, member Dana Wynn demands a division and also insists the rising vote be counted. Chair Milo Beck is unsure whether one member can force a count. There is no special rule on the subject. How should the chair proceed?
gold: One member can require the vote to be retaken by rising, but no individual member can compel a count. To have the vote counted, a member must move that it be counted; if seconded, that question is decided by a majority vote, since no special rule lowers the requirement. The chair counts only if a majority so orders (or on his own initiative).

ref.quote: If this motion is seconded, the question as to whether a count shall be ordered is voted on by voice vote, or by an uncounted rising vote or show of hands. Where no special rule has been adopted, a majority vote is required to order a count.

conceptName: Verifying and counting a rising vote
prompt: At the Lynwood Society, a count of a rising vote has been ordered. Newly appointed teller Priya Das asks Chair Sol Reyes exactly how to conduct the count and which side is counted first. What should the chair tell her?
gold: The count is taken by having those in the affirmative rise and remain standing until counted, then having those in the negative rise and remain standing until counted; the affirmative is always counted first. In all but small assemblies the doors should be closed and no one should enter or leave while the count is taken.

ref.quote: The count is taken by having those in the affirmative rise and stand until counted, then having those in the negative rise and stand until counted.

prompt: At the Meadowbrook Society, the bylaws require officers to be elected by ballot. With only one nominee for treasurer, member Cal Rhee moves to suspend the rules and elect by voice vote to save time. Chair Gwen Ives asks whether that is permissible. How do you rule?
gold: When the bylaws require a ballot vote, that requirement cannot be suspended—even by a unanimous vote—so as to take the vote by a nonsecret method. The motion to suspend the rules is out of order, and a ballot must be taken for treasurer even though there is only one nominee.

ref.quote: When the bylaws require a vote to be taken by ballot, this requirement cannot be suspended—even by a unanimous vote—so as to take the vote by a nonsecret method.

prompt: At the Parkside Club, a question is to be decided by ballot. Because the outcome seems obvious, member Ike Long moves "that the Secretary cast one ballot on behalf of the assembly." Chair Nora Vale asks whether the motion is in order. What is your ruling?
gold: The motion is not in order. Whenever a vote is to be taken by ballot, it is not in order to move that one person, such as the secretary, cast the ballot of the assembly. Each member must vote individually, so the chair should rule the motion out of order and proceed with a proper ballot.

ref.quote: Whenever a vote is to be taken by ballot, it is not in order to move that one person—the secretary, for example—cast the ballot of the assembly.

prompt: For the Glenhaven Guild election, the secretary prepares a ballot listing the single nominee for president, Ada Voss, with "For" and "Against" boxes beside her name. Member Kent Ross asks whether this ballot form is proper for an election. How do you rule?
gold: In elections, "for" and "against" boxes may not be used; they apply only to votes on motions. The ballot is improper. To oppose Ms. Voss, a voter must vote for another eligible person or write in a name; members remain free to write in any eligible candidate even when only one is nominated.

ref.quote: In elections, “for” and “against” spaces or boxes may not be used. They are applicable only with respect to votes on motions.

prompt: At the Templeton Association ballot election, everyone present appears to have deposited a ballot. Chair Beth Kwan asks you to script exactly how she should close the polls, and whether she needs a formal motion to do so. What do you advise?
gold: The chair normally closes the polls by unanimous consent: she asks, "Have all voted who wish to do so?" and, hearing no response, states, "If no one else wishes to vote... the polls are closed." No motion is needed. A member's motion to close the polls would require a two-thirds vote, and reopening later would require a majority.

ref.quote: When everyone appears to have voted, the chair inquires, “Have all voted who wish to do so?” If there is no response, he says, “If no one else wishes to vote… [pause], the polls are closed,” thus in effect declaring the polls closed by unanimous consent.

prompt: At the Sandton Society election, the chairman of tellers finishes reading the tellers' report and then announces, "Therefore Mr. Miller is elected President." Chair Lyle Foss is unsure whether the teller may declare the winner. What is the correct ruling?
gold: The reporting teller never declares the result of a ballot vote; he reads the report and hands it to the chair without declaring the outcome. The result is always declared by the chair, who reads the report again and then declares each officer elected. The teller's announcement was improper.

ref.quote: The reporting teller never declares the result of a ballot vote. The result is always declared by the chair, who also reads the tellers’ report before he does so.

prompt: At the Rockport Club, Chair Ivan Dell is a member and forgot to submit a ballot during the election. After the polls are closed but before counting, he asks whether he may still cast his ballot. How do you rule?
gold: On a ballot, the presiding officer who is a member may vote at the same time other members do. But because he failed to vote before the polls were closed, he cannot now cast a ballot without the permission of the assembly. He may vote only if the assembly grants that permission.

ref.quote: The presiding officer, if a member of the assembly, can always vote in the case of a ballot at the time other members do. Should he fail to vote before the polls are closed, he cannot then do so without the permission of the assembly.

conceptName: Scripts for voice, rising, show of hands
prompt: At the Bayside Club, a main motion draws a loud chorus of ayes. Chair Rita Sun says, "That's clearly unanimous—motion carried," and starts to move on without calling for the noes. A member protests. Was the chair's handling correct?
gold: No. On an ordinary motion the chair must always call for the negative vote, no matter how nearly unanimous the affirmative appears. The exception is only for complimentary or courtesy motions, and even then the negative must be called if any member objects. Chair Sun should have called for the noes before announcing the result.

ref.quote: The chair must always call for the negative vote, no matter how nearly unanimous the affirmative vote may appear, except that this rule is commonly relaxed in the case of noncontroversial motions of a complimentary or courtesy nature; but even in such a case, if any member objects, the chair must call for the negative vote.

conceptName: Scripts for voice, rising, show of hands
prompt: New chair Dale Hobb of the Fernwood Society has just taken the negative vote on an ordinary main motion by voice. He asks the parliamentarian when and how he should announce the result. What should you tell him?
gold: The chair, remaining standing, announces the result immediately after putting the question, as soon as he has paused for the negative vote. For an ordinary main motion a majority in the affirmative adopts it, so he states which side has it, declares the motion adopted or lost, and, if needed, notes its effect before moving to the next business.

ref.quote: The chair, remaining standing, announces the result of the vote immediately after putting the question—that is, as soon as he has paused to permit response to his call for the negative vote. A majority vote in the affirmative adopts any motion unless it is one of the particular motions that require a larger vote under parliamentary law or the rules of the organization.

conceptName: Scripts for voice, rising, show of hands
prompt: At the Ravenna Guild, an uncounted rising vote on a motion is still too close for Chair Uma Kell to judge. She asks you for the proper wording to take a counted rising vote. What script should she use?
gold: When a rising vote is inconclusive, the chair (or the assembly by majority vote) can order it counted. She should say, for example, "Those in favor of the motion will rise and remain standing until counted... Be seated. Those opposed will rise and remain standing until counted... Be seated," counting the affirmative first, then announcing the figures and the result.
keywords: rise and remain standing until counted

ref.quote: If a rising vote remains inconclusive, the chair or the assembly can order the vote to be counted .

prompt: The Summit Association's bylaws require that officers be elected by ballot. The board wants to use handheld electronic keypads for the election. Member Joan Pyle asks whether keypads can satisfy the bylaw's ballot requirement. How do you advise?
gold: Electronic devices may fulfill a bylaw ballot requirement only if they meet the criteria for a ballot vote: members must be able to indicate their choices without revealing how they voted. If the keypads preserve that secrecy (and, for an election, allow write-ins), they may be used; if they cannot keep votes secret, they may not satisfy the ballot requirement.

ref.quote: Their use to fulfill a ballot requirement in the bylaws may be directed in the same manner, provided that the devices meet the criteria for a ballot vote as stated in 45:18. Members must be able to indicate their choices without revealing how they have voted.

prompt: At the Kingsley Club meeting, Chair Ed Marsh wants to use standalone voting machines for one particular contested vote. There is no standing rule authorizing them. Member Fay Lin asks how, if at all, the machines may be authorized. What do you advise?
gold: Use of electronic or machine voting may be directed by a special rule of order or convention standing rule, or, for a particular vote, by a motion relating to methods of voting and the polls. Since no standing rule exists, the assembly may authorize the machines for this one vote by adopting such a motion.
keywords: methods of voting and the polls

ref.quote: The use of such devices to conduct voting may be directed by a special rule of order or convention standing rule or, for a particular vote, by a motion relating to methods of voting and the polls .

prompt: The Delmar Society plans to elect several officers at once using electronic voting devices. The vendor's default setup lists only the nominated candidates and pools all races into one total. Member Guy Tran asks whether this configuration is acceptable. How do you rule?
gold: It is not acceptable. For an election, the devices must allow voters to cast write-in votes, so a list confined to nominees is insufficient. And when several offices are voted on simultaneously, the devices must be programmed so the votes cast for computing the majority are tallied independently for each office, not pooled. The setup must be corrected before use.

ref.quote: If the devices are to be used for an election, provision must be made to allow voters to cast write-in votes. If the devices are to be used to conduct voting on several questions or several independent offices simultaneously, then they must be programmed to allow the number of votes cast for purposes of computing the majority to be tallied independently for each question or office .

conceptName: Detailed committee and floor nomination rules
prompt: At the Ellsworth Society, nominations from the floor were taken at last month's meeting. At this month's meeting, where the election is actually held, Chair Ola Byrd wants to proceed straight to voting without reopening nominations. There is no bylaw or special rule on the point. Is that proper?
gold: No. Unless the bylaws or a special rule provide otherwise, the chair must call for further nominations from the floor at the session at which the election is held, even if nominations were called for at a previous session. Chair Byrd must reopen the floor for nominations before the assembly votes.

ref.quote: Unless the bylaws or a special rule of order provides otherwise, the chair must call for further nominations at the session at which the election is held even if nominations were called for at a previous session.

conceptName: Detailed committee and floor nomination rules
prompt: At the Fenwick Club, three seats on the board are to be filled from the floor. Member Hal Reece stands and nominates four people at once. Another member objects. Chair Nina Ott asks how to handle the nominations. How do you rule?
gold: In no event may a member nominate more persons than there are places to fill, so Mr. Reece cannot nominate four for three seats. Moreover, because an objection was made, no member may nominate more than one person until every member wishing to nominate has had a chance. His attempt to nominate multiple candidates at once is out of order.
keywords: no more than places to fill

ref.quote: Where more than one person is to be elected to an office, such as to a board of directors or trustees, or to a position, such as to a committee, no one may nominate more than one person for the office or position, if an objection is made, until every member wishing to nominate has had an opportunity to do so. In no event may a member nominate more persons than there are places to fill.

conceptName: Detailed committee and floor nomination rules
prompt: At the Hartwell Guild, after a pause with no further names, Chair Gene Ash declared nominations for secretary closed. Moments later, member Rose Kidd wishes to place another name in nomination. She asks whether that is still possible. What is your ruling?
gold: The chair could declare nominations closed by unanimous consent once no further names came, and a member's motion to close nominations would need a two-thirds vote. But nominations that have been closed can be reopened by a majority vote. So Ms. Kidd may move to reopen nominations, and if a majority agrees, she may then nominate her candidate.

ref.quote: After nominations have been closed, they can be reopened by a majority vote.

conceptName: Basic committee and floor nomination rules
prompt: The Woodmere Club is about to hold elections, but its bylaws say nothing about how nominations are to be made, and the club has adopted no rule on the subject. Member Sara Voss asks how the assembly should decide the method of nomination. What do you advise?
gold: The recognized methods of nomination are by the chair, from the floor, by a committee, by ballot, by mail, and by petition. Because neither the bylaws nor any adopted rule specifies a method, any member may make a motion prescribing the method of nomination, which the assembly then decides. Nominations from the floor are the common default choice.
keywords: chair, floor, committee, ballot, mail, petition

ref.quote: Methods of nomination are: (a) by the chair; (b) from the floor (sometimes called "open nominations"); (c) by a committee; (d) by ballot; (e) by mail; and (f) by petition. If no method of nominating has been specified in the bylaws and if the assembly has adopted no rule on the subject, any member can make a motion prescribing the method .

conceptName: Basic committee and floor nomination rules
prompt: At the established Thornbury Association, President Marv Doss announces that he will personally appoint the nominating committee and will serve on it ex officio. Member Cleo Ray questions this. In an organized society, is the president's plan proper?
gold: No. In an organized society, the president should neither appoint the nominating committee nor be a member of it, ex officio or otherwise. President Doss's plan is improper. The nominating committee should be elected by the organization, or, if the bylaws so provide, chosen by some means other than presidential appointment.

ref.quote: Although in organizing a new society it may be feasible for the chair to appoint the nominating committee, in an organized society the president should not appoint this committee or be a member of it—ex officio or otherwise.

conceptName: Basic committee and floor nomination rules
prompt: At the Larkspur Society, the nominating committee wishes to nominate one of its own members, Paul Nye, for vice-president. Another member protests that serving on the committee disqualifies him from being a nominee. Chair Ida Frost asks for a ruling. What do you say?
gold: The protest is unfounded. Members of the nominating committee are not barred from becoming nominees for office themselves. Barring them would penalize service on the committee and could be used to keep a member from being nominated. Mr. Nye may properly be nominated for vice-president.

ref.quote: Members of the nominating committee are not barred from becoming nominees for office themselves.

prompt: During nominations at the Crestline Club, member Vic Hale, from his seat, begins a speech attacking nominee Dr. Reed's personal character and honesty. Chair Bess Lund asks whether this speech is in order. How do you rule?
gold: Speakers must avoid making personal criticisms of nominees who are members of the organization; rather than attack a nominee, a speaker may advocate the election of a rival candidate. Mr. Hale's personal attack on Dr. Reed is out of order, and the chair should stop it and require him to confine his remarks appropriately. (A nominating or seconding speech also requires prior recognition by the chair.)

ref.quote: If candidates are members of the organization, speakers must exercise caution to avoid making any personal criticisms of them in debate. Rather than attacking a nominee, a speaker may advocate the election of a rival candidate.

prompt: At the Ashby Convention, nominating and seconding speeches are dragging on. Member Lou Pane moves "that debate on nominations be limited to two minutes per speaker." Chair Wes Gill asks what vote is required to adopt this motion. What do you advise?
gold: A motion limiting debate—including debate on nominations—may be adopted, but only by a two-thirds vote, because it restricts members' freedom of debate. So Mr. Pane's motion to limit each speaker to two minutes requires a two-thirds vote for adoption.

ref.quote: By a two-thirds vote, the assembly may adopt a motion limiting debate to any extent desired .

prompt: The Brightwater Society wants to hold a candidates' forum before its election, giving each nominee an equal, fixed amount of time to present credentials rather than allowing open-ended nominating speeches at the meeting. Member Ann Doe asks whether they can structure debate on nominations this way. What do you advise?
gold: Yes. An organization may adopt a rule specifying that debate on nominations be conducted at a different time or in a different manner. Such a rule can, for example, allot each candidate (or a designee) equal time to state credentials and argue for election. The society should adopt such a rule (a special rule of order) to govern the forum.

ref.quote: Some organizations adopt rules specifying that debate on nominations be conducted at a different time or in a different manner. Such a rule might, for example, allot each candidate (or his or her designee) equal time to state the candidate’s credentials and to argue for the candidate’s election.

prompt: The Millbrook Club's bylaws are silent on how elections are to be conducted, and it has no established custom on the point. Member Cody Faw asks how the method of voting for the upcoming election should be determined. What do you advise?
gold: Where no rule or established custom prescribes the method of voting in elections, the voting may be by any of the accepted methods. The assembly may fix the method by motion—an incidental main motion if made before the election is pending, or an incidental motion if made while it is pending. Election by ballot is generally the appropriate choice for an organized society.

ref.quote: In an assembly or organization that does not have a rule or established custom prescribing the method of voting in elections, the voting can be by any of the accepted methods.

prompt: At the Norwood Association, one office remains unfilled because no candidate has yet obtained a majority, and members are tired and want to adjourn. Chair Reba Cole asks what should happen to the unfinished election if they adjourn. What do you advise?
gold: An election should be completed at the session at which it is taken up unless that is impossible or impractical. If the assembly wishes to adjourn with the election incomplete, it should provide for an adjourned meeting to finish it. If no adjourned meeting is set and another regular session falls within a quarterly time interval, the election is taken up automatically at that next regular meeting.

ref.quote: An election should be completed at the session at which it is taken up, unless it is impossible or impractical to do so. If an assembly wishes to adjourn when an election is incomplete, an adjourned meeting should therefore be provided for.

prompt: At the Seabrook Guild, Tom Vale is present when he is elected treasurer and does not decline. Later in the same meeting, a member moves to reconsider the vote on the election. Chair Dot Kerr asks whether that motion is in order. How do you rule?
gold: The election became final immediately, because the candidate was present and did not decline. Once an election has become final in that way, it is too late to reconsider the vote on it. The motion to reconsider is therefore out of order.

ref.quote: An election to an office becomes final immediately if the candidate is present and does not decline, or if he is absent but has consented to his candidacy.

prompt: The Ferndale Society is about to take a ballot vote in the meeting room. Chair Amy Suh asks who is responsible for handing out, gathering, and tallying the ballots, and what qualities those people should have. What do you advise?
gold: The chair appoints tellers to distribute, collect, and count the ballots and to report the vote. The tellers should be chosen for accuracy and dependability, should have the confidence of the membership, and generally should not have a direct personal stake in the result to a degree that would require them to refrain from voting.

ref.quote: In balloting in a meeting where the voting is in the same room as the meeting, the chair appoints tellers to distribute, collect, and count the ballots, and to report the vote.

prompt: At the Halcyon Club, the tellers are unsure how to collect ballots so that no one votes twice. Chair Neil Ward asks you what principle governs the collection of ballots and how it should be arranged. What do you advise?
gold: In collecting the ballots, the tellers are responsible for ensuring that no member votes more than once, and the assembly should adopt some reasonable and orderly method to assure this—such as passing a receptacle with a teller and a watcher, using a monitored central ballot box, or having a teller check each ballot by feel. The method should be fixed by rule or custom, not varied haphazardly.
keywords: no member votes more than once

ref.quote: In collecting the ballots, it is the tellers’ responsibility to see that no member votes more than once—for the assurance of which the assembly should adopt some reasonable and orderly method.

prompt: At the Ironwood Association, Chair Kim Rho has already closed the polls after everyone present appeared to have voted. A latecomer, member Sal Beck, now arrives and wants to cast a ballot. He asks whether he can still vote. How do you rule?
gold: The polls have been closed, so Mr. Beck cannot simply vote. If other members arrive who wish to vote after the polls are closed, a majority vote is required to reopen them. He may move to reopen the polls, and only if a majority agrees may he then cast his ballot.

ref.quote: Thereafter, if other members arrive who wish to vote, a majority vote is required to reopen the polls .

prompt: At the Glenmoor Society, tellers collect 100 ballots. Of these, 8 are completely blank and 3 are marked so illegibly that no choice can be made out. The rest show a clear preference. Teller-chair Rae Poll asks how these should be treated in computing the majority. How do you rule?
gold: The 8 blank ballots indicate no preference and are ignored as abstentions, not counted. The 3 unintelligible ballots are illegal votes: they are counted as votes cast but credited to no one. So the number of votes cast for computing the majority is 92, and the majority needed is 47.

ref.quote: Unintelligible ballots or ballots cast for an unidentifiable or ineligible candidate are treated instead as illegal votes—that is, they are counted as votes cast but are not credited to any candidate or choice.

prompt: In the Aldergrove Club election for a single office (president), one ballot is marked with two different names. Teller Marc Ives asks Chair Deb Yoon how to record that ballot. What is the correct ruling?
gold: A ballot marked for more candidates than there are places to fill for a given office is counted as one illegal vote for that office, because the tellers cannot determine which candidate the voter prefers. It is counted as a vote cast (affecting the majority) but is credited to neither name.

ref.quote: Similarly, a ballot that contains votes for too many candidates for a given office is counted as one illegal vote cast for that office, because it is not possible for the tellers to determine which candidate(s) the voter prefers.

prompt: At the Kensington Society, three board seats are filled from a single section of the ballot listing five candidates. Teller-chair Gus Reyes asks how many votes a candidate needs to be elected and how to seat the winners. What do you advise?
gold: Every ballot with a vote in that board section for one or more candidates counts as one vote cast, and a candidate must receive a majority of that total to be elected. Those reaching a majority are declared elected in order of their vote totals until the three seats are filled. If fewer than three reach a majority, or a tie affects the remaining seats, the assembly re-ballots for the unfilled position(s).

ref.quote: In an election of members of a board or committee in which votes are cast in one section of the ballot for multiple positions on the board or committee, every ballot with a vote in that section for one or more candidates is counted as one vote cast, and a candidate must receive a majority of the total of such votes to be elected.

prompt: After a close election at the Weston Guild, the defeated candidate asks that only the winner's name be recorded and that the vote totals be kept out of the minutes to spare hard feelings. Secretary Joy Lamb asks whether she may leave the totals out. How do you rule?
gold: No. The tellers' report is entered in full in the minutes and becomes part of the official records. It may never be omitted in an election (or a critical vote) out of deference to the feelings of unsuccessful candidates or the losing side. The full report, including all vote totals, must be recorded.

ref.quote: The tellers’ report is entered in full in the minutes, becoming a part of the official records of the organization. Under no circumstances may this be omitted in an election or in a vote on a critical motion out of a mistaken deference to the feelings of unsuccessful candidates or members of the losing side.

prompt: At the Oakmont Association, the chairman of tellers wants his written report to include the number of members eligible to vote and the number who abstained, in addition to the votes for each candidate. Chair Iris Penn asks whether those figures belong in the tellers' report. How do you rule?
gold: The tellers' report does not include the number of members eligible to vote nor the number abstaining; it reports votes cast, the number necessary for election, the totals for each candidate, and illegal votes. Those extra figures should be left out. The reporting teller also never declares the result—that is done by the chair after reading the report.

ref.quote: The tellers’ report does not include the number of members eligible to vote nor the number abstaining.

prompt: A week after the Pinehurst Club's election, member Cara Voss believes the tellers miscounted and wants a recount at the next regular meeting, which is within a quarterly time interval. She asks Chair Ned Ross whether a recount is still possible and what vote is needed. What do you advise?
gold: Yes. A recount may be ordered by the voting body by a majority vote, either at the same session where the result was announced or at the next regular session if that session is held within a quarterly time interval. Because the next regular meeting falls within that interval, the club may order a recount there by a majority vote; the secretary keeps the sealed ballots until the recount period expires.

ref.quote: A recount may be ordered by the voting body, by a majority vote, at the same session at which the voting result was announced, or at the next regular session if that session is held within a quarterly time interval .

prompt: At the Cedar Ridge Garden Club, member Dana insists that the club's Tuesday gathering and the whole "February session" are the same thing. President Ruiz asks you, the club's parliamentarian, to explain in parliamentary terms the difference between a meeting and a session. How do you explain it?
gold: A meeting is a single gathering with no cessation of proceedings, while a session is the complete unit of engagement that may consist of one or more connected meetings. In an ordinary society each regular meeting normally completes a separate session, so the club's Tuesday gathering is both one meeting and, by itself, one session. They coincide here but are not the same concept.

ref.quote: each event of the members’ being assembled to transact business constitutes a separate meeting; but the complete unit of engagement in proceedings by the assembly is a session, which (in the general case covering all types of assemblies) consists of one or more connected meetings.

prompt: During a debate at the Harbor Point Homeowners Association, the members disperse for an hour-long dinner break with no adjournment declared, then reconvene. Treasurer Nkemelu asks whether the gathering before and after dinner is still a single meeting. What is your ruling?
gold: A meeting is a single official gathering with no cessation of proceedings during which members do not separate except for a short recess. An hour-long dinner break during which members leave and disperse is not merely a short recess, so it likely broke the continuity; if it was intended as a recess and members remained nearby it is still one meeting, but a true dispersal would end the meeting. The decisive test is whether proceedings ceased and members separated beyond a short recess.

ref.quote: A meeting of an assembly is a single official gathering of its members in one room or area to transact business for a length of time during which there is no cessation of proceedings and the members do not separate, unless for a short recess, as defined below.

prompt: Secretary Alvarez of the Downtown Rotary drafts minutes that use "meeting" and "session" interchangeably and asks you whether that loose usage matters. How do you advise her?
gold: In ordinary local societies a meeting and a session usually coincide, so the two terms are commonly confused in everyday speech. While the casual usage rarely causes harm in such a club, the parliamentarian should note the context, because rules keyed to a session (such as renewal of motions) turn on the precise meaning. Advise precision wherever a rule depends on the distinction.

ref.quote: Because of the fact that a meeting and a session usually coincide in ordinary local societies or branches, these two terms often tend to become confused or used interchangeably in everyday speech.

prompt: At the Lakeside Chess League, the assembly adjourns its regular meeting having set no further meeting. Vice-president Osei asks whether that adjournment also ended the session. What do you tell him?
gold: An adjournment terminates the meeting and may also end the session. Because no adjourned meeting to continue the business was set for a definite time or at the call of the chair, this adjournment ended both the meeting and the session. Any business not finally disposed of does not carry forward within that same session.

ref.quote: An adjournment (that is, the act of the assembly’s adjourning) terminates a meeting; it may also end the session.

prompt: The Fairview Debate Society votes that when it adjourns, it will adjourn to meet again next Thursday to finish the pending budget. Member Chen asks whether Thursday's meeting starts a new session. How do you rule?
gold: Because another meeting to continue the same business has been set for a definite time, the adjournment does not end the session. Thursday's gathering is an adjourned meeting that is a continuation of the same session, so pending business resumes where it left off and nothing needs to be reintroduced.

ref.quote: If another meeting to continue the same business or order of business has been set for a definite time (or to be “at the call of the chair”), the adjournment does not end the session.

prompt: At the final gavel of the state association's biennial convention, the chair of the Meridian Educators Association asks whether he should declare an ordinary adjournment or an adjournment sine die. What do you advise?
gold: Adjournment sine die, or adjournment without day, refers to the close of a session of several meetings, as in a convention for which delegates are separately chosen. Since this is the end of the biennial convention and the body will not reconvene as this same session, an adjournment sine die is appropriate. The words may be, but need not always be, expressly stated in the declaration.

ref.quote: The term adjournment sine die (or adjournment without day) usually refers to the close of a session of several meetings: (a) where the adjournment dissolves the assembly -as in a series of mass meetings or in an annual or biennial convention for which the delegates are separately chosen for each convention; or (b) where, unless called into special session, the body will not be convened again until a time prescribed by the bylaws or constitution-as in the case of a session of a legislature.

prompt: At the Prairie Land Trust, a member moves to permanently bar the organization from ever revisiting a land-purchase question by a simple majority vote at this meeting. President Whitfield asks whether such a motion is proper. What is your ruling?
gold: One session cannot place a question beyond the reach of a majority at a later session except by adopting a special rule of order or amending the bylaws, each of which requires more than a majority vote. A bare majority at this session therefore cannot bind future sessions on the land-purchase question, so the motion as framed is out of order. The freedom of each new session protects the later majority.

ref.quote: As a general rule, one session cannot place a question beyond the reach of a majority at a later session except through the process of adopting a special rule of order or an amendment to the bylaws (either of which requires more than a majority vote; see immediately below).

prompt: The Glenwood Arts Council is meeting monthly. A member moves to postpone a controversial grant "until the meeting three months from now," skipping the next two regular meetings. Chair Boateng asks whether that postponement is in order. How do you rule?
gold: It is improper to postpone anything beyond the next regular session, because doing so would improperly prevent the intervening session from considering the question. The motion to postpone the grant past the next regular meeting is therefore out of order; postponement can extend only to the next regular session (or within the current session).

ref.quote: It is improper, for example, to postpone anything beyond the next regular session—which would be an attempt to prevent that session from considering the question.

prompt: After a motion to buy new uniforms was defeated an hour ago, a member of the Riverton Marching Band Boosters moves the identical proposal again in the same meeting. Presiding officer Kaur asks whether it is in order. What do you tell her?
gold: The same or substantially the same question cannot be brought up a second time during the same session except through motions that bring a question again before the assembly, such as reconsider. Since the uniform motion was defeated earlier in this same session and no such motion is being used, the renewed motion is out of order now. It could be renewed at a later session.

ref.quote: the same or substantially the same question cannot be brought up a second time during the same session except by means of the parliamentary motions that bring a question again before the assembly.

prompt: A new board member of the Ashford Historical Society asks you what makes a gathering a "regular meeting" as opposed to some other kind. How do you explain it?
gold: A regular (or stated) meeting is the periodic business meeting of a permanent society or board held at weekly, monthly, quarterly, or similar intervals. The day should be prescribed by the bylaws and the hour and place fixed by a standing rule. Any business within the society's objects may come up at a regular meeting.

ref.quote: The term regular meeting (or stated meeting) refers to the periodic business meeting of a permanent society, local branch, or board, held at weekly, monthly, quarterly, or similar intervals, for which the day (as, "the first Tuesday of each month") should be prescribed by the bylaws and the hour and place should be fixed by a standing rule.

prompt: The president of the Westbrook Volunteer Fire Auxiliary wants to convene the membership next week, outside the regular schedule, to act on an urgent equipment purchase. Member Tanaka asks how such a meeting differs from a regular one. What do you tell her?
gold: A special (or called) meeting is a separate session held at a time different from any regular meeting, convened only to consider business specified in the call. Unlike a regular meeting, only the items named in the call may be transacted, and it must be authorized in the bylaws with proper notice. It is the vehicle for urgent business between regular meetings.

ref.quote: A special meeting (or called meeting) is a separate session of a society held at a time different from that of any regular meeting, and convened only to consider one or more items of business specified in the call of the meeting.

prompt: At the monthly-meeting Oakdale Quilters Guild, a member argues that all twelve monthly meetings in a year make up one continuing "session" so a defeated motion can't be renewed until next year. President Iversen asks whether that is right. How do you rule?
gold: Each regular meeting of such a society normally completes a separate session. Therefore the twelve monthly meetings are twelve separate sessions, not one; a motion defeated at one monthly meeting may ordinarily be renewed at the next monthly meeting. The member's premise is incorrect.

ref.quote: Each regular meeting normally completes a separate session, as explained in 8:4 .

prompt: The Summit Ridge Booster Club held its last meeting in March and its next is scheduled for June. Secretary Delgado asks whether that gap is "more than a quarterly time interval." What do you tell her?
gold: No more than a quarterly time interval elapses if the second session begins during or before the third calendar month after the month in which the first ended. March plus three months reaches June, so a June meeting is within a quarterly time interval of the March meeting. Business may therefore carry over by the ordinary means such as unfinished business or postponement.

ref.quote: it is understood that no more than a quarterly time interval intervenes between two sessions if the second session begins at any time during or before the third calendar month after the calendar month in which the first session ends.

prompt: The Belmont Investors Circle is meeting this January and wants to know how far back a prior meeting could have been while still being within a quarterly time interval. Chair Odom asks you to apply the rule. What is your answer?
gold: For a session held in January, no more than a quarterly time interval has elapsed if the previous session ended on or after October 1st of the preceding calendar year. So a prior meeting held any time from October 1 onward qualifies as within the quarterly interval. Anything ending before October 1 would exceed it.

ref.quote: with reference to a session held in January, no more than a quarterly time interval has elapsed since the previous session if that session ended on or after October 1st of the preceding calendar year; and no more than a quarterly time interval will elapse before the next session if that session will begin on or before April 30th of the current year.

prompt: The Northgate Alumni Association meets only twice a year, six months apart. At the spring meeting an unfinished item remains. President Faye asks how, if at all, that item can be preserved for the fall meeting. How do you advise?
gold: When two sessions are separated by more than a quarterly time interval, business can go over from the earlier session to the later one only by means of referral to a committee. Since the spring and fall meetings are six months apart, the item cannot simply go over as unfinished business; it must be referred to a committee that will report at the fall meeting, or else it falls to the ground.

ref.quote: business can go over from the earlier session to the later one only by means of referral to a committee .

conceptName: Business at regular vs special meetings
prompt: At a regular meeting of the Clearwater Cycling Club, a member raises a brand-new topic not on any agenda. Another member objects that only listed items may be considered. President Marsh asks you to rule. What do you say?
gold: Any business within the objects of the society as defined in its bylaws can be transacted at any regular meeting, subject to the rules on matters already acted upon. A new topic need not have appeared on an agenda to be raised at a regular meeting, so the objection is not well taken. The item is in order provided it falls within the club's objects.

ref.quote: Any business that falls within the objects of the society as defined in its bylaws (or, in the case of a board, any business within the authority of the board) can be transacted at any regular meeting (provided that the parliamentary rules relating to action already taken, or to matters not finally disposed of and remaining within the control of the assembly, are complied with in cases where they apply; compare 10:26-27; see also 35 and 38).

conceptName: Business at regular vs special meetings
prompt: The call for the Hillcrest Booster Club's special meeting named only "approval of the new scoreboard contract." At the meeting a member moves to also amend the dues. Chair Nolan asks whether that dues motion may be considered. What is your ruling?
gold: The only business that can be transacted at a special meeting is that specified in the call. Since the call named only the scoreboard contract, the motion to amend the dues is out of order at this special meeting. It would have to be taken up at a regular meeting or a properly called special meeting for that purpose.

ref.quote: The only business that can be transacted at a special meeting is that which has been specified in the call of the meeting.

conceptName: Business at regular vs special meetings
prompt: At a special meeting, the Maple Grove PTA, in the excitement of the moment, voted to hire a new fundraising firm even though that was never in the call. President Ackerman asks whether the vote is valid and what can be done. How do you advise?
gold: Action taken at a special meeting on business not mentioned in the call is invalid unless later ratified. To become valid, the hiring must be ratified by the organization at a regular meeting or at another special meeting properly called for that purpose. Until then the PTA should not rely on the action.

ref.quote: If, at a special meeting, action is taken relating to business not mentioned in the call, that action, to become valid, must be ratified by the organization at a regular meeting (or at another special meeting properly called for that purpose).

prompt: A group of members of the Talbot County Beekeepers want the president to call a special meeting, but the bylaws are silent on special meetings. President Reyes asks whether he may call one anyway. What do you tell him?
gold: Special meetings can properly be called only as authorized in the bylaws (or when authorized by the assembly for a disciplinary trial). Because the bylaws are silent, there is no authority to call a special meeting, and any business transacted at one would be invalid. The remedy is to wait for a regular meeting or amend the bylaws to authorize special meetings.

ref.quote: Special meetings can properly be called only (a) as authorized in the bylaws ; or (b) when authorized by the assembly itself, as part of formal disciplinary procedures, for purposes of conducting a trial and determining a punishment .

prompt: The bylaws of the Cornerstone Community Chorus say the president "shall call a special meeting at the written request of ten members." Ten members submit a signed request, but President Vance refuses. Member Lindqvist asks whether the president may refuse. How do you rule?
gold: When the bylaws provide that the president shall call a special meeting at the written request of a specified number of members, that duty is mandatory. With ten members having submitted the written request as required, the president has no discretion to refuse and must call the meeting. Failure to do so is a breach of duty under the bylaws.

ref.quote: he shall call a special meeting at the written request of a specific number of members; and 2) the number of days' notice required.

prompt: The Elmwood Tenants Union is drafting a bylaw authorizing special meetings and asks what the provision should contain regarding timing. Chair Bello asks for your guidance. What do you advise?
gold: A bylaw authorizing special meetings should prescribe by whom the meeting is called and the number of days' notice required, computed in the same manner as for regular meetings. So the provision must state a specific notice period, counted by calendar days excluding the meeting day but including the day notice is sent. This prevents uncertainty about proper notice.

ref.quote: the number of days' notice required. The number of days is computed in the same manner as for regular meetings .

prompt: The Coastal Preservation Trust holds just one general membership meeting per year, with a board running things in between. New member Sato asks what to call that yearly gathering. What do you tell her?
gold: Where a society holds only one business meeting of the general membership each year, leaving management to a board in the meantime, that yearly gathering is the annual meeting of the society. It is the sole regular membership meeting, so all general business of the membership occurs there.

ref.quote: Certain types of societies may hold only one business meeting of the general membership each year, perhaps leaving the management of the organization’s affairs in the meantime to a board. Such a meeting is then the annual meeting of the society.

prompt: The Fairhaven Lions Club meets monthly, and its bylaws designate the September meeting as the "annual meeting." Member Quist asks how that annual meeting differs from the other monthly meetings. What do you explain?
gold: In a society with regular meetings throughout the year, the bylaws may designate one of them as the annual meeting. The only difference is that annual reports of officers and standing committees, election of officers, and any other business the bylaws prescribe for it are in order, besides the ordinary business. Otherwise it is a regular meeting like the rest.

ref.quote: the bylaws may provide that one of these regular meetings held at a specified time each year shall be known as the annual meeting.

prompt: Secretary Ibrahim of the Grandview Book Society plans to hold the minutes of this year's annual meeting for approval until next year's annual meeting. President Cho asks whether that is correct. How do you rule?
gold: The minutes of the previous regular meeting are read and approved as usual at the annual meeting, and the minutes of the annual meeting are read and approved at the next regular meeting. Holding the annual meeting minutes a full year for action is incorrect. They should be approved at the next regular meeting, not the next annual meeting.

ref.quote: The minutes of the previous regular meeting are read and approved as usual at the annual meeting, and the minutes of the annual meeting are read and approved at the next regular meeting.

prompt: The board of the Summit Health Coalition wants to discuss a sensitive personnel matter privately. A member asks what "executive session" actually means. How do you explain it?
gold: In general parliamentary usage, an executive session is any meeting, or portion of a meeting, at which the proceedings are secret. It is the proper device for a private discussion such as a personnel matter, and it may cover an entire meeting or just part of one. Its defining feature is the secrecy of the proceedings.

ref.quote: An executive session in general parliamentary usage has come to mean any meeting of a deliberative assembly, or a portion of a meeting, at which the proceedings are secret.

prompt: During a regular meeting of the Bayview Yacht Club board, President Osborne simply announces "we're now in executive session" and continues. Member Farrell asks whether the chair can do that unilaterally. What is your ruling?
gold: A meeting enters executive session only when required by rule or established custom, or upon adoption of a motion to do so, which is a question of privilege adopted by majority vote. Absent a rule or custom, the chair cannot unilaterally declare executive session; a motion must be adopted. So the announcement alone was improper.

ref.quote: A meeting enters into executive session only when required by rule or established custom, or upon the adoption of a motion to do so.

prompt: After an executive session of the Ridgeline Foundation, a member posts the confidential discussion on social media. President Vasquez asks what consequences that member faces. How do you advise?
gold: A member who violates the secrecy of an executive session can be punished under disciplinary procedure, and any nonmember permitted to be present is honor-bound not to divulge what occurred. The member who posted the confidential discussion is subject to discipline for breaching that secrecy. The organization may proceed under its disciplinary rules.

ref.quote: A member of a society can be punished under disciplinary procedure if he violates the secrecy of an executive session. Anyone else permitted to be present is honor-bound not to divulge anything that occurred.

prompt: The Trailhead Running Club's board wants to meet by videoconference, and its bylaws expressly authorize electronic meetings. Member Kirchner asks whether votes taken online will be as valid as in-person votes. What do you tell her?
gold: An electronic meeting properly authorized in the bylaws is treated as though it were a meeting at which all participating members are actually present, provided there is opportunity for simultaneous aural communication. Because the club's bylaws expressly authorize such meetings, the videoconference votes are as valid as those taken in person. The authorization in the bylaws is the key requirement.

ref.quote: an electronic meeting that is properly authorized in the bylaws is treated as though it were a meeting at which all the members who are participating are actually present.

prompt: The Lakemont Neighborhood Association proposes to conduct its "meeting" entirely by email over several days. President Adeyemi asks whether that qualifies as a deliberative meeting. How do you rule?
gold: Regardless of technology, the opportunity for simultaneous aural communication is essential to the deliberative character of a meeting. A process conducted entirely in writing by email is not a deliberative assembly and does not constitute a valid meeting. The association cannot treat the email exchange as a meeting.

ref.quote: It is important to understand that, regardless of the technology used, the opportunity for simultaneous aural communication is essential to the deliberative character of the meeting.

prompt: The board of the Cypress Community Land Trust, whose bylaws say nothing about electronic meetings, wants to hold a telephone conference to approve a purchase. Chair Nunez asks whether business done that way would be valid. What is your ruling?
gold: Except as authorized in the bylaws, business can be validly transacted only at a regular or properly called in-person meeting. Because the bylaws do not authorize electronic meetings, a telephone conference is not a valid meeting and business approved there would be invalid. The board must either meet in person or amend the bylaws to authorize electronic meetings.
keywords: except as authorized in the bylaws

ref.quote: Except as authorized in the bylaws, the business of an organization or board can be validly transacted only at a regular or properly called meeting-that is, as defined in 8:2, a single official gathering in one room or area-of the assembly of its members at which a quorum is present.

conceptName: Five conditions making a motion out of order
prompt: At the Verdant Valley Conservancy, a member moves an action that plainly contradicts a bylaw provision. President Halloran asks whether the motion can be entertained. What is your ruling?
gold: No main motion is in order that conflicts with the corporate charter, constitution, or bylaws. Since the motion plainly contradicts a bylaw, it is out of order and the chair should rule it so. The proper route would be a motion to amend the bylaws, not a motion that conflicts with them.

ref.quote: No main motion is in order that conflicts with the corporate charter, constitution, or bylaws (although a main motion to amend them may be in order; see 35, 57); and to the extent that procedural rules applicable to the organization or assembly are prescribed by federal, state, or local law, no main motion is in order that conflicts with such rules. 2) A main motion that proposes action outside the scope of the organization's object as defined in the corporate charter, constitution, or bylaws is not in order unless the assembly by a two-thirds vote authorizes its introduction.

conceptName: Five conditions making a motion out of order
prompt: A member of the Riverside Astronomy Club moves that the club endorse a candidate for city council, which is outside the club's stated objects. President Whitaker asks whether the motion is simply dead. How do you rule?
gold: A main motion proposing action outside the scope of the organization's object is not in order unless the assembly authorizes its introduction by a two-thirds vote. So the endorsement motion is not automatically dead; the chair can put the question of whether it shall be considered, and a two-thirds vote could authorize it. Without that two-thirds authorization it cannot be introduced.

ref.quote: A main motion that proposes action outside the scope of the organization’s object as defined in the corporate charter, constitution, or bylaws is not in order unless the assembly by a two-thirds vote authorizes its introduction.

conceptName: Five conditions making a motion out of order
prompt: The Brookfield Chamber Singers adopted a rehearsal-schedule policy last month that is still in force. Tonight a member moves a conflicting schedule as a new main motion. Chair Espinoza asks whether it is in order. What do you say?
gold: Apart from a motion to rescind or amend something previously adopted, no main motion is in order that conflicts with a motion previously adopted and still in force. The conflicting new main motion is therefore out of order. The proper approach is to move to amend or rescind the existing policy.

ref.quote: no main motion is in order that conflicts with a motion previously adopted at any time and still in force.

conceptName: Alternatives when a motion is out of order
prompt: The Pinehurst Garden Society adopted a fee schedule three months ago that members now want to change, but it is far too late to reconsider. Treasurer Okafor asks what motion can be used to change it. How do you advise?
gold: An adopted main motion, even long after it is too late to reconsider, can be changed by the motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted, or it can be rescinded and a new motion introduced. So the society should use Amend Something Previously Adopted (or Rescind) to change the fee schedule. Reconsideration is not required and is unavailable now.

ref.quote: an adopted main motion, at any time before or after it is too late to reconsider it, can be changed by means of the motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted, or it can be rescinded and the desired new motion can then be introduced. 5) No main motion is in order that would conflict with or that presents substantially the same question as one which has been temporarily but not finally disposed of-whether in the same or an earlier session-and which remains within the control of the assembly .

conceptName: Alternatives when a motion is out of order
prompt: A member of the Grand Mesa Camera Club offers a main motion outside the club's objects. President Lindgren wants to know what options exist to let the assembly decide whether to take it up. What do you advise?
gold: For a motion outside the object, the chair can put to the assembly the question whether it shall be considered, or, if the chair rules it out of order, any member can move to authorize its introduction. Either way a two-thirds vote is required to permit consideration, and the question is neither debatable nor amendable. This gives the assembly a way to reach the motion if it truly wishes.

ref.quote: the chair can put to the assembly the question whether it shall be considered; or, if the chair rules the motion out of order, any member can move to authorize its introduction.

conceptName: Alternatives when a motion is out of order
prompt: Earlier in tonight's meeting the Silverton Trail Alliance adopted a motion that a member now regrets, and it is still the same session. Chair Bauer asks how the assembly can undo or change it right now. How do you advise?
gold: Because it is still the same session and within the time limit, a motion to Reconsider the vote can be made; if it is reconsidered, the motion can be voted down or amended as desired in the reconsideration. Reconsideration is the preferable procedure when it is still available. Otherwise the assembly would use Amend Something Previously Adopted or Rescind.

ref.quote: if it is reconsidered, it can be voted down or amended as desired, in the reconsideration.

prompt: At the Northshore Sailing Club, a motion was defeated earlier this meeting. A member wants to know the general rule about bringing it up again and any exceptions within the same session. How do you explain it?
gold: The basic principle is that an assembly cannot be asked to decide the same, or substantially the same, question twice during one session. The exceptions within the same session are the motion to Reconsider a vote, the motion to Rescind an action, and amending something already adopted. Absent one of these, the defeated motion cannot be renewed until a later session.

ref.quote: Renewal of motions is limited by the basic principle that an assembly cannot be asked to decide the same, or substantially the same, question twice during one session-except through a motion to reconsider a vote or a motion to rescind an action , or in connection with amending something already adopted .

prompt: Earlier tonight a member of the Highland Gardeners withdrew her motion before any vote. Now she wants to make the same motion again this meeting. Chair Delacroix asks whether the renewal restriction blocks her. What is your ruling?
gold: The renewal restrictions do not apply to a motion that was withdrawn; a withdrawn motion is as if it had never been made and can be renewed whenever it would originally be in order. So the member may make her motion again this same meeting. The earlier withdrawal is no bar.

ref.quote: The rules restricting renewal of motions do not apply to any motion that was last disposed of by being withdrawn. A motion that is withdrawn becomes as if it had never been made and can be renewed whenever it would be originally in order.

prompt: At the Cobblestone Civic League, a motion died earlier this meeting for lack of a second. A member now wants to move it again. President Amara asks whether that is permitted in the same session. How do you rule?
gold: The renewal restrictions do not apply to a motion that dies for lack of a second, since the assembly was never called upon to decide it. The member may therefore renew the motion in the same session whenever it would originally be in order. Dying for want of a second does not count as the assembly deciding the question.

ref.quote: The rules restricting renewal of motions also do not apply to any motion that dies for lack of a second.

conceptName: Notice for regular and special meetings
prompt: The Aspen Ridge Alumni Group holds regular meetings only every six months. Secretary Portman asks whether notice must be sent before each such regular meeting. What do you advise?
gold: Notice must be sent a reasonable time in advance of each regular meeting that is separated by more than a quarterly time interval from the previous one. Because these regular meetings are six months apart, exceeding a quarterly interval, the secretary must send notice (the call) before each meeting. This ensures members are informed given the long gap.

ref.quote: notice must be sent a reasonable time in advance of each regular meeting that is separated by more than a quarterly time interval from the previous regular meeting.

conceptName: Notice for regular and special meetings
prompt: The bylaws of the Meadowlark Choir require "ten days' notice." Notice was sent on the 1st for a meeting on the 11th. Secretary Ruhl asks whether that satisfies the requirement. How do you rule?
gold: Unless the bylaws provide otherwise, days are counted as all calendar days, excluding the meeting day but including the day notice is sent. Counting the 1st (day sent) through the 10th, excluding the 11th (meeting day), gives ten days. So notice sent on the 1st for an 11th meeting satisfies the ten-day requirement.
