ref.quote: Unless otherwise provided in the bylaws, the number of days is computed by counting all calendar days (including holidays and weekends), excluding the day of the meeting but including the day the notice is sent.

conceptName: Notice for regular and special meetings
prompt: The president of the Fort Hill Restoration Society sends a special-meeting notice stating only "important business will be discussed." Member Cabrera asks whether that notice is adequate. What is your ruling?
gold: Notice of a special meeting must state the time, place, and purpose, clearly and specifically describing the subject matter of the business to be brought up, sent a reasonable number of days in advance. A vague notice saying only "important business" fails to specify the subject matter and is inadequate. Any action on unstated business would be invalid unless later ratified.

ref.quote: Notice of the time, place, and purpose of the meeting, clearly and specifically describing the subject matter of the motions or items of business to be brought up, must be sent to all members a reasonable number of days in advance.

prompt: A member of the Westland Debate Circle uses "previous notice" loosely to mean any advance warning. President Sorenson asks you to clarify what previous notice technically means for adopting certain motions. How do you explain it?
gold: Previous notice has a particular technical meaning: it is announcement that a specified motion will be introduced, with its exact content, either included in the call of the meeting or announced at the preceding meeting (if within a quarterly interval). It is a condition for adopting certain motions, such as to amend the bylaws or rescind without the higher vote. It is not merely any informal advance warning.

ref.quote: The term previous notice (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.

prompt: The Crestview Association wants to amend its bylaws, which require previous notice. Secretary Villanueva asks what must actually be communicated to satisfy that requirement. How do you advise?
gold: A requirement of previous notice means announcement that the motion will be introduced, indicating its exact content, must be included in the call of the meeting where it will come up, or given at the preceding meeting if no more than a quarterly interval has elapsed. So the members must be told the exact content of the proposed amendment in advance. A general statement of intent is not enough.

ref.quote: A requirement of previous notice means that announcement that the motion will be introduced-indicating its exact content as described below -must be included in the call of the meeting at which the motion will be brought up, or, as a permissible alternative, if no more than a quarterly time interval will have elapsed since the preceding meeting, the announcement must be made at the preceding meeting.

prompt: The Glenpark Society meets monthly and wants to give previous notice of a bylaw amendment without sending a special call. Chair Beaumont asks how notice may be given. What do you advise?
gold: Because the monthly meetings are within a quarterly time interval, previous notice may be given as a permissible alternative by making the announcement, with the motion's exact content, at the preceding meeting. So a member may simply announce the amendment at this month's meeting for action next month. Alternatively it could be included in the call.

ref.quote: the announcement must be made at the preceding meeting.

prompt: A member of the Rockport Aquarium Society wants to give previous notice of a motion but will miss the preceding meeting. Secretary Han asks whether notice can be given another way. What do you advise?
gold: Instead of being announced at a meeting, previous notice can be sent to every member with the call of the meeting at which the matter will come up for action, where there is a duty or custom of issuing a call. So the absent member can give notice through the call rather than in person. This satisfies the previous-notice requirement.

ref.quote: Instead of being given at a meeting, a notice can also be sent to every member with the call of the meeting at which the matter is to come up for action, in cases where there is a duty or established custom of issuing such a call.

prompt: Member Fitzgerald of the Lakemoor Historical League wants his notice included in the next call but is unsure of the mechanics. President Osei asks you to describe the correct procedure. How do you explain it?
gold: The member desiring to give notice writes to the secretary alone, requesting that the notice be sent with the call of the next meeting, and the secretary then does so at the society's expense. So Fitzgerald simply notifies the secretary, who includes it in the call. He need not announce it to the whole membership himself.

ref.quote: the member desiring to give the notice writes to the secretary alone, requesting that the notice be sent with the call of the next meeting, and the secretary then does this at the expense of the society.

prompt: The Brightwater Club has no practice of ever sending out a call for its regular meetings. A member asks whether he can nonetheless demand his previous notice be sent with a call. Chair Ndlovu asks you to rule. What do you say?
gold: Giving notice with the call is available where there is a duty or established custom of issuing such a call. Since Brightwater has no duty or custom of sending a call for its regular meetings, notice cannot be given by this method here. The member would instead give previous notice by announcement at the preceding meeting.

ref.quote: a notice can also be sent to every member with the call of the meeting at which the matter is to come up for action, in cases where there is a duty or established custom of issuing such a call.

conceptName: Quickly find, explain, and apply rules
prompt: A newer parliamentarian for the Oakmont Council asks how RONR helps her quickly find the exact wording a member and the chair should use for a particular motion. How do you point her to the resource?
gold: For the main motion and each motion section, RONR provides a subsection headed Form and Example giving a script of the words to be spoken by the member making the motion and by the chair in stating and putting the question. So she can turn to the relevant motion's Form and Example subsection to find and apply the correct wording quickly. This is the fastest way to locate the proper script.

ref.quote: is a subsection headed Form and Example giving a script showing the words to be spoken by a member making the motion and by the chair in stating and putting the question on it.

conceptName: Quickly find, explain, and apply rules
prompt: During a heated meeting of the Fairmont Society, President Lascelles needs to rule on an unfamiliar motion fast. He asks you how the book is organized so he can locate the governing rule quickly. What do you tell him?
gold: RONR devotes a numbered section to each of the different motions of parliamentary law, so the presiding officer can go directly to that motion's section to find its rules, characteristics, and Form and Example. Knowing the book is organized motion-by-motion lets you locate and apply the governing rule quickly. This structure is the key to fast, accurate rulings.

ref.quote: in each of the succeeding sections covering the different motions of parliamentary law , is a subsection headed Form and Example giving a script showing the words to be spoken by a member making the motion and by the chair in stating and putting the question on it.

conceptName: Quickly find, explain, and apply rules
prompt: A study group led by parliamentarian Ferreira is confused by the abbreviations and notation in RONR's Form and Example scripts. A member asks why understanding the notation matters for applying the rules. How do you respond?
gold: RONR states the methods of notation it uses so that the Form and Example scripts can be read and applied correctly. Understanding that notation lets a parliamentarian interpret the scripts accurately and explain to members exactly what to say. Misreading the notation risks misapplying the rule.

ref.quote: the following methods of notation are used: - The examples generally show the presiding officer addressed as "Mr. President" or "Madam President."

conceptName: Write scripts for meetings and motions
prompt: President-elect Marsh of the Sunnyvale Toastmasters asks you to write the exact wording she and a member should use to bring a main motion before the assembly, from the member's motion through the chair stating the question. What script do you provide?
gold: The member says, "I move that [proposal]," another member says "Second," and the chair then states the question: "It is moved and seconded that [proposal]. Is there any discussion?" After debate the chair puts the question: "Those in favor say aye... those opposed say no." This mirrors RONR's Form and Example script for a main motion.

conceptName: Write scripts for meetings and motions
prompt: The new secretary of the Brookhaven Club asks you to draft the opening script the chair should use to call a regular meeting to order and move through the first items of the standard order of business. What do you write?
gold: The chair says, "The meeting will come to order," then "The Secretary will read the minutes," and after any corrections, "The minutes are approved as read [or corrected]." The chair then proceeds: "The next item of business is the reports of officers..." following the standard order of business. This tracks RONR's scripted order of business.

conceptName: Write scripts for meetings and motions
prompt: A member of the Delphi Debate Society wants a written script for moving to amend a pending motion and for the chair handling that amendment. President Achterberg asks you to supply it. What do you provide?
gold: The member says, "I move to amend by inserting [word] after [word]," and after a second the chair states: "It is moved and seconded to amend by inserting [word]. The question is on the amendment." After debate the chair puts the amendment to a vote, then returns to the main motion as amended. This follows RONR's Form and Example for the motion to Amend.

prompt: You are teaching a parliamentary-procedure workshop to the experienced board of the Fairlane Foundation. A colleague asks how Knowles's principles of adult learning should shape your approach. How do you respond?
gold: Knowles's andragogy holds that adults are self-directed, bring a reservoir of experience, are ready to learn what is relevant to their roles, are problem-centered rather than subject-centered, and are internally motivated. So the workshop should draw on the board members' own meeting experience, focus on real problems they face, and let them direct their learning. Lecturing them as if they were empty vessels would ignore these principles.

prompt: A trainee instructor with the Cascade Parliamentary Unit designs a lecture that ignores participants' backgrounds and gives no rationale for the material. Applying Knowles's principles, what feedback do you give her?
gold: Knowles's principles say adults need to know why they are learning something and that their prior experience is a resource. The lecture fails by omitting the rationale (need to know) and ignoring the learners' experience. She should explain the relevance up front and build activities that tap participants' own parliamentary experiences.

prompt: At a training review for the Northgate Educators, a member asks you to name and briefly explain Knowles's five principles of adult learning as they apply to teaching parliamentary procedure. What is your answer?
gold: The five principles are: self-concept (adults are self-directing), experience (adults bring rich experience to draw on), readiness to learn (tied to their roles and tasks), orientation to learning (problem-centered and immediately applicable), and motivation (largely internal). Applied to parliamentary teaching, lessons should be self-directed, experience-based, role-relevant, problem-focused, and appeal to intrinsic motivation.

prompt: Instructor Delaney of the Riverbend Parliamentary Academy writes the objective "students will understand motions." Applying SMART criteria, what is your critique and how would you rewrite it?
gold: "Students will understand motions" is not SMART because it is vague and not measurable. A SMART objective is Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound, for example: "By the end of the session, each participant will correctly classify five given motions as main, subsidiary, privileged, or incidental." That version specifies the behavior, is measurable, and is time-bound.

prompt: A colleague preparing a class for the Summit Chapter asks what components a complete lesson plan should contain. How do you advise?
gold: A complete lesson plan should include measurable learning objectives (ideally SMART), the content or topics, the teaching methods and activities, materials and time allocations, and a means of assessment or evaluation. Objectives drive the plan, and assessment checks whether they were met. Sequencing and timing keep the lesson on track.

prompt: The training director of the Bellhaven Unit reviews an objective: "Participants will learn a lot about voting." She asks you to make it measurable and time-bound. What do you propose?
gold: Rewrite it as a SMART objective, for instance: "By the end of the 60-minute session, participants will correctly determine the vote required (majority, two-thirds, or majority of the entire membership) for at least four out of five sample motions." This makes the objective specific, measurable, and time-bound. The original was neither measurable nor time-bound.

prompt: You are coaching a first-time instructor for the Oakhaven Society on delivering a lesson on the motion to Amend. She asks how to structure and present the session effectively. What do you advise?
gold: Open by stating the objectives and why the topic matters, present the content in a logical sequence with clear examples, and involve learners through practice such as drafting and processing amendments. Use demonstrations and check understanding as you go, then summarize and assess against the objectives. Active practice and clear examples make the lesson stick.

prompt: During a rehearsal for the Kingsley Chapter, an instructor reads slides verbatim with no learner interaction or examples. What presentation feedback do you give?
gold: Effective presentation requires engaging learners, not reading slides verbatim. Advise her to use concrete examples, ask questions, and build in hands-on practice so learners apply the material. Interaction and examples improve retention far more than passive reading.

prompt: An instructor for the Fairview Unit asks how to open and close a lesson on privileged motions so learners retain the key points. How do you advise?
gold: Open by previewing the objectives and the relevance of privileged motions, and close by summarizing the key points and checking learners' understanding against those objectives. A strong opening frames attention and a strong close reinforces retention. Tie both directly to the stated objectives.

prompt: After teaching a workshop for the Glenmoor Council, instructor Pryce wants to evaluate her own teaching. What methods do you recommend and why?
gold: Recommend gathering learner feedback (evaluations), assessing whether the learning objectives were actually met through the learners' performance, and self-reflection on what worked and what did not. Comparing results against the objectives gives an objective measure, while feedback and reflection reveal delivery issues. Together they guide improvement for the next session.

prompt: A colleague insists his class was excellent simply because he covered all his slides. How do you challenge that basis for self-evaluation?
gold: Covering the slides measures delivery of content, not learning. Effective self-evaluation asks whether the learners achieved the objectives, using assessment results and feedback rather than material covered. He should judge success by learner outcomes, not by how much he presented.

prompt: Instructor Vandermeer of the Ashcombe Unit gets consistent feedback that her pacing was too fast. How should she use that in evaluating and improving her teaching?
gold: She should treat the feedback as evidence about her delivery, confirm it against how well learners met the objectives, and adjust pacing in future lessons. Self-evaluation is a cycle: gather feedback, compare with outcomes, and revise the lesson plan. Acting on the pacing feedback is the improvement step.

conceptName: Background of the Code of Responsibility
prompt: A new member of the Tidewater parliamentarians' unit asks why a Code of Professional Responsibility exists for parliamentarians and what its purpose is. How do you explain its background?
gold: The Code of Professional Responsibility was adopted to set ethical standards governing how professional parliamentarians serve clients and the public. Its purpose is to protect the integrity of the profession and clients by prescribing duties such as competence, confidentiality, and impartiality. It provides an enforceable standard of conduct for members.

conceptName: Background of the Code of Responsibility
prompt: At a professional-development session for the Cornerstone Registered Parliamentarians, a member claims the Code is merely advisory with no teeth. Is that accurate, and what is the Code's role?
gold: That is inaccurate: the Code sets binding professional standards and includes specific items for which a member can be disciplined. Its role is to define the parliamentarian's ethical obligations and provide a basis for enforcement. It is more than advice; violations can lead to disciplinary action.

conceptName: Background of the Code of Responsibility
prompt: A client of parliamentarian Escobar asks what assurance the Code of Professional Responsibility gives about how she will be treated. How do you describe the Code's protections?
gold: The Code assures clients that the parliamentarian will act competently, maintain confidentiality, remain impartial, and avoid conflicts of interest, among other duties. It exists to protect clients and the public by holding parliamentarians to enforceable ethical standards. Breaches of these duties are subject to discipline.

prompt: Parliamentarian Okonkwo is hired to advise the chair of the Fairhaven Assembly, but a faction privately offers to pay her extra to steer a ruling their way. What is the ethical response under the parliamentarian's professional obligations?
gold: The parliamentarian must remain impartial and serve the assembly through the chair, so accepting a secret payment to steer a ruling would violate the duties of impartiality and avoiding conflicts of interest. She must refuse the offer and disclose the conflict as appropriate. Her advice must be neutral and based solely on parliamentary law.

prompt: A parliamentarian learns confidential strategy while advising the board of the Lakemont Trust, and a rival group asks him to share it. How should he respond under his ethical obligations?
gold: Confidentiality is a core professional duty, so he must not disclose the client's confidential information to the rival group. He should decline and protect the information obtained in the course of the engagement. Breaching confidentiality would be a disciplinable ethics violation.

prompt: A parliamentarian is asked by a client to advise on a matter where she lacks competence and has a personal financial interest. What does professional responsibility require her to do?
gold: Professional responsibility requires competence and disclosure of conflicts, so she should decline or not undertake work beyond her competence and must disclose her financial interest. If the conflict cannot be managed, she should not accept the engagement. Candor with the client about both limits is essential.

prompt: A member of the Westfield parliamentarians' unit asks broadly what kinds of conduct can lead to discipline under the Code of Professional Responsibility. How do you summarize the categories?
gold: Disciplinable conduct under the Code includes breaches such as failing to maintain competence, breaching confidentiality, failing to remain impartial, having undisclosed conflicts of interest, misrepresenting credentials, and otherwise engaging in dishonest or unprofessional conduct. Each enumerated item defines a specific violation. Committing any of them subjects a member to disciplinary action.

prompt: Parliamentarian Reyes advertises credentials he does not actually hold. Is that a disciplinable violation, and on what basis?
gold: Yes. Misrepresenting one's credentials or qualifications is among the enumerated disciplinable items in the Code of Professional Responsibility. Such dishonesty about qualifications is a direct ethics violation subject to discipline. Reyes could face disciplinary proceedings for it.

prompt: A parliamentarian abandons an engagement without notice, leaving the client without counsel at a critical meeting. Does the Code treat this as a disciplinable matter, and why?
gold: Yes. Neglecting or improperly abandoning a client engagement breaches the duty to serve the client competently and diligently, which is among the disciplinable items of the Code. Leaving the client without counsel at a critical moment is exactly the kind of failure the Code sanctions. It is subject to discipline.

conceptName: Develop and write a business plan
prompt: Newly certified parliamentarian Halvorsen wants to start a consulting practice and asks what a business plan for it should contain. How do you advise?
gold: A parliamentarian's business plan should describe the services offered, the target market and clients, a marketing strategy, a fee structure and financial projections, and operating logistics. It should also define goals and how success will be measured. This gives the practice direction and a basis for financial decisions.

conceptName: Develop and write a business plan
prompt: A colleague's draft business plan lists only "help clients with meetings" and no financials. What is missing and how would you strengthen it?
gold: The draft lacks a defined market, a marketing strategy, a fee structure, and financial projections. Strengthen it by specifying who the clients are, how they will be reached, what services and fees apply, and projected revenue and costs. Concrete goals and metrics should replace the vague service description.

conceptName: Develop and write a business plan
prompt: Parliamentarian Sowande asks how a business plan helps her set direction and secure any needed financing for her practice. How do you respond?
gold: A business plan clarifies her services, market, and financial projections, which lets her set measurable goals and make informed decisions. It also demonstrates viability to any lender or partner by showing realistic revenue and cost estimates. In short, it is both a roadmap and a credibility document.

prompt: Parliamentarian Ferraro is unsure how to price her services for a large convention. What factors should guide her in setting professional fees?
gold: Fees should reflect the complexity and scope of the work, the time required including preparation and travel, her experience and expertise, prevailing market rates, and the client's nature. She may charge hourly, by the day, or a flat project fee, but the basis should be agreed in advance and put in writing. Clarity prevents later disputes.

prompt: A client of parliamentarian Nakamura objects that a flat fee seems high for "just one meeting." How should the fee basis have been handled, and what do you advise?
gold: Fees should be set in advance based on total work, including preparation and travel, not just meeting hours, and communicated clearly before the engagement. The apparent objection reflects a client who was not shown what the fee covered. Advise documenting the fee basis in writing up front to avoid such misunderstandings.

prompt: A new parliamentarian asks whether to bill hourly or use a flat fee for a multi-day convention. How do you advise him to decide?
gold: The choice depends on predictability of the work: a flat or daily fee suits well-defined engagements a convention, while hourly billing fits open-ended or uncertain work. Whichever is chosen, the rate should reflect his experience and market norms and be agreed in advance in writing. Match the billing method to the scope and risk.

prompt: Parliamentarian Osborne finishes a convention engagement and asks what a proper invoice to the client should include. How do you advise?
gold: An invoice should identify the parliamentarian and client, the dates and description of services rendered, the fee basis and amount (itemizing hours or days and any expenses), the total due, and payment terms. Clear itemization tied to the agreed fee prevents disputes. It should match what was agreed in the letter of agreement.

prompt: A client disputes an invoice from parliamentarian Kaur because it shows only a lump sum with no detail. What was done wrong and how should invoices be prepared?
gold: A lump-sum invoice with no detail invites disputes. Invoices should itemize the services, dates, hours or days, expenses, and the total tied to the agreed fee. Kaur should reissue an itemized invoice consistent with the letter of agreement so the client can see what is being charged.

prompt: Parliamentarian Beltran incurred travel and printing costs during an engagement. How should these appear on the invoice, and on what basis can they be billed?
gold: Reimbursable expenses such as travel and printing should be listed separately on the invoice, itemized and, where possible, supported by receipts, and billed only if the agreement provided for their reimbursement. They are distinct from the professional fee. Billing expenses not covered by the agreement is improper.

prompt: Parliamentarian Whitfield is retained to advise the Crestline Association at its convention and asks whether an oral understanding is enough or whether she needs a letter of agreement. How do you advise?
gold: She should use a written letter of agreement rather than rely on an oral understanding. It should state the scope of services, fees and expenses, dates, and other terms so both parties share the same expectations. A written agreement protects both parliamentarian and client and prevents later disputes.

prompt: A colleague asks what essential terms a parliamentarian's letter of agreement or contract should contain. How do you answer?
gold: It should specify the services to be provided, the fee and how expenses are handled, the dates and duration, cancellation or termination terms, and confidentiality if relevant. Signatures of both parties make it binding. These terms define expectations and reduce the chance of disputes.

prompt: Parliamentarian Adebayo's client wants to cancel the engagement a week before the convention. Their letter of agreement had no cancellation clause. What does this reveal about the agreement, and what do you advise going forward?
gold: The absence of a cancellation clause leaves the parties without agreed terms for this situation, which is exactly why such clauses matter. Advise Adebayo to negotiate a fair resolution now and, going forward, to include cancellation and termination terms in every letter of agreement. Clear contract terms prevent this uncertainty.

prompt: Parliamentarian Sinclair is beginning a long-term relationship advising the board of the Marisol Foundation. She asks for guidance on how best to work with a client to be effective. How do you advise?
gold: She should clarify the scope and expectations up front, confer with the presiding officer before meetings to anticipate problems, keep her role advisory and confidential, and communicate clearly and impartially. Building trust with the client while preserving neutrality is key. Preparation and clear communication make her advice most useful.

prompt: A parliamentarian complains that he is constantly interrupting meetings to correct the chair, causing friction with his client. What advice do you give about working with the presiding officer?
gold: He should confer with the presiding officer before the meetings and during recesses to anticipate problems and avoid frequent, conspicuous consultation during the meeting. Preparing with the chair in advance reduces the need for interruptions and preserves the client relationship. Advice during the meeting should be given as inconspicuously as possible.

prompt: A new parliamentarian asks how to handle a client who wants her to take sides on the substance of a contested motion. How do you advise her?
gold: She should explain that her role is advisory and impartial, limited to procedure, and that she cannot take sides on the merits of a motion. Maintaining neutrality protects both her professional standing and the client's process. She can advise on how to proceed procedurally without advocating an outcome.

prompt: During a meeting of the Lakeshore Civic Association, a member loudly refuses to comply with the chair's legitimate order to be seated. President Marchetti asks you whether the member is obligated to obey. What is your ruling?
gold: All persons present at a meeting have an obligation to obey the legitimate orders of the presiding officer. The member is therefore obligated to comply, though he may appeal the chair's decision or move to suspend the rules if he disagrees. Simply refusing a legitimate order is a breach of order.

ref.quote: All persons present at a meeting have an obligation to obey the legitimate orders of the presiding officer.

prompt: A member becomes disruptive at the Brightwater Council meeting and Chair Delacroix is tempted to shout him down with the gavel. She asks you how she should conduct herself. What do you advise?
gold: In dealing with any case of disorder, the presiding officer should always maintain a calm, deliberate tone and must not try to drown out a disorderly member with voice or gavel or be drawn into a verbal duel. Advise the chair to stay composed and, if necessary, invoke proper disciplinary procedures. Escalating with the gavel is exactly what to avoid.

ref.quote: In dealing with any case of disorder in a meeting, the presiding officer should always maintain a calm, deliberate tone-although he may become increasingly firm if a situation demands it.

prompt: At the Fairmont Association meeting, a person refuses to obey a lawful order to leave the hall. President Okoro asks what he, as chair, may do to enforce the order. How do you advise?
gold: When a person refuses to obey a lawful order to leave, the chair should take necessary measures to enforce it, guided by a judicious appraisal of the situation. He may appoint a committee to escort the offender out, ask the sergeant-at-arms to do so, or if needed have the person removed by police. Force should be a last resort given possible legal consequences.

ref.quote: the chair should take necessary measures to see that the order is enforced, but should be guided by a judicious appraisal of the situation.

prompt: At a meeting of the Northwind Society, the parliamentarian starts announcing rulings directly to the assembly. President Vega asks you what the parliamentarian's proper role is during the meeting. What do you say?
gold: During a meeting the parliamentarian's work should be limited to giving advice to the chair and, when requested, to any other member. The parliamentarian does not make rulings to the assembly; only the chair rules. So announcing rulings directly to the members is improper the parliamentarian advises, the chair decides.

ref.quote: During a meeting the work of the parliamentarian should be limited to giving advice to the chair and, when requested, to any other member.

prompt: Parliamentarian Coles advises the chair of the Elmhurst Assembly one way, but President Tanaka rules the opposite. A member protests that the chair must follow the parliamentarian. How do you rule?
gold: After the parliamentarian expresses an opinion, the chair has the duty to make the final ruling and may either follow or disregard that advice. So the chair was entirely within her authority to rule contrary to the parliamentarian. The member's claim that the chair must follow the parliamentarian is incorrect.

ref.quote: After the parliamentarian has expressed an opinion on a point, the chair has the duty to make the final ruling and, in doing so, has the right to follow the advice of the parliamentarian or to disregard it.

prompt: Before a convention of the Highpoint Federation, the organizers ask where the parliamentarian should be seated relative to the presiding officer, and why. What do you advise?
gold: The parliamentarian should be seated next to the chair so as to be convenient for consultation in a low voice. This placement lets the chair confer quietly and promptly without disrupting the meeting. The chair should still avoid checking with the parliamentarian too frequently or too obviously.

ref.quote: The parliamentarian should be assigned a seat next to the chair, so as to be convenient for consultation in a low voice, but the chair should try to avoid checking with the parliamentarian too frequently or too obviously.

prompt: A member of the Rosewood Society uses "table" to mean "postpone" and "meeting" to mean "session" during debate, causing confusion. President Iqbal asks why using precise parliamentary terminology matters. How do you respond?
gold: Standard parliamentary terms have distinct, defined meanings, so using "table" for "postpone" or "meeting" for "session" invites confusion and misapplication of the rules. As parliamentarian, you should employ and encourage the correct standard terminology so members and the chair understand exactly what is being proposed. Precise terms are essential to applying the rules correctly.

prompt: In minutes for the Glenaire Club, the secretary records that the assembly "adjourned" when it actually took a short recess. Parliamentarian Boone is asked whether the wording matters. How do you advise?
gold: The terms recess, adjournment, and adjournment sine die have distinct parliamentary meanings, so recording a short recess as an "adjournment" is inaccurate and could mislead about whether the meeting or session ended. Advise the secretary to use the precise standard term that matches what actually occurred. Consistent, correct terminology keeps the record accurate.

prompt: A presiding officer of the Marlowe Assembly keeps saying "the motion passes" and "unanimous consent" loosely. Parliamentarian Frost is asked why consistent standard terminology should be used throughout. What do you say?
gold: Using standard terminology consistently such as saying a motion is "adopted" and correctly distinguishing "unanimous consent" ensures everyone understands the exact parliamentary effect of what is happening. Loose or inconsistent terms create ambiguity in both the meeting and the record. The parliamentarian should model and encourage the correct terms throughout.

prompt: The Lakeside Camera Club's bylaws contain an article on amendment but it only says amendments "may be adopted at any regular meeting." It states no notice requirement and no vote threshold. At a meeting, President Alvarez is asked what is actually required to amend the bylaws. What is your ruling?
gold: Because the bylaws neglect to specify complete requirements, RONR's default applies: a bylaw amendment requires previous notice and a two-thirds vote, or as an alternative the vote of a majority of the entire membership. A bare majority of those present is not sufficient. President Alvarez should rule that both notice and the two-thirds (or majority-of-entire-membership) threshold govern.

ref.quote: they should always include at least notice and a two-thirds vote, which (with a vote of a majority of the entire membership as an allowable alternative) are the requirements for its adoption if such specification in the bylaws is neglected

prompt: At the Fairview Historical Society, member Boyd moves to amend the bylaws and insists that since the bylaws are silent on the process, a simple majority at any meeting suffices. Secretary Nunez disagrees. As parliamentarian, rule on the requirement.
gold: The silence of the bylaws does not lower the bar; it invokes RONR's default. Amendment requires previous notice plus a two-thirds vote, or alternatively a majority of the entire membership. Boyd is incorrect that a simple majority of those present will do.

ref.quote: they should always include at least notice and a two-thirds vote, which (with a vote of a majority of the entire membership as an allowable alternative) are the requirements for its adoption if such specification in the bylaws is neglected

prompt: The Delta Rowing Association has 300 members; typically 40 attend meetings. Its bylaws are silent on amendment procedure. At tonight's meeting, with no prior notice given, member Okafor wants to amend the bylaws by a vote of a majority of the entire membership. Chair Prentice asks whether that is possible tonight. Rule.
gold: A majority of the entire membership (here 151) is an allowable alternative to notice-plus-two-thirds when the bylaws neglect to specify requirements. So an amendment could be adopted tonight without notice only if at least 151 members vote for it; with only 40 present that is impossible, so notice and a two-thirds vote would be the practical route.

ref.quote: which (with a vote of a majority of the entire membership as an allowable alternative) are the requirements for its adoption if such specification in the bylaws is neglected

prompt: At the Riverton Garden Club annual meeting, three separate bylaw amendments have been noticed, all changing the dues article to different amounts, and they conflict so that not all can take effect. Chair Ibarra asks how to handle them. Rule.
gold: All noticed amendments are entitled to consideration, so none is dropped merely for conflicting. The chair should arrange the conflicting amendments in a logical order, generally taking the least inclusive first and the most inclusive last, so the last one adopted is the one given effect. The assembly may rearrange that order by majority vote.

ref.quote: the chair should arrange them in a logical order, much as in the case of filling blanks , generally taking the least inclusive amendment first and the most inclusive last so that the last one adopted is given effect.

prompt: At the Hillcrest Alumni Association, an amendment to Article V was just adopted. Member Faye now objects that a second, noticed amendment to the same article should be dropped because it conflicts with what the assembly just passed. Chair Downs asks for a ruling.
gold: The second amendment is not dropped simply because it conflicts with one just adopted. All bylaw amendments of which notice was given are entitled to be considered as a right of their proposers. Once the first amendment passed it became part of the bylaws, so the second is now considered against the amended language.
keywords: becomes part of the bylaws immediately

ref.quote: all bylaw amendments of which notice was given are entitled to be considered, as a matter of the rights of their proposers, and a bylaw amendment is not dropped simply because it would conflict with one previously adopted.

prompt: Member Lund argues that once the Westbrook Choir Society adopts one bylaw amendment, the ordinary rule barring a later conflicting main motion should block any competing noticed amendment. President Marsh asks whether that ordinary rule applies here. Rule.
gold: The ordinary rule that a conflicting main motion is out of order after one is adopted does not apply to bylaw amendments. Because several notices representing different approaches may have been given, all noticed amendments are entitled to be considered. Lund's objection is not well taken.

ref.quote: The rule that, when a main motion is adopted, no other conflicting main motion is thereafter in order is not applicable to the motion to amend the bylaws, since several notices of proposals representing different approaches to the same problem may have been given, and all such bylaw amendments are entitled to be considered .

prompt: The Oakmont Booster Club wants to abolish the office of Historian and delete every reference to it scattered through several bylaw sections. Member Tran asks whether each deletion must be a separate motion. Rule.
gold: Because these isolated changes are all needed to achieve one end (abolishing the office and removing its references), they should be offered in a single motion rather than separately. If the changes are so related that all must be made together for the bylaws to be coherent, the motion cannot even be divided.

ref.quote: When a series of isolated changes to the bylaws are needed to achieve one end-such as abolishing the office of "Historian" and eliminating all references to it-the changes should be offered in a single motion.

prompt: After the Cedar Valley Land Trust adopts a bylaw amendment on a show of hands that looked close, Secretary Pham asks whether she must record anything beyond "adopted." Rule.
gold: The final vote on a bylaw amendment should be counted and recorded in the minutes unless it was nearly unanimous. Since this vote looked close rather than near-unanimous, the count should be taken and entered in the minutes.

ref.quote: The final vote on a bylaw amendment should be counted and recorded in the minutes unless it is nearly unanimous.

prompt: At the Northgate Toastmasters, member Ruiz wants to change a single word in one bylaw sentence. Chair Beck asks what kind of motion this is and how it is treated. Rule.
gold: A single isolated change to the bylaws is treated as an ordinary motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted, subject to the special rules for bylaw amendment (notice and the required vote). It need not be bundled with anything else.

ref.quote: If only an isolated change is to be made in the bylaws, it can be treated as any motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted , subject to the particular rules indicated immediately above.

prompt: The Summit Cycling Club noticed an amendment to raise annual dues from $10 to $25. At the meeting, member Quill moves to amend that amendment to make the dues $40 instead. Chair Adeyemi asks whether Quill's amendment is in order. Rule.
gold: Quill's amendment is out of order. When notice is required, no amendment to a bylaw amendment may increase the modification beyond the scope of the notice; $40 exceeds the noticed change to $25. An amendment lowering the figure to somewhere between $10 and $25 would be in order, but going above $25 is not, even by unanimous consent.

ref.quote: no amendment to a bylaw amendment is in order that increases the modification of the article or provision to be amended ).

prompt: At the Brookdale Association, a noticed amendment would change dues from $10 to $25. Member Sol moves to change the pending $25 figure down to $18. Another member moves to change it to $8. Chair Vasquez must rule on each. Rule.
gold: Changing $25 to $18 is in order because $18 lies between the existing $10 and the noticed $25, diminishing the change. Changing it to $8 is out of order, even with unanimous consent, because $8 is below $10 and thus exceeds the scope of the notice.

ref.quote: an amendment to change the 25 to any number between 10 and 25 would be in order, but an amendment to change the number to less than 10 or greater than 25 would not be in order, even with unanimous consent.

prompt: Member Ellery asks Chair Hobbs of the Pinewood Society whether amending a pending bylaw amendment itself requires its own notice and a two-thirds vote. Rule.
gold: Amendments to a pending bylaw amendment can be adopted by a majority vote without separate notice. They are not free-form, however: they are restricted to changes within the scope of the original notice and may not increase the extent of the proposed change.

ref.quote: can be adopted by a majority vote without notice, they are subject to restrictions on the extent of the changes they propose.

conceptName: Write isolated amendments in recommended form
prompt: At the Maple Ridge Association, member Cortes wants to give notice of a bylaw amendment but simply says "I think we should change the meeting month." Chair Diaz asks whether that is proper notice, and who may give it. Rule.
gold: Notice must be formally worded, stating the exact change (for example, "to amend Article IV, Section 2, by striking out 'March' and inserting 'April'"). Cortes's vague statement is inadequate. When the bylaws place no limit on who may give notice, any member is entitled to do so.

ref.quote: Notice of a bylaw amendment should be formally worded in a form such as "To amend Article IV, Section 2, by striking out 'March' and inserting 'April' after the words 'second Tuesday in.'" When the bylaws do not place a limitation on those who can give notice of a bylaw amendment, any member is entitled to do so.

conceptName: Write isolated amendments in recommended form
prompt: A drafting committee of the Glenwood Foundation debates whether to reword a bylaw for smoother reading, though the plainer version is slightly ambiguous. Chair Bellamy asks which should prevail. Rule.
gold: In bylaws, indisputability of meaning and application outranks readability, and readability must be sacrificed when both cannot be achieved. The committee should keep the precise, unambiguous wording even if it reads less smoothly.

ref.quote: Indisputability of meaning and application is a more important consideration than "readability," and the latter must be sacrificed when both cannot be achieved.

conceptName: Write isolated amendments in recommended form
prompt: The bylaws committee of the Ashford Club wants to send notice using parallel columns showing the current and proposed text. Member Yates asks whether that satisfies the notice requirement. Rule.
gold: Parallel columns showing the existing bylaw and the proposed text are a good device, provided the exact amendment, stated formally, is set out at the top across both columns. So long as the notice fairly informs members of the changes contemplated, this format is proper.

ref.quote: The notice should fairly inform the members of the changes contemplated. Showing the existing bylaw and the bylaw with the proposed changes in parallel columns is a good device so long as the exact amendment, stated in a formal manner, is set out at the top across both columns.

prompt: At the Trinity Service League, a bylaw amendment is pending and members want it not to take effect until after the annual meeting closes. Chair Odom asks how that delay can be arranged and what vote it needs. Rule.
gold: While the amendment is pending, a motion may be made to amend the enacting words by adding a proviso (e.g., "provided that this amendment shall not go into effect until after the close of this annual meeting"), or an incidental motion may set a later effective date. Either method requires only a majority vote.

ref.quote: While the amendment is pending, a motion can be made to amend the enacting words of the motion to amend by adding a clause such as this: "… with the proviso that [or, "… provided, however, that"] this amendment shall not go into effect until after the close of this annual meeting." Or, while the amendment is pending, an incidental motion can be adopted that, in the event of the amendment's adoption, it shall not take effect until a specified time. Either method requires only a majority vote.

prompt: The bylaws revision of the Harborview Club includes captions and article numbers. Member Fenn assumes the secretary can freely edit those afterward as mere notations. Chair Salas asks you to rule.
gold: That old practice is outdated. Captions, headings, and article or section numbers are now included as an integral part of what the assembly adopts, not as mere marginal notations the secretary can rewrite at will. Fenn is mistaken.
keywords: integral part of what is adopted

ref.quote: It is now the usual practice to include these subtitles or identifying numbers or letters as an integral part of what is adopted by action of the assembly.

prompt: After the Elmwood Society adopts a revision, the secretary wants to reword a heading in a way that would subtly change its meaning. Member Grady objects. Chair Lowe asks for a ruling.
gold: Only the assembly can amend captions or headings where the change could affect meaning, and that authority may not be delegated. The secretary may make purely technical corrections to numbering or cross-references that cannot change meaning, but not this substantive rewording.
keywords: only the assembly can amend headings

ref.quote: Only the assembly can amend captions or headings under the rules applicable to bylaws or other papers if such change could have any effect on meaning, and this authority may not be delegated.

prompt: The Parkside Association's revision committee wants to write the one-time transition steps directly into the permanent bylaws. Member Ito asks whether that is advisable. Rule.
gold: It is a mistake to encumber the bylaws themselves with provisions that have effect for only a limited time. Transitional steps should be handled as provisos, not embedded permanently in the bylaws.

ref.quote: It is a mistake to encumber the bylaws themselves with provisions which have effect for only a limited time.

prompt: The Kingsley Club faces complicated transition mechanics for its bylaws revision. Chair Reyes asks how to provide for them temporarily without cluttering the revision itself. Rule.
gold: The temporary transition provisions should be numbered and attached to the revision draft on a separate sheet headed "Provisos Relating to Transition." The revision is then moved for adoption together with the provisos attached, keeping the permanent bylaws free of temporary matter.

ref.quote: such provisions can be numbered and attached to the revision draft on a separate sheet headed "Provisos Relating to Transition." The motion to adopt the revision can then be made in this form: "I move the adoption of the revised bylaws with the provisos attached thereto."

prompt: The Vernon Society adopts a bylaw amendment but by proviso delays its operation for six months. Member Cho asks whether, in the meantime, the amendment is "really" part of the bylaws. Rule.
gold: Yes. Although the assembly can delay when a bylaw amendment operates, the amendment becomes part of the bylaws immediately upon adoption. If the bylaws are reprinted, a footnote should note the language is not yet in effect.

ref.quote: although the time when a bylaw amendment takes effect can be delayed by the assembly, the amendment becomes part of the bylaws immediately upon adoption.

conceptName: Draft a special rule of order
prompt: The Bayview Society adopts RONR as its parliamentary authority, then adopts a special rule of order limiting each speech to three minutes, which conflicts with RONR's ten-minute default. Member Hale asks which controls. Rule.
gold: The special rule of order controls. Special rules of order supersede any rules in the parliamentary authority with which they conflict, so the club's three-minute limit governs over RONR's default.

ref.quote: Special rules of order supersede any rules in the parliamentary authority with which they may conflict.

conceptName: Draft a special rule of order
prompt: The Crestline Club wants to permanently limit debate speeches. Member Ojeda asks whether this belongs in a special rule of order and whether such rules are commonly needed. Rule.
gold: A rule relating to the length or number of speeches permitted each member in debate is one of the notable cases where a special rule of order is often found necessary. Adopting it as a special rule of order is the correct vehicle.

ref.quote: A rule relating to the length or number of speeches permitted each member in debate is often found necessary. - A society with a small assembly-such as one having a dozen or fewer members-may wish to adopt a rule that its meetings will be governed by some or all of the somewhat less formal procedures applicable to small boards .

conceptName: Draft a special rule of order
prompt: Secretary Rankin of the Dunmore League asks how a special rule of order should be moved, and how it should look when later printed in the club's rules. Rule.
gold: Special rules of order are usually adopted in the form of resolutions. When they are later printed, the enacting words such as "Resolved, That" are normally dropped.

ref.quote: Special rules of order are usually adopted in the form of resolutions , but when they are printed, the enacting words (such as "Resolved, That") are normally dropped.

prompt: The Aldergrove Society has only ten members at its meetings and finds full formality cumbersome. President Wren asks whether the club may formally adopt the less formal small-board procedures for its meetings. Advise the chair.
gold: Yes. A society with a small assembly, such as a dozen or fewer members, may adopt a special rule of order providing that its meetings will be governed by some or all of the less formal procedures applicable to small boards. The chair should advise adopting such a special rule of order.

ref.quote: A society with a small assembly-such as one having a dozen or fewer members-may wish to adopt a rule that its meetings will be governed by some or all of the somewhat less formal procedures applicable to small boards .

prompt: President Vance of the Merefield Club is unsure whether a proposed special rule of order should be introduced as a plain motion or as a resolution, and how it will appear once printed. Advise the chair.
gold: Advise that special rules of order are usually adopted in the form of resolutions. The chair should have it moved as a resolution; when it is later printed among the club's rules, the enacting words are normally dropped.

ref.quote: Special rules of order are usually adopted in the form of resolutions , but when they are printed, the enacting words (such as "Resolved, That") are normally dropped.

prompt: The Foxglove Guild wants its meetings to follow a customized order of business different from RONR's standard order. President Amos asks how to make that binding. Advise the chair.
gold: Advise the chair that it is sometimes desirable to adopt a special rule of order establishing the society's own order of business. Adopting such a special rule of order makes the customized order binding.

ref.quote: It is sometimes desirable to adopt a rule establishing the society's own order of business . - A rule relating to the length or number of speeches permitted each member in debate is often found necessary. - A society with a small assembly-such as one having a dozen or fewer members-may wish to adopt a rule that its meetings will be governed by some or all of the somewhat less formal procedures applicable to small boards .

prompt: The Southport Club is split over what a bylaw means. Member Kerr claims a court or the national office must decide; another says the club decides but can just vote to "reinterpret" a clear provision. Chair Voss asks you to rule.
gold: Each society decides for itself the meaning of its own bylaws, so the club, not an outside body, resolves the question. But where the meaning is clear, the society cannot change that meaning even by unanimous vote except by amending the bylaws. Interpretation arises only when there is a genuine ambiguity.

ref.quote: Each society decides for itself the meaning of its bylaws. When the meaning is clear, however, the society, even by a unanimous vote, cannot change that meaning except by amending its bylaws.

prompt: The Harrowgate Society's bylaws generally allow any adult resident to be elected to membership by a two-thirds vote, but a specific section caps membership at 200. With 200 members, member Blum insists a two-thirds vote can still elect a 201st. Chair Neal asks for a ruling.
gold: No. A general statement yields to a specific one, so the specific 200-member cap controls over the general provision on electing residents. The society cannot elect a 201st member even by a two-thirds vote.

ref.quote: A general statement or rule is always of less authority than a specific statement or rule and yields to it.

prompt: The Wexford Society's bylaws list its officers as president, vice-president, secretary, and treasurer. Member Dane moves to create a sergeant-at-arms office by ordinary motion. Chair Ruiz asks whether that is possible without amending the bylaws.
gold: No. When the bylaws specifically authorize certain officers, other officers of the same class are thereby prohibited. Creating a sergeant-at-arms not named in the bylaws requires a bylaw amendment, not an ordinary motion.

ref.quote: If the bylaws authorize certain things specifically, other things of the same class are thereby prohibited.

prompt: A genuinely ambiguous bylaw of the Calverton Club has produced two readings. Member Estes asks whether resolving the ambiguity needs the two-thirds vote used to amend bylaws. Chair Pike asks you to rule.
gold: No. An ambiguity must exist before interpretation is warranted, and where one does exist, only a majority vote is required to decide the question. Resolving the ambiguity is not an amendment, so the two-thirds threshold does not apply; the ambiguous provision should later be amended for clarity.

ref.quote: An ambiguity must exist before there is any occasion for interpretation. If a bylaw is ambiguous, it must be interpreted, if possible, in harmony with the other bylaws. The interpretation should be in accordance with the intention of the society at the time the bylaw was adopted, as far as this can be determined. Again, intent plays no role unless the meaning is unclear or uncertain, but where an ambiguity exists, a majority vote is all that is required to decide the question.

prompt: At the Rosedale Society, one reading of a bylaw would clash with and render absurd another bylaw provision, while the alternative reading harmonizes them. Member Falk favors the clashing reading. Chair Imani asks for a ruling.
gold: When a provision is susceptible to two meanings, and one conflicts with or renders absurd another bylaw provision while the other does not, the harmonizing meaning must be taken as the true meaning. Falk's clashing reading must be rejected.

ref.quote: When a provision of the bylaws is susceptible to two meanings, one of which conflicts with or renders absurd another bylaw provision, and the other meaning does not, the latter must be taken as the true meaning.

prompt: The Ashby Club's bylaws limit a member to holding one office at a time. Member Cade asks whether that also bars holding two or three offices, and Chair Ono asks how the limitation should be read.
gold: A limitation prohibits everything greater than what is limited but permits what is less. The one-office limit therefore also prohibits holding two or three offices, since those exceed the stated limit. The prohibition reaches everything beyond the limitation.

ref.quote: A prohibition or limitation prohibits everything greater than what is prohibited, or that goes beyond the limitation; but it permits what is less than the limitation, and also permits things of the same class that are not mentioned in the prohibition or limitation and that are not evidently improper.

prompt: A new organization, the Linden Neighborhood Association, is drafting its first bylaws. President Favre asks which subjects the bylaws should typically cover. Advise.
gold: The bylaws should typically cover the standard articles: name, object, members, officers, meetings, executive board (if needed), committees, parliamentary authority, and amendment of bylaws. These are the subjects typical of an average society's bylaws.

ref.quote: the general nature of the subjects covered will be indicated by the following list of articles, typical of those found in the bylaws of the average unincorporated society: Name of the organization; its Object; Members; Officers; Meetings; Executive Board (if needed); Committees; Parliamentary Authority (that is, the name of the manual of parliamentary procedure that the organization is to follow; see below); and Amendment of Bylaws (prescribing the procedure for making changes in the bylaws).

prompt: The Marbury Club debates whether a certain important rule belongs in the bylaws or in an easily changed standing rule. President Yoon asks what kind of rule belongs in the bylaws. Advise.
gold: Rules the society considers so important that they should not be changeable without previous notice and a specified large majority (such as a two-thirds vote), and that should not be suspendible, belong in the bylaws. Routine, easily changed matters do not.

ref.quote: includes all rules that the society considers so important that they (a) cannot be changed without previous notice to the members and the vote of a specified large majority (such as a two-thirds vote), and (b) cannot be suspended (with the exception of clauses that provide for their own suspension under specified conditions, or clauses in the nature of rules of order as described in 2:14; see also 25:7-13 and 56:50- 56).

prompt: While revising its bylaws, the Ashcombe Society wants to insert a clause covering one-time transition mechanics into the bylaws text itself. Member Reidy asks whether that is appropriate content. Rule.
gold: No. Provisions of a temporary nature or relating to the mechanics of transition from old to revised bylaws should not be included within the bylaws. Such matters belong in transitional provisos, not in the permanent document.

ref.quote: Provisions of a temporary nature or relating to the mechanics of transition from old to revised bylaws should not be included within bylaws .

prompt: At the unincorporated Delmar Society, a standing rule appears to conflict with the bylaws. Member Spence asks which document prevails and where the bylaws rank. Rule.
gold: Except for a corporate charter where one exists, the bylaws comprise the highest body of rules and supersede all other rules of the society. The conflicting standing rule yields to the bylaws.

ref.quote: the bylaws (as the single, combination-type instrument is called in this book) comprise the highest body of rules in societies as normally established today. Such an instrument supersedes all other rules of the society, except the corporate charter, if there is one.

prompt: The incorporated Brightwater Foundation finds a bylaw provision that appears to conflict with its articles of incorporation. Member Hoyt asks which governs. Rule.
gold: In an incorporated organization the corporate charter supersedes all other rules, none of which may legally conflict with it. The conflicting bylaw provision is void to the extent of the conflict; the charter governs.

ref.quote: In an incorporated organization, the corporate charter supersedes all its other rules, none of which can legally contain anything in conflict with the charter.

prompt: The Oldfield Society keeps a separate constitution and separate bylaws. A bylaw seems to conflict with a constitutional provision. Member Alcott asks which controls. Rule.
gold: Where an organization has both a separate constitution and separate bylaws, the constitution is the higher of the two and supersedes the bylaws. The constitutional provision controls over the conflicting bylaw.

ref.quote: the constitution is the higher of the two bodies of rules and supersedes the bylaws.

conceptName: Procedural law may supersede org rules
prompt: The Kensington Homeowners Association adopts a bylaw procedure that conflicts with a mandatory procedural provision of the state condominium statute. Member Rhodes asks which prevails. Rule.
gold: Applicable procedural rules prescribed by local, state, or national law govern, and any action of the body in violation of such law is null and void. The statute prevails over the conflicting bylaw provision.

ref.quote: the actions of any deliberative body are also subject to applicable procedural rules prescribed by local, state, or national law and would be null and void if in violation of such law.

conceptName: Procedural law may supersede org rules
prompt: At the incorporated Fairmont Club, an action was taken following the bylaws but contrary to a governing statute. Member Cole insists that because the bylaws were followed, the action stands. Chair Ng asks for a ruling.
gold: Following the bylaws does not save an action that violates applicable procedural law. Because the body's actions are subject to procedural rules prescribed by law and are null and void if in violation, the action does not stand.

ref.quote: the actions of any deliberative body are also subject to applicable procedural rules prescribed by local, state, or national law and would be null and void if in violation of such law.

conceptName: Procedural law may supersede org rules
prompt: The bylaws committee of the Danbury Labor Council, an organization governed by statutes affecting its procedure, asks whether its parliamentary-authority article should acknowledge those statutes. Advise.
gold: Where an organization is subject to local, state, or national law containing procedural provisions, as with a labor organization or an incorporated association, it may be desirable to add a phrase in the parliamentary-authority article recognizing applicable statutes. Such statutes take precedence over conflicting bylaw provisions.

ref.quote: Where a particular type of organization is subject to local, state, or national law containing provisions relating to its procedure-as for certain procedures in a labor organization, in condominium associations, or in an incorporated association-it may be desirable to add at this point a phrase such as, "and any statutes applicable to this organization that do not authorize the provisions of these bylaws to take precedence."

prompt: The Watertown Cooperative plans to buy real estate, hire staff, and sign contracts. Member Iqbal asks whether incorporation matters for the organization. Advise.
gold: Incorporation is sometimes necessary or advisable, depending on state law, when an organization intends to hold property, make binding contracts, hire employees, or be in a position to sue or be sued as a society. Given the cooperative's plans, incorporation is worth serious consideration.

ref.quote: Incorporation is sometimes necessary or may be advisable, depending upon the differing laws of each state, if the organization is to hold property, inherit a legacy, make legally binding contracts, hire employees, be in a position to sue or be sued as a society, protect its officers and members from personal liability, or the like.

prompt: Now incorporated, the Watertown Cooperative discovers a bylaw that conflicts with its corporate charter. Member Boone asks what corporation status changes about the rules' hierarchy. Rule.
gold: Once incorporated, the corporate charter supersedes all the organization's other rules, none of which may legally conflict with the charter. The conflicting bylaw is void to that extent, and nothing in the charter can be suspended by the organization unless the charter or law allows.

ref.quote: In an incorporated organization, the corporate charter supersedes all its other rules, none of which can legally contain anything in conflict with the charter.

prompt: The Watertown Cooperative's board wants to draft its own corporate charter to save money and file it themselves. Member Diaz asks whether that is wise. Advise.
gold: A corporate charter should be drafted by an attorney and processed according to the state's legal procedure for incorporation. Self-drafting is inadvisable; later amendments are also subject to legal requirements and any limits in the charter itself.

ref.quote: A corporate charter should be drafted by an attorney and must then be processed in accordance with the legal procedure for incorporation in the state (or under federal law if applicable).

conceptName: Established custom and setting it aside
prompt: For years the Glenbrook Club has opened with a social hour, though no rule requires it. This week Chair Peralta wants to skip it. Member Ford objects that custom binds them. Rule.
gold: An established custom that has no contrary written rule is adhered to unless the assembly, by majority vote, agrees in a particular instance to do otherwise. So the club may skip the social hour this week by a majority vote; Ford's objection does not prevent it.

ref.quote: such an established custom is adhered to unless the assembly, by a majority vote, agrees in a particular instance to do otherwise.

conceptName: Established custom and setting it aside
prompt: Member Salgado of the Riverbend Society claims a long-standing practice is as binding as a written rule and can never be departed from. Chair Uwais asks you to rule on the status of such a custom.
gold: A practice followed as established custom is treated much like a rule, but only where there is no contrary provision in the parliamentary authority or written rules. Even then it may be set aside in a particular instance by majority vote, so it is not absolutely binding as Salgado claims.

ref.quote: If there is no contrary provision in the parliamentary authority or written rules of the organization, such an established custom is adhered to unless the assembly, by a majority vote, agrees in a particular instance to do otherwise.

conceptName: Established custom and setting it aside
prompt: The Fairhaven Guild has always seated the immediate past president at the head table, with no written rule saying so. Member Croft asks whether this is merely habit or something the club is expected to honor. Advise.
gold: A practice long followed can become an established custom, treated practically as if it were prescribed by a rule. Absent a contrary written rule, the club is expected to honor it unless it decides by majority vote to depart in a given instance.

ref.quote: a particular practice may sometimes come to be followed as a matter of established custom so that it is treated practically as if it were prescribed by a rule.

prompt: At the Stanwick Club, the custom has been to allow proxy voting, but the bylaws actually prohibit it. Member Oduya raises a point of order citing the conflict. Chair Lem asks for a ruling.
gold: When a customary practice conflicts with the parliamentary authority or a written rule and a point of order citing the conflict is raised, the custom falls to the ground. The bylaws' prohibition on proxy voting must be followed; the custom cannot continue.

ref.quote: if a customary practice is or becomes in conflict with the parliamentary authority or any written rule, and a Point of Order citing the conflict is raised at any time, the custom falls to the ground, and the conflicting provision in the parliamentary authority or written rule must thereafter be complied with.

prompt: After a member of the Holloway Society points out that a long-standing custom violates a bylaw, President Ames asks what the club must do going forward. Rule.
gold: Once the conflict is raised by point of order, the custom falls and the conflicting written rule must thereafter be complied with. Going forward the club must follow the bylaw, not the abandoned custom.
keywords: written rule must be complied with

ref.quote: the conflicting provision in the parliamentary authority or written rule must thereafter be complied with.

prompt: The Larkspur Club liked its old custom that a point of order just killed. Member Voss asks whether there is any legitimate way to restore the former practice. Advise.
gold: Yes. If the club still wants the former practice, it can adopt or amend a special rule of order (or, where appropriate, a standing rule or bylaw provision) to incorporate it. That makes the practice a written rule rather than a custom that conflicts with existing rules.

ref.quote: If it is then desired to follow the former practice, a special rule of order (or, in appropriate circumstances, a standing rule or a bylaw provision) can be added or amended to incorporate it. > 1.

prompt: Three months ago the Medford Association took an action that violated a mandatory procedural provision of state law, but no one objected at the time. Now member Pruitt wants to raise a point of order. Chair Salinas asks whether it is too late. Rule.
gold: It is not too late. A point of order about action taken in violation of applicable procedural law prescribed by federal, state, or local law concerns a continuing breach and may be raised at any time the breach continues. Pruitt's point of order is timely.
keywords: point of order at any time

ref.quote: any action has been taken in violation of applicable procedural rules prescribed by federal, state, or local law, d) any action has been taken in violation of a fundamental principle of parliamentary law , or e) any action has been taken in violation of a rule protecting absentees, a rule in the bylaws protecting the secrecy of the members' votes (as on a ballot vote), or a rule protecting a basic right of an individual member . (For particular rules applicable to boards, see 23:9.) 23:7 Remedy for Violation of the Right to Vote.

prompt: Member Frayne of the Northcott Club insists every point of order must be raised the instant a rule is broken, even for a bylaw-conflicting motion adopted last year. Chair Reddy asks you to rule.
gold: Frayne overstates the rule. For breaches of a continuing nature the point of order may be made at any time during the continuance of the breach, regardless of how much time has elapsed. Last year's bylaw-conflicting action can still be challenged.
keywords: point of order at any time

ref.quote: a point of order can be made at any time during the continuance of the breach-that is, at any time that the action has continuing force and effect-regardless of how much time has elapsed.

prompt: The Ellsworth Society adopted a main motion two meetings ago that plainly conflicts with its bylaws. Member Tao asks whether that adopted motion can still be attacked as void. Rule.
gold: Yes. A main motion adopted in conflict with the bylaws is a continuing breach and null and void, so a point of order may be raised at any time while it remains in force. The motion can still be challenged despite the delay.

ref.quote: a main motion has been adopted that conflicts with the bylaws (or constitution) of the organization or assembly

conceptName: Suspend the Rules cannot suspend law
prompt: At the incorporated Bellsize Club, member Ojo moves to suspend the rules to skip a notice period that is actually required by the state nonprofit statute. Chair Marks asks whether that suspension is possible. Rule.
gold: No. An applicable procedural rule prescribed by law cannot be suspended unless the law itself provides for its own suspension. The statutory notice period cannot be waived by a motion to Suspend the Rules; the motion is out of order.

ref.quote: No applicable procedural rule prescribed by federal, state, or local law can be suspended unless the rule specifically provides for its own suspension.

conceptName: Suspend the Rules cannot suspend law
prompt: The Pinehill Association wants to suspend the rules to hold a vote by a method its governing statute forbids. Member Sena argues a two-thirds vote can suspend anything. Chair Ade asks for a ruling.
gold: A two-thirds vote cannot suspend a procedural rule prescribed by law unless the law provides for its own suspension. The statutory voting requirement stands, and the motion to suspend is out of order.

ref.quote: No applicable procedural rule prescribed by federal, state, or local law can be suspended unless the rule specifically provides for its own suspension.

conceptName: Suspend the Rules cannot suspend law
prompt: Member Gale of the Rowan Society points out that a particular statutory rule expressly states it may be waived by the organization. He asks whether the club may then suspend it. Chair Voss asks for a ruling.
gold: Because that statutory rule specifically provides for its own suspension, it may be suspended as the statute allows. The general bar on suspending procedural law prescribed by law does not apply where the law itself authorizes waiver.

ref.quote: No applicable procedural rule prescribed by federal, state, or local law can be suspended unless the rule specifically provides for its own suspension.

prompt: A brand-new group, the Tolliver Study Circle, has adopted no bylaws or parliamentary authority yet. Member Frey asks what procedural rules govern their meetings in the meantime. Advise.
gold: With no rules adopted, the group is understood to be bound by the general (common) parliamentary law, to the extent the meeting agrees on what those rules and practices are. Until it adopts written rules, that body of common parliamentary law governs.

ref.quote: A deliberative assembly that has not adopted any rules is commonly understood to hold itself bound by the rules and customs of the general parliamentary law-or common parliamentary law (as discussed in the Introduction)-to the extent that there is agreement in the meeting body as to what these rules and practices are.

prompt: President Udall of the Marlowe Society wants to know the full set of rule sources that can govern the club's procedure. Advise.
gold: Most assemblies operate under one or more classes of written rules the body, or a higher authority over it, has formally adopted, layered on top of the general parliamentary law. The applicable procedure is found by consulting those adopted rules together with the common parliamentary law.

ref.quote: Most assemblies operate subject to one or more classes of written rules, however, that the particular body-or, sometimes, a higher authority under which it is constituted-has formally adopted.

prompt: The Ashwood Association is mapping out which authorities control its procedure. Member Neel asks whether applicable law belongs in that analysis and how it ranks. Advise.
gold: Yes. Beyond its own rules and the parliamentary authority, a deliberative body is subject to applicable procedural rules prescribed by local, state, or national law, and its actions are null and void if in violation of such law. Applicable law sits at the top of the analysis.

ref.quote: the actions of any deliberative body are also subject to applicable procedural rules prescribed by local, state, or national law and would be null and void if in violation of such law.

prompt: The Cedarville Lodge holds a charter granted by its national organization authorizing it to exist as a local unit. Member Osei asks what such a charter is and where it comes from. Advise.
gold: That kind of charter is a certificate issued by a national or state organization granting the right to form a particular local or subordinate unit. It is not an instrument of incorporation but a grant of authority from the superior organization.

ref.quote: The word charter may also refer to a certificate issued by a national or state organization, granting the right to form a particular local or subordinate unit.

prompt: The Cedarville Lodge adopts a local rule that conflicts with the rules of its national grantor. Member Bako asks which prevails. Rule.
