gold: The national charter supersedes any rules the subordinate lodge may adopt, because it carries the requirement that the subordinate unit adopt no rules conflicting with those of the grantor. The local rule is void to the extent it conflicts; the grantor's rules prevail.

ref.quote: it supersedes any rules the subordinate body may adopt, because it carries with it the requirement that the subordinate unit adopt no rules that conflict with those of the grantor. > 5.

prompt: Member Adler of the Cedarville Lodge assumes that because the national charter is "just general language" and not an incorporation document, local rules can freely override it. Chair Nyong asks for a ruling.
gold: Adler is mistaken. Although the charter is not an instrument of incorporation and is quite general in its terms, it nonetheless supersedes any rules the subordinate body adopts. Local rules cannot override it where they conflict.

ref.quote: While such a charter is not an instrument of incorporation and is usually quite general in its terms, it supersedes any rules the subordinate body may adopt, because it carries with it the requirement that the subordinate unit adopt no rules that conflict with those of the grantor. > 5.

prompt: At the Brookfield Society, the executive board passed a policy and now claims the general membership cannot touch it. Member Salas disagrees. Chair Voss asks how a society board relates to the full assembly. Rule.
gold: A board within an organized society is an instrumentality of the society's full assembly, to which it is subordinate. Unlike a business-corporation board, this board is not supreme; the assembly may generally instruct it and review its actions, so the board cannot place its policy beyond the membership's reach.

ref.quote: A board within an organized society, on the other hand, is an instrumentality of the society's full assembly, to which it is subordinate.

prompt: The membership of the Harmon Club voted to hold its gala in June. The executive board later voted to move it to September. Member Pruett objects. Chair Odell asks for a ruling.
gold: The board's action is null and void. No action of the board can alter or conflict with a decision made by the assembly of the society. The gala remains in June unless the assembly itself changes it.

ref.quote: no action of the board can alter or conflict with any decision made by the assembly of the society, and any such action of the board is null and void .

prompt: The Kentbridge Society's bylaws create an executive committee within its large board. Member Faro asks whether that executive committee follows the rules for boards or for committees, and whether it can override the board. Rule.
gold: An executive committee is in reality a "board within a board" and operates under the rules applicable to boards, not those for committees. Like a board under the society, it cannot alter any decision made by the board itself.

ref.quote: The executive committee is thus in reality a "board within a board" and operates under the rules in this book applicable to boards rather than those applicable to committees.

prompt: At the Portland Civic League, the newly elected secretary, Ruiz, asks what her core recording duty is. Advise.
gold: The secretary's first duty is to keep a record of all the proceedings of the organization, usually called the minutes. That, along with keeping the records and giving required notices, is central to the office.

ref.quote: To keep a record of all the proceedings of the organization— usually called the minutes

prompt: A member of the Westhaven Society asks to inspect the minutes and record books. Secretary Blau refuses, saying only officers may see them. Chair Ono asks for a ruling.
gold: Any member has the right to examine the society's reports and record books, including minutes, at a reasonable time and place, though this must not be abused to annoy the secretary. Blau cannot categorically refuse; the member's right of inspection stands.

ref.quote: Any member has a right to examine these reports and the record book(s) referred to in 47:33, including the minutes of an executive session, at a reasonable time and place, but this privilege must not be abused to the annoyance of the secretary.

prompt: The Grantham Club is choosing a president chiefly for popularity. Member Sable asks what a presiding officer really needs to know, even with a parliamentarian available. Advise.
gold: A presiding officer should be well versed in parliamentary law and thoroughly familiar with the bylaws and other rules, even if a parliamentarian is available to assist. Ability to preside, not popularity, should be the principal qualification.

ref.quote: This person should be well versed in parliamentary law and should be thoroughly familiar with the bylaws and other rules of the organization-even if he or she is to have the assistance of a parliamentarian.

prompt: On the board of the Fairlawn Foundation, director Meade will personally profit from a contract the board is about to vote on with a firm he owns. Chair Rios asks whether Meade should vote. Rule.
gold: Meade should abstain. No member should vote on a question in which he has a direct personal or pecuniary interest not common to other members, and a contract from which he would personally profit is exactly such a question. He ought to refrain from voting on it.

ref.quote: No member should vote on a question in which he has a direct personal or pecuniary interest not common to other members of the organization.

prompt: At the Fairlawn Foundation board, director Meade has a personal financial interest but insists on voting anyway. Some directors want to bar him. Chair Rios asks whether they can force him to abstain. Rule.
gold: Although Meade should abstain because of his direct personal interest, no member can be compelled to refrain from voting in such circumstances. The board may urge him to abstain but cannot forcibly prevent him from voting.

ref.quote: However, no member can be compelled to refrain from voting in such circumstances.

prompt: During a board election at the Fairlawn Foundation, director Kwan is nominated and wonders whether the conflict-of-interest rule bars her from voting for herself. Chair Rios asks for a ruling.
gold: The abstention rule does not bar a member from voting for herself for an office or position to which members generally are eligible, nor when she is merely included with others in a motion. Kwan may vote for herself in the election.

ref.quote: does not mean that a member should not vote for himself for an office or other position to which members generally are eligible, or should not vote when other members are included with him in a motion.

prompt: The bylaws of the Cliffside Academy make the state governor an ex-officio trustee, though he is not otherwise under the academy's authority. Member Doss asks what rights the governor has on the board. Rule.
gold: Because he is not under the academy's authority, this ex-officio trustee has all the privileges of board membership, including the right to make motions and vote, but none of the obligations. He may participate fully but is not required to.

ref.quote: he has all the privileges of board membership, including the right to make motions and to vote, but none of the obligations-just as in a case, for example, where the governor of a state is ex officio a trustee of a private academy.

prompt: The Cliffside Academy board has nine members plus the governor as an ex-officio trustee not under the academy's authority. He rarely attends. Secretary Yee asks whether he counts toward quorum. Rule.
gold: An ex-officio board member not under the organization's authority, who has no obligation to participate, is not counted in determining the number required for a quorum or whether a quorum is present. Quorum is figured on the nine regular members, not counting the governor.

ref.quote: The latter class of ex-officio board member, who has no obligation to participate, is not counted in determining the number required for a quorum or whether a quorum is present at a meeting.

prompt: At the Redfield Society, the committee chairmanship that made Alvez an ex-officio board member passes to someone else. Member Cobb asks whether Alvez stays on the board. Rule.
gold: No. When an ex-officio member ceases to hold the office that entitled him to board membership, his board membership terminates automatically. Alvez leaves the board, and his successor in the office becomes the ex-officio member.

ref.quote: When an ex-officio member of a board ceases to hold the office that entitles him to such membership, his membership on the board terminates automatically.

prompt: The Ridgeway Society refers a matter to a committee and wants to tell it when to meet and when to report. Member Sato asks whether such instructions can be part of the referring motion. Rule.
gold: Yes. Instructions to the committee can be included in the motion to Commit, whether the committee is standing, special, or a committee of the whole. Such instructions are binding on the committee.

ref.quote: Instructions to the committee can also be included in the motion to Commit, whether the committee is to be a standing or a special one, or a committee of the whole.

prompt: Two weeks after referring a question to committee, the Marston Club wants to add new instructions before the committee reports. Member Vega asks whether it is too late and what vote is needed. Rule.
gold: It is not too late. After referral and before the committee reports, even at a later session, the assembly may by majority vote give the committee additional instructions on the referred question. A majority vote suffices.

ref.quote: the assembly by a majority vote can give the committee additional instructions in reference to the referred question ).

prompt: The Halden Guild wants its motion to Commit to direct exactly how the committee handles the matter and its deadline. Member Ott asks how binding those directions are. Rule.
gold: The instructions in the motion to Commit are binding on the committee and may cover matters such as when it is to meet, how it is to consider the question, whether to employ a consultant, and when it is to report. The committee must follow them.

ref.quote: These instructions, which are binding on the committee, may involve such matters as when the committee is to meet, how it is to consider the question, whether it is to employ an expert consultant, and when it is to report.

conceptName: Power to appoint, fill, and remove
prompt: A vacancy opens on a committee of the Sherwood Society that the assembly originally appointed. Member Lund asks who fills it. Rule.
gold: The power to appoint a committee carries with it the power to appoint its chairman and to fill any vacancy that arises. Since the assembly was the appointing power, the assembly fills the vacancy.

ref.quote: The power to appoint a committee carries with it the power to appoint the chairman and to fill any vacancy that may arise in the committee.

conceptName: Power to appoint, fill, and remove
prompt: At the Dalton Club, the president alone appointed a special committee under the bylaws' authority and now wants to remove one member. Member Poe questions whether he can. Rule.
gold: If a single person such as the president has the power of appointment, that person has the power to remove or replace a member so appointed. The president may remove the committee member by his own act.

ref.quote: If a single person, such as the president, has the power of appointment, he has the power to remove or replace a member so appointed; but if the assembly has the power of selection, removal or replacement can take place only under rules applicable to the motions to Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted .

conceptName: Power to appoint, fill, and remove
prompt: The Fenwick Society itself elected a committee's members. Now the assembly wants to remove one. Member Cray asks what vote or procedure is required. Rule.
gold: When the assembly holds the power of selection, removal or replacement can occur only under the rules for Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted, which require a two-thirds vote, a majority of the entire membership, or a majority with previous notice. A bare majority without notice will not do.
keywords: two-thirds or majority of entire membership

ref.quote: if the assembly has the power of selection, removal or replacement can take place only under rules applicable to the motions to Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted .

prompt: At the Millbrook Society, a committee claims the same broad power to act for the society that the board has. Member Honce questions this. Chair Voss asks how a committee's authority compares to a board's. Rule.
gold: Generally a committee has less authority to act independently for the society than a board is understood to have. Unless empowered otherwise, a committee typically reports findings or recommendations rather than acting on its own; it does not automatically wield board-like power.

ref.quote: the committee has less authority to act independently for the society (or other constituting power) than a board is usually understood to have.

prompt: The Oakhurst Club's program committee wants to sign a venue contract on its own. Member Frey asks whether it may act, or only report. Rule.
gold: Unless empowered otherwise, a committee that is to do more than report its findings or recommendations may act for the society only on specific instructions. Without such authorization, the committee should bring a recommendation to the assembly rather than sign the contract itself.

ref.quote: if the committee is to do more than report its findings or recommendations to the assembly, it may be empowered to act for the society only on specific instructions; or, if it is given standing powers, its actions may be more closely subject to review than a board's, or it may be required to report more fully.

prompt: The Denby Society appoints a committee "with power." Member Iles asks what that phrase actually authorizes. Rule.
gold: When a committee is appointed "with power," it means with power to take all the steps necessary to carry out its instructions. The phrase authorizes the committee to act to accomplish the assigned task, not to range beyond its instructions.

ref.quote: When a committee is appointed "with power," this means with power to take all the steps necessary to carry out its instructions.

conceptName: Terms of standing vs special committees
prompt: President Aziz of the Cranford Club wants to explain the difference between the club's finance committee and a one-time gala committee. Advise on how each type's existence works.
gold: A standing committee has a continuing existence, while a special committee goes out of existence as soon as it has completed its specified task. The finance committee is standing; the gala committee is special and dissolves once its work is done.

ref.quote: Ordinary committees are of two types-standing committees (which have a continuing existence) and special committees (which go out of existence as soon as they have completed a specified task).

conceptName: Terms of standing vs special committees
prompt: At the Ashland Society, no bylaw fixes standing-committee terms. Member Boyd asks how long its standing-committee members serve. Rule.
gold: Absent a contrary bylaw, standing-committee members serve for a term corresponding to that of the officers, or until their successors have been chosen. A new set is normally appointed at the beginning of each administration.

ref.quote: the members of such a committee serve for a term corresponding to that of the officers, or until their successors have been chosen, unless the bylaws or other rules otherwise expressly provide.

conceptName: Terms of standing vs special committees
prompt: The Milton Club's special committee has just presented its final report. Member Ng asks whether the committee still exists and could take up related work. Rule.
gold: No. A special committee automatically ceases to exist upon presentation of its final report to the assembly. It cannot continue to new work; any further task would require a new appointment.

ref.quote: on presentation of its final report to the assembly-it automatically ceases to exist.

prompt: The Danforth Society wants to appoint a subject-matter expert who is not a member to its committee, even as chairman. Member Ruiz says non-members can never serve. Chair Voss asks for a ruling.
gold: Ruiz is wrong. Persons who are not members of the assembly or society can be appointed to committees, even to the position of committee chairman. Non-members may serve, subject to the assembly's control over each such appointment.

ref.quote: It is possible for persons who are not members of the assembly or the society to be appointed to committees-even to the position of committee chairman-but control over each such appointment is reserved to the assembly in the individual case.

prompt: At the Danforth Society, the president wants to unilaterally place several non-members on committees. Member Ola questions whether the president may do so freely. Rule.
gold: Control over each appointment of a non-member is reserved to the assembly in the individual case. The president cannot freely install non-members; the assembly retains control over each such appointment.

ref.quote: control over each such appointment is reserved to the assembly in the individual case.

prompt: The Danforth Society is choosing how to appoint a committee that will include a non-member. Member Sena asks which appointment methods allow a non-member. Rule.
gold: Non-assembly members may be appointed by methods (a), (b), (c), and (e) among the five appointment methods. When method (d), appointment by the chair, is used, the special rule for naming non-members applies instead.
keywords: methods a b c and e

ref.quote: may be appointed to committees by methods (a), (b), (c), and (e), as listed below.

prompt: At the Wexbury Club, several methods for selecting a special committee are proposed at once. Chair Ame asks in what order the assembly votes on them. Rule.
gold: When different methods are proposed, they are voted on in this order: (1) election by ballot; (2) nominations from the floor with viva-voce election; (3) nominations by the chair; and (4) appointment by the chair. The first method to receive a majority is inserted and the rest are ignored.

ref.quote: they are voted on in the following order: election by ballot; nominations from the floor ("open nominations") with viva-voce election; nominations by the chair; and appointment by the chair .

prompt: The Holloway Society is forming an important standing committee with broad powers and wants the most deliberate selection method. Member Roy asks which method fits and what vote elects. Advise.
gold: Election by ballot is principally applicable to important standing committees having extensive powers. Nominees are voted on by ballot, with a majority vote necessary to elect, just as in electing a board.

ref.quote: Election by ballot. This method is principally applicable to important standing committees having extensive powers. Under this procedure, nominations for committee membership can be made by any of the methods described in 46; then the nominees are voted on by ballot just as in an election of a board, a majority vote being necessary to elect.

prompt: After several selection methods are moved at the Marden Club, the first one voted on passes by majority. Member Poe asks whether the assembly still votes on the others. Rule.
gold: No. The first method of selection that receives a majority vote is inserted into the motion to Commit and the remaining methods are ignored. Once one passes, the others are not voted on.

ref.quote: The first of these methods of selection that receives a majority vote is then inserted into the motion to Commit and the remainder are ignored.

prompt: The Fenmore Society voted to hold a fundraiser and now appoints a committee to carry it out. Member Ide suggests including known opponents "for balance." Chair Voss asks for a ruling on composition.
gold: When a special committee is appointed to implement an order of the assembly, it should be small and consist only of those in favor of the action to be carried out. Opponents should not be placed on an implementation committee; anyone not in sympathy should ask to be excused.

ref.quote: When a special committee is appointed to implement an order of the assembly, it should be small and should consist only of those in favor of the action to be carried out.

prompt: The Aldridge Club appoints a committee to investigate a contentious issue and recommend a course. Member Osei asks how its membership should be composed. Advise.
gold: A special committee appointed for deliberation or investigation should often be larger and should represent, as far as possible, all points of view in the organization, so its recommendation carries maximum weight. Leaving out an important faction would impair its usefulness.

ref.quote: When a special committee is appointed for deliberation or investigation, however, it should often be larger, and it should represent, as far as possible, all points of view in the organization, so that its opinion will carry maximum weight.

prompt: The Barnwell Society is packing an investigative committee with only one faction's members. Member Cray warns this is unwise. Chair Ono asks for a ruling on the risk.
gold: For a deliberative or investigative committee, its usefulness will be greatly impaired if any important faction of the assembly is not represented. The committee should be balanced to reflect all significant viewpoints; a one-faction committee undermines the weight of its recommendation.

ref.quote: The usefulness of the committee will be greatly impaired, on the other hand, if any important faction of the assembly is not represented.

prompt: The executive board of the Kelso Society wants to adopt its own special rules of order. Member Frey asks how far the board may go. Rule.
gold: The board may adopt its own special rules of order or standing rules only to the extent they do not conflict with the society's bylaws, parliamentary authority, or applicable special rules or standing rules. Any board rule conflicting with those higher rules is invalid.

ref.quote: Such a board may adopt its own special rules of order or standing rules only to the extent that such rules do not conflict with any of the rules of the society listed above.

prompt: A committee of the Ravenna Club wants to adopt procedural rules of its own choosing. Member Doss asks whether it may. Rule.
gold: A committee may not adopt its own rules except as authorized in the rules of the society or in instructions given by the parent assembly in a particular case. Without such authorization the committee cannot make up its own rules.
keywords: committee may not adopt own rules

ref.quote: A committee may not adopt its own rules except as authorized in the rules of the society or in instructions given to the committee by its parent assembly in a particular case.

prompt: During a heated board meeting of the Sutton Society, a director is disorderly. Another director moves to suspend him from the board. Chair Voss asks what penalty the board itself may impose. Rule.
gold: The board itself may protect order, but the maximum penalty it may impose on a disorderly member is to require him to leave the meeting room for the remainder of that meeting. It cannot suspend or expel him from the board; that lies beyond the board's power.

ref.quote: the maximum penalty which may be imposed upon a disorderly member of the board is that he be required to leave the meeting room during the remainder of the meeting.

prompt: The president of the Talbot Society phones each board member individually and gets everyone's approval to sign a lease, with no meeting held. Member Vane asks whether that binds the board. Rule.
gold: It does not. A board can transact business only in a regular or properly called meeting of which every member has had required notice, and at which a quorum is present. Separate individual approvals by phone are not board action, because members lacked the chance to deliberate together.

ref.quote: a board can transact business only in a regular or properly called meeting of which every board member has been sent any required notice -or at an adjournment of one of these meetings-and at which a quorum is present.

prompt: The board chair of the Everly Club circulates a proposal by email and each director replies "yes" separately. She announces it as an official board decision. Member Ojo objects. Rule.
gold: The personal approval of an action obtained separately by telephone, individual interviews, or in writing, even from every member, is not the approval of the board, because the members had no opportunity to mutually debate and decide as a deliberative body. It cannot be announced as an official board decision.

ref.quote: The personal approval of a proposed action obtained separately by telephone, by individual interviews, or in writing, even from every member of the board, is not the approval of the board, since the members lacked the opportunity to mutually debate and decide the matter as a deliberative body. (See also Electronic Meetings, 9:30-36.) 49:17 A record of the board's proceedings is kept by the secretary, just as in any other assembly.

prompt: The chair of the Redwood Committee drafts a report reflecting views she gathered by phoning members one at a time, and presents it as the committee's official report. Member Sable objects. Rule.
gold: Except in narrow circumstances, a report of a board or committee can contain only what was agreed to by a majority vote at a properly noticed meeting with a quorum present. Facts or recommendations gathered by separate consultation must be described as such, not presented as the official report.

ref.quote: a report of a board or committee can contain only what has been agreed to by a majority vote at a regular or properly called meeting of which every member has been notified (or at an adjournment of one of these meetings, 9:17-19)-where a quorum of the board or committee was present.

prompt: The nine-member board of the Larkin Society debates whether the full formality of a large assembly applies to its meetings. Chair Voss asks when the relaxed procedures apply. Rule.
gold: In a board meeting where there are not more than about a dozen members present, the relaxed small-board procedures apply, because full formality would hinder business. With only nine members, the Larkin board may use the informal small-board rules.
keywords: not more than about a dozen

ref.quote: In a board meeting where there are not more than about a dozen members present, some of the formality that is necessary in a large assembly would hinder business.

prompt: A standing committee of the Norwood Club asks whether the informal small-board procedures apply to its meetings. Member Frey asks for a ruling.
gold: The informal small-board procedures apply during the meetings of all standing and special committees, unless the committee is otherwise instructed by the society. So the Norwood committee uses the small-board rules by default.

ref.quote: for use in small boards are applicable during the meetings of all standing and special committees, unless the committee is otherwise instructed by the society ; also, the rules governing the motions to Rescind, to Amend Something Previously Adopted, and to Reconsider are modified as stated in 35:2 and 37:35.

prompt: The Pemberton Society has a very large standing committee that operates like a full assembly. Chair Ono asks whether the informal small-board rules must still govern it. Rule.
gold: If a committee is so large that it functions best like a full-scale assembly, it should be instructed that the small-board informalities do not apply to its proceedings. The parent assembly may adopt such instructions by majority vote.

ref.quote: If a standing or special committee is so large that it can function best in the manner of a full-scale assembly, it should be instructed that the informalities and modifications of the regular rules of parliamentary procedure listed for small boards in 49:21 are not to apply to its proceedings.

prompt: A new board of the Halcyon Society asks whether it must keep minutes and who keeps them. Advise.
gold: Yes. A record of the board's proceedings, its minutes, is kept by the secretary, just as in any other assembly. Board minutes are a normal and required record.

ref.quote: A record of the board's proceedings is kept by the secretary, just as in any other assembly.

prompt: At the Weston Society, an ordinary society member (not on the board) demands to inspect the board's minute book. Secretary Ola is unsure. Chair Voss asks for a ruling.
gold: Only members of the board have the right to examine the board's minute book, unless the board orders otherwise. A non-board society member has no automatic right to inspect them; the board could grant access by its own action.

ref.quote: Only members of the board have the right to examine the minute book kept by the secretary (cf. 47:36), unless the board orders otherwise .

prompt: A small three-person committee of the Ardley Club asks whether it needs a separate secretary and formal minutes. Advise.
gold: In small committees the chairman usually acts as secretary. Large committees may choose a secretary to keep a brief memorandum in the nature of minutes for the committee's own use, but a small committee need not have a separate secretary or elaborate minutes.

ref.quote: In small committees, the chairman usually acts as secretary, but in large ones and many standing committees, a secretary may be chosen to keep a brief memorandum in the nature of minutes for the use of the committee.

prompt: At a small-board meeting of the Wentworth Society, a director makes a motion while seated and no one seconds it. A member objects that it is out of order. Chair Voss asks for a ruling.
gold: Under small-board rules, members may raise a hand instead of standing and may remain seated while making motions or speaking, and motions need not be seconded. The seated, unseconded motion is perfectly in order.

ref.quote: Members may raise a hand instead of standing when seeking to obtain the floor, and may remain seated while making motions or speaking. 2) Motions need not be seconded. 3) There is no limit to the number of times a member can speak to a debatable question.

prompt: In a small-board meeting of the Ellery Club, a director has already spoken twice on a pending question and wants to speak again. Another member says he has used up his turns. Chair Ono asks for a ruling.
gold: Under small-board rules there is no limit to the number of times a member may speak to a debatable question. The director may speak again; the ordinary two-speech limit does not apply in a small board.
keywords: no limit on number of speeches

ref.quote: There is no limit to the number of times a member can speak to a debatable question.

prompt: At the Foxton Society's small board, the chair wants to argue a point in debate and then vote, without stepping down. Member Ade questions whether the chair may do so. Rule.
gold: Under small-board rules, if the chairman is a member he may, without leaving the chair, speak in informal discussions and in debate and vote on all questions. The chair may participate and vote as described.

ref.quote: If the chairman is a member, he may, without leaving the chair, speak in informal discussions and in debate, and vote on all questions.

prompt: The Kingsford Society elects one-third of its board each year to three-year terms. When the new third takes office, member Vane asks what happens to matters the board had only temporarily disposed of. Rule.
gold: Because a portion of the board is chosen periodically, the board becomes, in effect, a new board each time a new group assumes membership. Matters only temporarily but not finally disposed of fall to the ground when the outgoing portion leaves, except those referred to a committee.

ref.quote: it becomes, in effect, a new board each time such a group assumes board membership.

prompt: After its annual partial turnover, the board of the Aldous Club (which elects its own officers) debates whether it must choose officers anew. Member Ott asks for a ruling.
gold: Because the partially renewed board is in effect a new board, if it elects its own officers or appoints standing committees it chooses new ones as soon as the new members take up their duties, just as if the entire membership had changed. New officers should be chosen.

ref.quote: If the board is one that elects its own officers or appoints standing committees, it chooses new officers and committees as soon as the new board members have taken up their duties, just as if the entire board membership had changed.

prompt: Between elections, one director of the Marlow Society resigns and is replaced. Member Cray asks whether this triggers the "new board" consequences. Rule.
gold: No. The individual replacement of a person who occasionally vacates board membership at other times does not produce the "new board" effects. Only the periodic, scheduled turnover of a defined portion of the board triggers those consequences.

ref.quote: The individual replacement of persons who may occasionally vacate board membership at other times, however, does not have these effects.

conceptName: Limiting debate in boards and committees
prompt: In a committee of the Pennfield Society, a member moves the previous question to cut off debate. Chair Voss asks whether that motion is in order in committee. Rule.
gold: It is not in order. Motions to close or limit debate, such as the previous question, are not allowed in committees, so that the assembly may have the benefit of the committee's matured judgment. The motion to cut off debate must be rejected.

ref.quote: motions to close or limit debate are not allowed in committees.

conceptName: Limiting debate in boards and committees
prompt: A member of the Rowanberry Club argues that the committee chair should stay neutral and quiet like a large-assembly presiding officer. Chair Ono asks for a ruling on the chair's role in committee.
gold: In committees the chairman is usually the most active participant in the discussions and work of the committee. Unlike a large-assembly presiding officer, the committee chair is expected to take an active part rather than remain neutral and silent.

ref.quote: In committees, the chairman is usually the most active participant in the discussions and work of the committee.

conceptName: Limiting debate in boards and committees
prompt: During a small-board meeting of the Hartwell Society, a member appeals a ruling of the chair and wants to speak on the appeal several times as with other debate. Chair Voss asks for a ruling.
gold: Although small boards allow unlimited speaking on ordinary debatable questions, appeals are debatable under the regular rules: each member except the chair may speak only once on the appeal, while the chair may speak twice. The member cannot speak on the appeal repeatedly.

ref.quote: Appeals, however, are debatable under the regular rules-that is, each member (except the chair) can speak only once in debate on them, while the chair may speak twice. 4) Informal discussion of a subject is permitted while no motion is pending. 5) When a proposal is perfectly clear to all present, a vote can be taken without a motion's having been introduced.

prompt: A seven-member committee of the Belvoir Society has three members present and wants to act. Member Frey asks whether there is a quorum. Rule.
gold: The quorum in a committee is a majority of its membership unless the assembly has prescribed a different quorum. For seven members that is four, so three members present is not a quorum and the committee cannot transact business.

ref.quote: The quorum in a committee is a majority of its membership unless the assembly has prescribed a different quorum .

prompt: The chair of a committee of the Marchmont Club has failed to call a needed meeting. Two members want to call one themselves. Member Ola asks whether they may. Rule.
gold: If the chairman fails to call a necessary meeting, a committee meeting may be called by any two of its members, unless the assembly's rules require a larger number. The two members may call the meeting, ensuring reasonable notice reaches every member.

ref.quote: a meeting of the committee may be called by any two of its members, unless (such as for very large committees) the assembly's rules or instructions prescribe, or empower the committee itself to require, a larger number.

prompt: A standing committee expressly established in the bylaws of the Overton Society wants to meet by videoconference. The bylaws are silent on electronic meetings. Member Sable asks whether it may. Rule.
gold: A committee expressly established by the bylaws can hold a valid electronic meeting only if the bylaws authorize it. Since the bylaws are silent, this bylaws-established committee may not meet electronically until the bylaws are amended to allow it.

ref.quote: committees that are expressly established by the bylaws can hold a valid electronic meeting only if authorized in the bylaws to do so.

conceptName: Committee hearings and who may attend
prompt: A committee of the Grantley Society will make important recommendations affecting many members. Member Iles asks whether the committee should let members be heard first. Advise.
gold: When a committee is to make substantive recommendations or decisions on an important matter, it should give members of the society an opportunity to appear before it and present their views. Such a meeting is usually called a hearing.

ref.quote: it should give members of the society an opportunity to appear before it and present their views on the subject at a time scheduled by the committee. Such a meeting is usually called a hearing.

conceptName: Committee hearings and who may attend
prompt: After holding a hearing, a committee of the Dunmore Club is ready to deliberate. A society member who testified wants to stay in the room. Chair Voss asks for a ruling.
gold: During the committee's actual deliberations, only committee members have the right to be present. The society member who testified at the hearing has no right to remain for the deliberations and should withdraw.

ref.quote: During actual deliberations of the committee, only committee members have the right to be present.

conceptName: Committee hearings and who may attend
prompt: Members of the Radnor Society demand to appear before a committee immediately, whenever they choose. Chair Ono asks whether members control when they are heard. Rule.
gold: While the committee should give members an opportunity to present their views before deciding an important matter, that hearing occurs at a time scheduled by the committee, not whenever members demand. The committee controls the scheduling of the hearing.

ref.quote: at a time scheduled by the committee.

conceptName: Types of board and committee reports
prompt: The standing finance committee of the Halsey Society submits its yearly report. Member Frey asks what such an annual report is for. Advise.
gold: Annual or periodic reports of boards or standing committees are usually submitted per bylaw requirements and are primarily for information, summarizing the important work done during the period. They may also include recommendations for action by the assembly.

ref.quote: Annual or periodic reports of boards or standing committees are usually submitted in accordance with requirements in the bylaws, are primarily for information, and should summarize important work done by the board or committee during the year or other period covered by the report.

conceptName: Types of board and committee reports
prompt: A board of the Kenwick Club is unsure what belongs in its annual report to the membership. Member Ola asks what it should contain. Advise.
gold: The annual report should summarize the important work done by the board during the year or other period it covers. It is chiefly informational, though it may also contain recommendations, which can propose specific action by the assembly.

ref.quote: should summarize important work done by the board or committee during the year or other period covered by the report.

conceptName: Types of board and committee reports
prompt: During the year a committee of the Ashby Society must report on one specific matter referred to it. Member Cray asks how this differs from the annual report. Advise.
gold: Reports relating to single items of business arising during the year fall into a number of particular forms and can often be quite brief, unlike the comprehensive annual or periodic report. Such a report addresses the specific matter referred, not the year's overall work.

ref.quote: Reports relating to single items of business arising during the year fall into a number of particular forms-which are described for the case of committees in 51:30ff.

prompt: A committee of the Denholm Society scatters its recommendations throughout a long report. Member Frey asks how the recommendations should be presented. Advise.
gold: Specific recommendations for immediate action by the parent assembly should be grouped at the end of the report, repeating them if already noted earlier, and generally cast as one or more proposed resolutions. Scattering them through the text is poor form.

ref.quote: specific recommendations for immediate action by the parent assembly should be grouped at the end-repeating them if they have already been noted at separate places in the report -and should generally be cast in the form of one or more proposed resolutions.

prompt: A committee of the Marbury Club wants to end its report with vague suggestions rather than motions. Member Ola asks how recommendations should be worded so the assembly can act. Advise.
gold: Recommendations for action should generally be cast in the form of one or more proposed resolutions, so their adoption clearly authorizes action rather than leaving confusion about their effect. The committee should draft resolutions, not vague suggestions.

ref.quote: should generally be cast in the form of one or more proposed resolutions.

prompt: A committee of the Sefton Society wants to hand the assembly its findings and let someone else draft the implementing resolutions. Member Cray questions this. Advise.
gold: A board or committee is usually best fitted to prepare the resolutions that carry out its recommendations, and it should never leave that responsibility to others. The committee itself should draft the implementing resolutions.

ref.quote: A board or committee is usually best fitted to prepare resolutions to carry out its recommendations, and it should never leave this responsibility to others.

prompt: Parliamentarian Devon Ackerly received his first parliamentary credential two months ago and has only advised a small garden club. The membership chair of the Statewide Educators Federation, planning a heated 400-delegate convention, asks how much large-convention experience Devon has. Wanting the job, Devon replies, "I've staffed dozens of national conventions this size for years." Impressed, she engages him on the spot. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. The parliamentarian knew he lacked large-convention experience and misrepresented his experience and qualifications to obtain the engagement. This breaches Joint Code section 3.1.

prompt: Coretta Vance holds a Registered Parliamentarian credential but is not certified as a Professional Registered Parliamentarian. The Harborview Credit Union asks whether she is a PRP because their policy requires one. Coretta answers, "Yes, I'm a PRP," and is hired for the annual meeting on that basis. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. The parliamentarian falsely claimed a credential she does not hold in order to obtain the engagement, misrepresenting her qualifications. This breaches Joint Code section 3.1.

prompt: The Ridgeline Homeowners Association asks parliamentarian Marcus Ohl whether he has ever handled a disciplinary trial, which is the sensitive matter on their agenda. Marcus has never done one and says so: "I have not conducted a disciplinary procedure before, though I have studied the process thoroughly and can advise on it." The board weighs this and still hires him. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: No. The parliamentarian honestly disclosed his lack of experience and did not misrepresent his qualifications to obtain the engagement, so there is no breach of Joint Code section 3.1.

prompt: A national trade association interviewing parliamentarian Priya Sundaram asks if she is experienced with electronic and hybrid meetings, which their convention will use. Priya has never advised a single electronic meeting but replies, "Absolutely, hybrid meetings are my specialty and I run them all the time." On the strength of that claim she is selected over other candidates. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. The parliamentarian misrepresented her competence with electronic meetings to obtain the engagement when she knew she had none. This breaches Joint Code section 3.1.

prompt: Parliamentarian Gordon Reyes prints business cards and a website banner reading "Gordon Reyes, PRP, Certified Parliamentarian-Teacher," though he has never earned either designation. He distributes the cards at a state association conference to attract new clients. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. Advertising credentials he does not hold is a false statement about himself and his services. This breaches Joint Code section 3.3.

prompt: After a meeting of the Lakeside Rotary, a satisfied member tells parliamentarian Alicia Bunt she did fine work. Alicia responds, "Thank you, I'm the top-rated parliamentarian in the entire tri-state area and no ruling of mine has ever been reversed," though she has no such ranking and rulings of hers have been overturned. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. The parliamentarian made false statements about herself and her services when she claimed a ranking and record that do not exist. This breaches Joint Code section 3.3.

prompt: Parliamentarian Nathaniel Cruz mails a brochure to prospective clients promising, "Hire me and I guarantee your motions will never be challenged and every vote will hold up to any appeal." No parliamentarian can guarantee such outcomes, and Nathaniel knows it. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. Guaranteeing outcomes he cannot deliver is a false statement about his services. This breaches Joint Code section 3.3.

prompt: Parliamentarian Yusuf Kane, a Registered Parliamentarian, describes his services to the Meadowbrook Library Board as follows: "I am a Registered Parliamentarian with eight years advising nonprofit boards; I cannot guarantee any particular outcome, but I will advise the chair accurately." Every statement he makes is true. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: No. The parliamentarian described himself and his services accurately and made no false statement, so there is no breach of Joint Code section 3.3.

prompt: The executive director of the Coastal Arts Guild engages parliamentarian Helen Morday for a contested board meeting but says she cannot send the bylaws "for legal reasons." Helen accepts and serves at the meeting anyway, rendering opinions on questions governed by those bylaws without ever having read them. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. The parliamentarian served and gave opinions without obtaining and reviewing the governing documents before the meeting. This breaches Joint Code section 4.1.

prompt: Parliamentarian Owen Trask reads the bylaws of the Ferndale Teachers Union before their assembly but does not ask for or review the union's adopted special rules of order and standing rules, which were available on request and directly govern the pending amendments. He advises the chair based on the bylaws alone. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. The parliamentarian failed to obtain and review all relevant governing documents, including the special rules and standing rules, before serving. This breaches Joint Code section 4.1.

prompt: The Summit Athletic Association calls parliamentarian Rosa Delk at 8 a.m. to staff a special meeting at 9 a.m. that same morning. She agrees and shows up without requesting the bylaws, standing rules, or the adopted parliamentary authority, and begins advising the chair. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. The parliamentarian began serving without obtaining or reviewing any of the relevant governing documents beforehand. This breaches Joint Code section 4.1.

prompt: Before agreeing to advise the Grandview Symphony Society board, parliamentarian Ibrahim Nadeer requests and studies the bylaws, the standing rules, and the society's adopted parliamentary authority, and reviews the prior minutes relevant to the disputed motion. Only then does he attend and advise the chair. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: No. The parliamentarian obtained and reviewed all relevant governing documents before serving, so there is no breach of Joint Code section 4.1.

prompt: During a meeting of the Northgate Condominium Association, the chair announces that a motion to amend the bylaws will pass on a simple majority. Parliamentarian Dana Whitcombe knows the bylaws require a two-thirds vote with prior notice, but because the room is calm and she does not want to interrupt, she says nothing and the chair proceeds on the wrong threshold. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. The parliamentarian knew the chair was misapplying the vote requirement and failed to advise the chair of the proper application of the rules. This breaches Joint Code section 4.2.

prompt: At the Willow Creek Booster Club, the chair is about to rule a properly moved and seconded main motion out of order simply because he personally dislikes it. Parliamentarian Leon Prewitt recognizes the motion is in order but remains silent, and the chair improperly declares it dead. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. The parliamentarian recognized the chair was misapplying the rules and failed to advise him of their proper application. This breaches Joint Code section 4.2.

prompt: During the Eastvale Chamber of Commerce meeting, the chair starts to treat a motion to Postpone Indefinitely as though it were amendable. Parliamentarian Sofia Grendel immediately and quietly advises the chair that the motion is not amendable and explains the correct handling, which the chair then follows. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: No. The parliamentarian promptly advised the chair of the proper application of the rules, which is exactly what the Code requires, so there is no breach of Joint Code section 4.2.

prompt: At the Pinehill Alumni Association assembly, the chair mistakenly announces that a main motion carried on a tie vote. Parliamentarian Curtis Mabry knows a tie fails a motion requiring a majority, but he decides it is not his place to speak up and lets the erroneous announcement stand. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. The parliamentarian knew the chair was wrongly applying the majority rule on a tie vote and failed to advise the chair of its proper application. This breaches Joint Code section 4.2.

prompt: When parliamentarian Bethany Locke receives payment from the Cedar Falls Foundation for a contested meeting, the check exceeds her invoice by 25 percent, with a note from the chair: "A little extra for helping our side win." She deposits the full amount and continues advising the board on remaining matters. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. Accepting a bonus tied to the outcome creates the appearance that her advice is not impartial. This breaches Joint Code section 4.3.

prompt: The evening before a divisive vote at the Brightwater Yacht Club, a board member who strongly favors one side sends parliamentarian Hector Salas an expensive watch and tickets to a resort weekend, saying he hopes Hector will "see things our way." Hector accepts the gifts and serves at the meeting the next day. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. Accepting a valuable gift from an interested member creates the appearance that his advice is not impartial. This breaches Joint Code section 4.3.

prompt: After a heated meeting of the Oakmont Village Council, the winning faction's leader tries to hand parliamentarian Grace Ilnyk a cash "thank-you bonus" on top of her agreed fee. Grace declines it, saying, "I can only accept my invoiced fee; anything more could make my advice look partial," and takes only the amount she billed. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: No. The parliamentarian declined the bonus and accepted only her agreed fee, avoiding any appearance that her advice was not impartial, so there is no breach of Joint Code section 4.3.

prompt: While advising the Rivertown Merchants Guild on a proposal that would benefit one member's business, parliamentarian Simon Delacroix accepts that member's offer of a lavish paid dinner and a share of season tickets before rendering his opinions on the proposal. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. Accepting the dinner and tickets from a member with a stake in the outcome creates the appearance that his advice is not impartial. This breaches Joint Code section 4.3.

prompt: At the Fairmont Parent-Teacher Association meeting, one member declares that anyone who teaches the fee-based tutoring cannot vote on the tutoring policy, and the chair announces those members may not vote and takes the vote without them. Parliamentarian Tomas Bergen knows these members retain the right to vote but says nothing, and the exclusion changes the result. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. The parliamentarian failed to advise regarding the members' rights when they were improperly denied the right to vote. This breaches Joint Code section 4.6.

prompt: During the Glenwood Neighborhood Association meeting, the chair refuses to recognize a member who has properly risen to make a motion, telling her, "You've said enough tonight," and moves on. Parliamentarian Aisha Farrow knows the member has a right to make her motion but chooses not to advise the chair, and the member is shut out. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. The parliamentarian failed to advise regarding the member's right to make a motion when the chair improperly denied it. This breaches Joint Code section 4.6.

prompt: At the Harmony Choir Society assembly, the chair says members who are behind on dues may not vote, though the bylaws impose no such disqualification. Parliamentarian Nolan Priest immediately advises the chair that these members retain the right to vote and must be allowed to do so, and the chair corrects course. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: No. The parliamentarian advised the chair regarding the members' right to vote and prevented their improper exclusion, so there is no breach of Joint Code section 4.6.

prompt: During a contentious Bayshore Cooperative meeting, the chair orders that all members seated in the back section be barred from the pending vote because they "came in late," though nothing in the rules disqualifies them. Parliamentarian Renee Coyle recognizes this improperly denies their right to vote but stays silent, and the barred members are not counted. Did the parliamentarian breach the Joint Code? Why or why not? Which section?
gold: Yes. The parliamentarian failed to advise regarding the members' rights when they were improperly denied the right to vote. This breaches Joint Code section 4.6.
